topics.title: Programmable Logic Controllers - Power Supplies and Isolators
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAET604 - LOGIC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Programmable Logic Controllers - Power Supplies and Isolators

# ⚡ PLC Power Supplies and Isolators — KTU Module 1

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 PLC Power Supply — Formal Definition
A **PLC Power Supply Unit (PSU)** is the internal DC power module that converts the incoming AC mains (or external DC source) into the regulated, low-voltage DC rails required by the PLC's central processing unit (CPU), memory, I/O interface cards, and communication modules. It is the **"silent heart"** of the PLC — without a clean, regulated, and isolated power rail, the deterministic scan-cycle behaviour of the controller cannot be guaranteed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBAET604 / 2024 Scheme):**
> Under Module 1 ("Introduction to PLCs"), the university specifically asks for the study of *"power supply, isolators, I/O modules, and wiring techniques."* Power supply and isolators together form the **signal-integrity foundation** of every PLC rack.

**Standardised Output Rails (Industrial Practice):**
- **+5 V DC** → Backplane logic, CPU, memory
- **+24 V DC** → Discrete I/O sensors, relays, sinking/sourcing inputs
- **±15 V DC** → Analog I/O, op-amp signal conditioning
- **+3.3 V DC** → Modern compact PLCs and SoC-based CPUs

### 1.2 Isolator — Formal Definition
An **Isolator** (in PLC context) is an electronic barrier — typically a **photo-electric (opto-isolator)**, **transformer**, or **relay-based** device — that transfers an electrical signal between two circuits while providing **complete galvanic isolation** (zero direct conductive path) between them. Its primary role is to **block high-voltage transients, ground loops, and electrical noise** from reaching the sensitive 5 V logic of the CPU.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Galvanic Isolation:**
> *Galvanic isolation* is the principle of separating two circuit sections so that **no direct current (DC) can flow** between them, but **signal, power, or information can still be transferred** via electromagnetic, capacitive, or optical coupling. The standard isolation voltage for industrial PLCs is **1500 V RMS** for 1 minute (per IEC 61131-2).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

| Component | Real-World Analogy | Plain-English Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| **PLC Power Supply** | The **blood-pumping heart** of a human body | Just as the heart delivers clean, oxygenated blood at a steady pressure to every organ, the PSU delivers clean, regulated DC at a steady voltage to every card in the rack. If the heart falters, organs fail. If the PSU falters, the CPU misreads inputs. |
| **Opto-Isolator** | A **glass window** between two rooms | Imagine two rooms separated by a thick glass window. A person in Room A can shine a torch (light) to send a message to Room B, but **no sound, heat, electricity, or virus can cross the glass**. The opto-isolator works exactly like this — it uses **light** to cross an insulating barrier. |
| **Galvanic Isolation** | Two lakes separated by a mountain | Even if one lake is poisoned, the mountain ensures the poison never reaches the other lake. Likewise, a 480 V spike on the field side **cannot** travel through the isolator to destroy the 5 V CPU. |
| **Surge Protection (MOV/TVS)** | A **pressure relief valve** on a water pipeline | When pressure (voltage) suddenly spikes, the valve opens and dumps the excess safely to ground, protecting downstream instruments. |

### 1.4 Why Power Supplies and Isolators Are Co-Taught in KTU
The KTU 2024 syllabus deliberately pairs these two topics in Module 1 because they are **functionally inseparable**:
- A PSU **creates** the clean internal DC rail.
- An isolator **protects** that clean rail from the noisy, hazardous outside world (field side).

Without isolation, even a perfect PSU is vulnerable. Without a clean PSU, isolation has nothing clean to protect.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 PLC Power Supply — Architecture & Operating Principle

A modern PLC power supply is a **switch-mode power supply (SMPS)** operating on the principle of high-frequency PWM chopping followed by rectification and filtering. The block diagram sequence is:

1. **EMI/RFI Input Filter** — Suppresses conducted noise from the mains.
2. **Input Rectifier & Bulk Capacitor** — Converts AC to unregulated DC (~300 V DC for 230 V AC input).
3. **High-Frequency Inverter (20 kHz – 200 kHz)** — Chops the DC into a high-frequency AC square wave. *Why high frequency?* — because at high frequency, the transformer becomes **tiny, light, and efficient** (transformer mass is inversely proportional to frequency).
4. **High-Frequency Isolation Transformer** — Steps the voltage down AND provides **galvanic isolation** between mains and the secondary.
5. **Output Rectifier & LC Filter** — Rectifies the high-frequency AC back to clean DC.
6. **Feedback & PWM Controller** — Samples the output and adjusts the PWM duty cycle to maintain regulation (typically ±2–5 %).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Memory Hook:**
> **"FRI-HIT-OLF"** — Filter → Rectifier → Inverter → High-frequency Transformer → Output Rectifier → LC Filter. Memorise this order; it appears in nearly every descriptive question on PLC power supplies.

### 2.2 Comparison: Linear vs Switching Power Supplies

| Parameter | Linear PSU | Switching PSU (SMPS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Efficiency** | 30–50 % | 75–92 % |
| **Heat Dissipation** | High (large heatsinks) | Low |
| **Weight & Size** | Heavy (50/60 Hz transformer) | Light (HF transformer) |
| **Output Noise (Ripple)** | Very low (< 5 mV) | Moderate (20–50 mV) |
| **Hold-up Time** | Long | Short (depends on bulk cap) |
| **Cost** | Low (for < 50 W) | Higher (for > 50 W) |
| **KTU Use Case** | Small micro-PLCs | All industrial PLCs (Siemens S7, Allen-Bradley CompactLogix) |

### 2.3 Critical PSU Specifications (KTU High-Yield)

| Specification | Symbol / Unit | Typical Industrial Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input Voltage Range** | $V_{in}$ | **85–264 V AC** or **19.2–28.8 V DC** | Acceptable mains variation window |
| **Output Voltage** | $V_{out}$ | **5 V, 24 V, ±15 V** | Rails supplied to backplane |
| **Output Current (per rail)** | $I_{out}$ | **3 A, 5 A, 10 A** | Maximum load current |
| **Total Power Rating** | $P_{max}$ | **30 W – 480 W** | Sum of all rail powers |
| **Hold-up Time** | $t_{hold}$ | **≥ 20 ms** at full load | Time PSU survives a mains dip before CPU resets |
| **Inrush Current** | $I_{inrush}$ | **< 30 A peak** | Cold-start surge current |
| **Ripple & Noise** | $V_{ripple}$ | **< 50 mV pk-pk** | AC component on the DC output |
| **Efficiency** | $\eta$ | **> 85 %** | Power delivered / Power drawn |
| **MTBF** | Hours | **> 300,000 hrs** | Mean Time Between Failures |
| **Operating Temperature** | $T_{op}$ | **0 °C to +60 °C** | Industrial range |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{out} = V_{out} \times I_{out}$ | DC output power of a single rail | Watts (W) |
| 2 | $P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_{i} \times I_{i}$ | Total PSU power = sum of all rails | W |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Power supply efficiency | % |
| 4 | $P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out} = P_{out}\left(\dfrac{1}{\eta} - 1\right)$ | Power dissipated as heat | W |
| 5 | $E_{hold} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | Energy stored in bulk hold-up capacitor | Joules |
| 6 | $t_{hold} = \dfrac{E_{hold}}{P_{out}}$ | Hold-up time approximation | seconds |
| 7 | $V_{ripple} = \dfrac{I_{load}}{f_{sw} \times C_{filter}}$ | Output ripple voltage (SMPS) | Volts pk-pk |
| 8 | $V_{iso}$ (isolation voltage) | **1500 V RMS / 1 min** (IEC 61131-2) | V RMS |
| 9 | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{cm}}{V_{out}}\right)$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio of isolator | dB |
| 10 | $CTR = \dfrac{I_{C}}{I_{F}} \times 100\%$ | Current Transfer Ratio of opto-isolator | % |
| 11 | $t_{pd} = \dfrac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$ | Propagation delay of digital isolator | ns / µs |
| 12 | $\tau = R \times C$ | RC filter time constant for noise suppression | seconds |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Rendering Note (KTU Formula Sheet):** In the table above, the symbol `\vert` (or `\mid`) is used wherever a mathematical vertical bar would normally appear (e.g., inside `\log_{10}\vert V_{cm}\vert`). This is to **prevent markdown table syntax corruption**. When writing these in your answer scripts, use plain $\vert$ or just `|`.

### 2.5 Isolators — Deep Working Principle

#### 2.5.1 Opto-Isolator (Optical Coupler)
A typical opto-isolator has:
- **Infrared LED** on the input side
- **Phototransistor** (or photodiode, or phototriac) on the output side
- **Glass / plastic dielectric barrier** in between (typical creepage **> 7 mm**)

**Working:** When the input side is energised, the LED emits IR light → light crosses the gap → phototransistor conducts → output side sees a logic LOW or HIGH. There is **NO electrical connection** between the two sides.

#### 2.5.2 Transformer Isolation
Used mainly in **analog signal isolation** and in **SMPS power supplies**. The signal is transferred via a modulated magnetic field across a high-permeability ferrite core.

#### 2.5.3 Solid-State Relay (SSR) as an Isolator
For **AC output isolation**, a triac-based or back-to-back-SCR-based SSR combines an opto-isolator with a power switch in a single package.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

| Application | Why PSU/Isolator matters |
|---|---|
| **Automobile Assembly Line** | PLC must survive 480 V spikes from welding robots — opto-isolators prevent CPU damage. |
| **Pharmaceutical Batch Control** | Clean ±15 V rails are mandatory for accurate analog pH/temperature readings. |
| **Substation Automation** | 1500 V isolation is **legally mandatory** for personnel safety (IEC 61850). |
| **Marine PLCs** | SMPS must operate from dirty 24 V DC shipboard supply with ±20 % variation. |
| **Hazardous Area (ATEX)** | Isolators are mandatory intrinsic-safety barriers in Zone 0/1 explosive atmospheres. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

### 3.1 Derivation 1: PSU Power, Efficiency, and Heat Loss

**Given:**
- A Siemens S7-1200 PLC PSU provides:
  - +5 V rail at $I_{5V} = 3 \text{ A}$
  - +24 V rail at $I_{24V} = 1.2 \text{ A}$
- Input: 230 V AC, $I_{in} = 0.21 \text{ A}$, PF = 0.95
- Efficiency $\eta = 88\%$

**Find:** Total output power, input power, and heat dissipation.

#### Step 1: Compute Output Power of Each Rail

$$
P_{5V} = V_{5V} \times I_{5V} = 5 \text{ V} \times 3 \text{ A} = 15 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{24V} = V_{24V} \times I_{24V} = 24 \text{ V} \times 1.2 \text{ A} = 28.8 \text{ W}
$$

#### Step 2: Sum All Rails (Total Output Power)

$$
P_{out} = P_{5V} + P_{24V} = 15 \text{ W} + 28.8 \text{ W} = 43.8 \text{ W}
$$

#### Step 3: Compute Input Power Using Efficiency

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{43.8}{0.88} = 49.77 \text{ W}
$$

#### Step 4: Compute Heat Dissipated

$$
P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out} = 49.77 - 43.8 = 5.97 \text{ W}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Roughly **6 W of heat** must be removed by natural convection inside the PLC enclosure — adequate for a small fanless design, but explains why PLCs in sealed cabinets may need thermal management above 45 °C ambient.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Hold-Up Time Calculation

**Problem:** A PLC PSU has a bulk capacitor of $C = 470 \text{ µF}$ charged to $V = 380 \text{ V DC}$ (after rectification of 230 V AC). The CPU draws $P_{out} = 25 \text{ W}$ during a mains dip. The PSU regulator can operate down to $V_{min} = 250 \text{ V DC}$. Calculate the **hold-up time**.

#### Step 1: Energy Stored in the Capacitor (Initial)

$$
E_{initial} = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 470 \times 10^{-6} \times (380)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{initial} = \frac{1}{2} \times 470 \times 10^{-6} \times 144400 = 33.93 \text{ J}
$$

#### Step 2: Energy Stored at Minimum Voltage

$$
E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} C V_{min}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 470 \times 10^{-6} \times (250)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} \times 470 \times 10^{-6} \times 62500 = 14.69 \text{ J}
$$

#### Step 3: Usable Energy

$$
E_{usable} = E_{initial} - E_{min} = 33.93 - 14.69 = 19.24 \text{ J}
$$

#### Step 4: Hold-Up Time

$$
t_{hold} = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{out}} = \frac{19.24}{25} = 0.77 \text{ s} = 770 \text{ ms}
$$

**Result:** This PSU will keep the CPU alive for **~770 ms** during a complete mains dip — well above the KTU/IEC requirement of **20 ms**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Opto-Isolator CTR Verification

**Given:** An opto-coupler (e.g., 4N35) has:
- Forward current $I_F = 10 \text{ mA}$ through the LED
- Collector current $I_C = 5 \text{ mA}$ measured on the phototransistor side
- $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2 \text{ V}$

**Find:** Current Transfer Ratio (CTR).

$$
CTR = \frac{I_C}{I_F} \times 100\% = \frac{5 \text{ mA}}{10 \text{ mA}} \times 100\% = 50\%
$$

**Design Implication:** Industry rule-of-thumb — operate the LED at **40–60 % of its CTR** to ensure reliable switching over temperature and aging. Hence $I_F$ should be designed at **15–20 mA** if the output needs to switch 10 mA reliably.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Common-Mode Rejection of an Isolator

**Given:** A 50 V peak common-mode noise is applied across an isolator that produces 0.05 V at the output.

**Find:** CMRR in dB.

$$
CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{cm}}{V_{out}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{50}{0.05}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(1000) = 60 \text{ dB}
$$

**Interpretation:** 60 dB of CMRR is **good for industrial use** (≥ 80 dB is medical-grade). The noise has been attenuated by a factor of 1000.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Output Ripple of an SMPS

**Given:** 
- Load current $I_{load} = 2 \text{ A}$
- Switching frequency $f_{sw} = 100 \text{ kHz}$
- Filter capacitor $C = 470 \text{ µF}$

**Find:** Peak-to-peak ripple voltage.

$$
V_{ripple} = \frac{I_{load}}{f_{sw} \times C} = \frac{2}{100{,}000 \times 470 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2}{47} = 0.0425 \text{ V} = 42.5 \text{ mV}
$$

**Result:** 42.5 mV ripple — well within the typical < 50 mV industrial spec.

---

### 3.6 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix

| Scenario | Recommended PSU Type | Recommended Isolation | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tiny micro-PLC, 12 V battery powered | Linear regulator (LM7805) | None needed (single-rail) | Low cost, no mains |
| Mid-range PLC, factory floor | SMPS (24 V DC input) | Opto-isolation on all I/O | DIN-rail mount, surge-prone |
| Large PLC, redundant operation | Hot-swappable SMPS modules | Opto + transformer (dual) | Zero downtime required |
| Analog-heavy process control | Linear PSU (low noise) | Instrumentation amp + isolation amp | 4–20 mA loop accuracy |
| Hazardous area (Zone 1) | Intrinsically-safe PSU (zener barrier) | Galvanic + zener + opto | ATEX/IECEx compliance |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — SMPS-Based PLC Power Supply

```mermaid
graph LR
    AC[AC Mains 230V] --> F1[EMI Filter]
    F1 --> F2[Input Fuse and MOV Surge Protector]
    F2 --> F3[Bridge Rectifier and Bulk Cap 380V DC]
    F3 --> F4[High Frequency Inverter 100kHz PWM]
    F4 --> F5[HF Isolation Transformer]
    F5 --> F6[Output Rectifier and LC Filter]
    F6 --> F7[+5V Rail to CPU]
    F6 --> F8[+24V Rail to I/O Modules]
    F6 --> F9[+15V Rail to Analog]
    F6 --> F10[Feedback to PWM Controller]
    F10 -.-> F4
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — PLC Power Supply with Isolation Stages

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FIELD_SIDE [Field Side - Hazardous]
        FS1[230V AC Mains]
        FS2[Industrial Sensors and Actuators]
        FS3[Motor Contactors 480V]
    end
    
    subgraph PSU_BLOCK [PSU and Isolation Block]
        P1[Step 1 Surge Protection MOV and TVS]
        P2[Step 2 Input Rectifier and Filter]
        P3[Step 3 SMPS 100kHz Inverter]
        P4[Step 4 HF Isolation Transformer 1500V]
        P5[Step 5 Output Regulation to 5V and 24V]
    end
    
    subgraph CPU_SIDE [CPU Side - Safe Low Voltage]
        C1[CPU 5V Logic]
        C2[Memory 3.3V]
        C3[Opto-Isolators on Every I/O Channel]
    end
    
    FS1 --> P1
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5
    P5 --> C1
    P5 --> C2
    FS2 --> C3
    FS3 --> C3
    C3 -.optical signal.-> C1
```

### 4.3 Opto-Isolator Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Signal 24V DC] --> B[Current Limiting Resistor R1]
    B --> C[IR LED inside Opto Coupler]
    C -- light beam --> D[Phototransistor Base]
    D --> E[Output to PLC Logic 5V]
    E --> F[Pull-up Resistor R2 to 5V]
    C:::iso
    D:::iso
    classDef iso fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow — PLC Input Module with Opto-Isolation

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FIELD [Field Devices]
        SW1[Limit Switch 1]
        SW2[Proximity Sensor 2]
        SW3[Push Button 3]
    end
    
    subgraph TERMINAL [Wiring Terminal Block]
        TB[Common 24V Return]
    end
    
    subgraph ISOLATION [Opto Isolation Stage]
        OPTO1[Opto Coupler Channel 1]
        OPTO2[Opto Coupler Channel 2]
        OPTO3[Opto Coupler Channel 3]
    end
    
    subgraph BACKPLANE [Backplane to CPU]
        CPU[CPU Scans 5V Logic]
    end
    
    SW1 --> OPTO1
    SW2 --> OPTO2
    SW3 --> OPTO3
    OPTO1 --> CPU
    OPTO2 --> CPU
    OPTO3 --> CPU
    TB -.common.-> SW1
    TB -.common.-> SW2
    TB -.common.-> SW3
```

### 4.5 Surge Protection and Grounding Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1[Lightning Strike 10kV] --> SP1[Stage 1 Lightning Arrester]
    SP1 --> SP2[Stage 2 Surge Protector SPD Type 2]
    SP2 --> SP3[Stage 3 TVS Diode on PLC PSU]
    SP3 --> ISO[Galvanic Isolation 1500V RMS]
    ISO --> CPU[CPU Protected at 5V]
    
    G1[Chassis Ground PE] --> SP1
    G2[Signal Ground] --> ISO
    G3[Safety Earth] --> CPU
    
    G1 -.separate.-> G2
    G2 -.separate.-> G3
```

### 4.6 Hold-Up Time Waveform — Conceptual Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0[Normal 230V AC] -- t=0 ms Mains Fails --> T1[Capacitor Discharges from 380V]
    T1 -- 0 to 20 ms --> T2[PSU Output Still in Regulation]
    T2 -- 20 ms to 770 ms --> T3[Output Drops Linearly to 250V]
    T3 -- beyond 770 ms --> T4[CPU Resets and Scan Stops]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*List any three functions of a PLC power supply unit. Mention the typical DC voltage rails it provides.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three primary functions of a PLC power supply unit are:
1. **Voltage conversion and regulation** — converts the incoming AC mains (or external DC) into the stable low-voltage DC rails required by the CPU, I/O, and memory.
2. **Galvanic isolation** — provides electrical separation between the hazardous mains side and the safe 5 V logic side via a high-frequency isolation transformer.
3. **Surge and noise filtering** — incorporates EMI/RFI filters and surge protectors (MOV/TVS) to reject mains-borne transients and conducted noise.

**Typical DC rails:** **+5 V DC** (CPU and logic), **+24 V DC** (discrete I/O and sensors), and **±15 V DC** (analog I/O signal conditioning).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*What is an opto-isolator? State its role in a PLC.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **opto-isolator** (or optical coupler) is a semiconductor device that transfers an electrical signal between two isolated circuits using a beam of **infrared light** across a non-conductive dielectric barrier.

**Construction:** It consists of an **IR LED** on the input side and a **phototransistor** (or phototriac) on the output side, separated by a glass or plastic gap.

**Role in a PLC:**
1. Provides **galvanic isolation** (typically **1500 V RMS**) between the field-side I/O wiring and the CPU's 5 V backplane.
2. **Protects the CPU** from voltage transients, ground loops, and electrical noise generated by inductive loads (motors, solenoids, relays) on the field side.
3. **Prevents false triggering** of inputs by isolating common-mode noise.
4. Implements **signal-level shifting** (24 V field → 5 V logic).

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper-style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **switched-mode power supply (SMPS)** used in a PLC rack. Mention the role of the high-frequency transformer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PLC PSU delivers +5 V at 6 A and +24 V at 1.5 A to the backplane. The input is 230 V AC, 0.6 A, power factor 0.9. Calculate **(i)** total output power, **(ii)** input power assuming η = 90 %, and **(iii)** heat that must be dissipated by the enclosure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A Switched-Mode Power Supply (SMPS) is the industry-standard PSU for all modern PLCs (Siemens, Allen-Bradley, Schneider). It converts the 230 V AC mains into regulated, low-voltage DC rails using **high-frequency PWM switching** rather than a 50 Hz linear transformer.

**Block Diagram Description (5 marks):**

| Block | Function | Detail |
|---|---|---|
| **1. EMI/RFI Filter** | Mains noise suppression | LC low-pass filter on the input |
| **2. Input Rectifier + Bulk Capacitor** | AC → Unregulated DC | Diode bridge + 470 µF electrolytic cap → ~380 V DC |
| **3. High-Frequency Inverter** | DC → High-Frequency AC | MOSFET/IGBT chopper at 50–200 kHz |
| **4. HF Isolation Transformer** | Step-down + **Galvanic isolation** | Replaces bulky 50 Hz transformer; mass inversely proportional to frequency |
| **5. Output Rectifier + LC Filter** | HF AC → Smooth DC | Fast-recovery diodes + LC π-filter |
| **6. PWM Feedback Controller** | Regulation | Compares output to reference; adjusts duty cycle |

**Role of the High-Frequency Transformer (1 mark):**
The HF transformer performs **two simultaneous functions**:
1. **Voltage step-down** from 380 V DC to low-voltage AC.
2. **Galvanic isolation** of 1500 V RMS between primary (mains) and secondary (logic) — this is the **fundamental safety barrier** of the PLC.

*Why high frequency?* — Because transformer core size $\propto \dfrac{1}{f}$, a 100 kHz transformer is **~2000 times smaller** than a 50 Hz transformer of the same power rating.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $V_{5V} = 5 \text{ V}, \ I_{5V} = 6 \text{ A}$
- $V_{24V} = 24 \text{ V}, \ I_{24V} = 1.5 \text{ A}$
- $V_{in} = 230 \text{ V}, \ I_{in} = 0.6 \text{ A}, \ \cos\phi = 0.9$
- $\eta = 90\% = 0.9$

**Step 1: Output Power of Each Rail (1 mark)**

$$
P_{5V} = V_{5V} \times I_{5V} = 5 \times 6 = 30 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{24V} = V_{24V} \times I_{24V} = 24 \times 1.5 = 36 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2: Total Output Power (1 mark)**

$$
P_{out} = P_{5V} + P_{24V} = 30 + 36 = 66 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3: Input Power Using Efficiency (2 marks)**

$$
P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{66}{0.9} = 73.33 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4: Heat Dissipated (1 mark)**

$$
P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out} = 73.33 - 66 = 7.33 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5: Verification Using Mains VA (2 marks)**

$$
S_{in} = V_{in} \times I_{in} = 230 \times 0.6 = 138 \text{ VA}
$$

$$
P_{in,measured} = S_{in} \times \cos\phi = 138 \times 0.9 = 124.2 \text{ W}
$$

*Note:* The discrepancy (124.2 W vs 73.33 W) indicates the PSU is operating at only **~59 % of its theoretical input** — possibly because the PSU has additional 3.3 V and ±15 V rails not listed, or the load is not at full draw. Always include this interpretation step in KTU answers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** What is **galvanic isolation**? With a circuit diagram, explain how an **opto-isolator** provides isolation between a 24 V DC field input and a 5 V DC PLC logic input. List the advantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk hold-up capacitor of **1000 µF** is charged to **400 V DC**. The PLC CPU draws **50 W** during a mains failure. Calculate **(i)** the initial energy stored, **(ii)** the usable energy if the regulator shuts down at **300 V DC**, and **(iii)** the **hold-up time**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):**
**Galvanic isolation** is a design technique in which two electrical circuits are coupled such that **no direct current path (no conductive wire)** exists between them, but **signal or power can still be transferred** via an alternative medium — light, magnetic field, or capacitance.

**Circuit Diagram Description (3 marks):**

```
+24V ──[R1]──┤LED├──||photo gap||──┤Photo-Transistor├── 5V ──[R_pullup]── to PLC input
              │                            │
            field side                CPU side
            (24V common)              (5V logic common)
```

**Working:**
1. The 24 V field input energises the LED through current-limiting resistor R1.
2. The LED emits IR light that crosses the **non-conductive dielectric gap** (typical creepage ≥ 7 mm).
3. The phototransistor on the CPU side receives the light and conducts.
4. The collector voltage drops from 5 V to near 0 V → CPU reads a logic LOW (or HIGH, depending on inverting/non-inverting configuration).
5. **No wire connects the two sides** → any 24 V transient, 480 V spike, or ground loop on the field side is blocked.

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. **CPU protection** from voltage transients and field-side faults.
2. **Eliminates ground loops** between separately-powered field devices.
3. **Operator safety** — no high voltage can reach the logic side.
4. **Noise immunity** — common-mode rejection ≥ 60 dB.
5. **Level shifting** — 24 V sensor signal safely converted to 5 V logic.
6. **Compliance** with IEC 61131-2 / IEC 61131-3 isolation standards.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $C = 1000 \text{ µF} = 1000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}$
- $V_{initial} = 400 \text{ V DC}$
- $V_{min} = 300 \text{ V DC}$ (regulator drop-out)
- $P_{out} = 50 \text{ W}$

**Step 1: Initial Energy Stored (2 marks)**

$$
E_{initial} = \frac{1}{2} C V_{initial}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 10^{-6} \times (400)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{initial} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 160000 = 80 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 2: Energy at Minimum Voltage (2 marks)**

$$
E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} C V_{min}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 10^{-6} \times (300)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{min} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 90000 = 45 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3: Usable Energy (1 mark)**

$$
E_{usable} = E_{initial} - E_{min} = 80 - 45 = 35 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4: Hold-Up Time (2 marks)**

$$
t_{hold} = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{out}} = \frac{35}{50} = 0.7 \text{ s} = 700 \text{ ms}
$$

**Result:** The CPU can continue scanning for **~700 ms** after a complete mains failure — easily covering a 20 ms KTU/IEC spec with **35× margin**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Avoid These Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Mixing up power and energy** — many students compute power in Joules or energy in Watts. Remember: $P = W/s$, $E = J$.
> 2. **Forgetting to square the voltage** in $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^{2}$ — partial marking is given only if the $\frac{1}{2}$ is included.
> 3. **Using VA instead of W** — for input power, the KTU expects $P = V \times I \times \cos\phi$ in Watts, not just $S = V \times I$.
> 4. **Confusing linear PSU with SMPS** — in descriptive answers, always specify the block you are describing.
> 5. **Writing "isolation transformer" without "galvanic"** — always use the phrase **galvanic isolation**, not just "isolation".
> 6. **Skipping units in the final answer** — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark if the numerical answer is unitless.
> 7. **Confusing CTR with β** — CTR is the ratio $I_C/I_F$ in an opto-isolator; it is **not** the transistor β.
> 8. **Omitting the high-frequency transformer role** — this is a 1-mark item frequently missed; mention both *step-down* AND *galvanic isolation*.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **PLC PSU is the heart** of the PLC — converts mains to **+5 V, +24 V, ±15 V DC** regulated rails.
- ✅ **SMPS is the industry standard** (linear PSU is used only in tiny micro-PLCs).
- ✅ **SMPS block order** = *Filter → Rectifier → Inverter → HF Transformer → Output Rectifier → LC Filter → Feedback.* Mnemonic: **"FRI-HIT-OLF"**.
- ✅ **High-frequency transformer** does two jobs — **voltage step-down** AND **galvanic isolation**.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in}$ — typical industrial SMPS is **> 85 %**.
- ✅ **Heat loss** $P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out}$ — must be removed by convection; key in sealed cabinets.
- ✅ **Hold-up time** $t_{hold} = E_{usable}/P_{out}$ — must be **≥ 20 ms** (IEC 61131-2).
- ✅ **Energy in capacitor** $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^{2}$ — used in hold-up calculations.
- ✅ **Isolation voltage** standard = **1500 V RMS for 1 minute** per IEC 61131-2.
- ✅ **Opto-isolator** uses **light** across a **dielectric barrier** — no conductive path between input and output.
- ✅ **Current Transfer Ratio (CTR)** = $I_C/I_F \times 100\%$ — design rule: operate at 40–60 % of rated CTR.
- ✅ **CMRR** measures isolator's noise rejection — measured in **dB**, higher is better.
- ✅ **Surge protection cascade** = Lightning Arrester → SPD Type 2 → TVS Diode → Isolator → CPU.
- ✅ **Grounding hierarchy** = Chassis (PE) → Signal Ground → Safety Earth — must remain separate to avoid ground loops.
- ✅ **Three PSU types** = Linear, SMPS, and Redundant Hot-Swap. Industrial PLCs use **SMPS with hot-swap** for zero downtime.
- ✅ **KTU favourite questions** = Block diagram of SMPS (7 marks), Opto-isolator explanation (7 marks), and numerical on hold-up/efficiency (7 marks).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Program and Data Organization in PLC - Input and Output Modules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAET604 - LOGIC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Program and Data Organization in PLC - Input and Output Modules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Program Organization in PLC

**Program Organization** in a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) refers to the systematic, hierarchical structuring of the control logic into executable units such as **Tasks**, **Programs**, and **Routines** so that the CPU can execute them in a deterministic, repeatable, and time-bound sequence during every scan cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Concept)**
> A PLC program is not a single linear block of code. It is a structured collection of **one or more tasks**, where each task contains **one or more programs**, and each program contains **one or more routines** (main routine, sub-routines, fault routines).

### 1.2 Input and Output (I/O) Modules

**Input/Output Modules** are the physical, isolated interface cards mounted on the PLC rack (or connected remotely) that act as the electrical "gateway" between the real-world field devices (sensors, actuators) and the low-voltage, digital CPU backplane of the PLC.

- **Input Module (I-Module):** Receives signals **FROM** field devices (Push Buttons, Limit Switches, Proximity Sensors, Transmitters) **TO** the CPU.
- **Output Module (O-Module):** Sends control signals **FROM** the CPU **TO** field actuators (Contactors, Solenoid Valves, Relays, Variable Frequency Drives).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Reference (IEC 61131-3):** The PLC open standard defines the program organization units (POUs) as the backbone of any modular PLC program. This is a **direct KTU syllabus weightage** topic.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy — "The Human Body"

Think of a PLC system exactly like a **human body controlling a car**:

| Human Body Part | PLC Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Eyes, Ears, Skin (Senses) | **Input Modules** | Perceive the outside world (temperature, pressure, position) |
| Brain (CPU) | **Processor + Memory** | Thinks, decides, stores memories |
| Hands, Legs (Muscles) | **Output Modules** | Performs physical actions (brake, accelerate) |
| Memory of "How to Drive" | **Program Organization (Tasks/Programs)** | Stored set of instructions |
| Knowledge of "Where Keys are" | **Data Organization (Tags/Files)** | Organized information storage |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Check:** Just as you cannot "think" with your hands, a PLC cannot directly control a motor without an output module translating its low-voltage logic decision into a high-current muscle movement.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards

- **Standard PLC Scan Time:** **10–50 ms** (typical for mid-range PLCs).
- **Backplane Voltage:** **5 V DC** (logic level).
- **Field Voltage at I/O Terminals:** **24 V DC, 120 V AC, 230 V AC**.
- **Standard I/O Density per Module:** **8, 16, or 32 channels**.
- **IEC 61131-3:** The international standard governing PLC programming and organization.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Program Organization (Top-Down Breakdown)

The PLC program is structured in **four strict hierarchical levels**:

**Level 1 — Task (Highest Level)**
- A Task is a **scheduling unit** that triggers a collection of programs.
- **Types of Tasks:**
  - **Continuous (Periodic) Task:** Runs every scan cycle (lowest priority, runs when CPU is idle).
  - **Periodic Task:** Runs at a fixed time interval (e.g., every 100 ms).
  - **Event Task:** Triggered by a specific event (e.g., input change, fault).

**Level 2 — Program**
- A Program is a **logical grouping of routines** related to a specific machine function.
- Example: "Conveyor_Control", "Tank_Level_Control", "Alarm_Handler".

**Level 3 — Routine**
- A Routine is the **actual executable code** written in Ladder Logic (LD), Function Block Diagram (FBD), Structured Text (ST), or Instruction List (IL).
- **Types:**
  - **Main Routine:** The primary routine of a program (always executed first).
  - **Sub-Routine:** Called by the main routine for specific functions.
  - **Fault Routine:** Executed only when a specific fault occurs.

**Level 4 — Tag / Variable (Data Level)**
- A **Tag** is a named memory location that holds a value used by the program.
- **Scope of Tags:** Program-scoped, Controller-scoped, or System-scoped.

---

### 2.2 Data Organization in PLC

PLC data is organized into **Data Files** (in older Allen-Bradley SLC 500) or **Tag Database** (in modern ControlLogix/CompactLogix).

**Standard Data File Structure (Allen-Bradley Convention):**

| File Number | File Type | Identifier | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **O0** | Output | **O** | Status sent to output modules |
| **I1** | Input | **I** | Status received from input modules |
| **S2** | Status | **S** | Processor status & fault bits |
| **B3** | Bit | **B** | General-purpose binary storage |
| **T4** | Timer | **T** | Timer values, presets, bits |
| **C5** | Counter | **C** | Counter values, presets, bits |
| **N7** | Integer | **N** | 16-bit integer storage |
| **F8** | Float | **F** | 32-bit floating-point storage |
| **R6** | Control | **R** | Sequencer / shift register control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Tag-Based PLCs (ControlLogix):** Instead of fixed data files, these PLCs use a **symbolic tag database** where each tag has a unique name (e.g., `Motor_1_Start_PB`, `Tank_Level_PV`) and a defined data type (BOOL, INT, REAL, TIMER, etc.).

---

### 2.3 Input Module Architecture (Deep-Dive)

**Operation of a Typical DC Input Module (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Field Terminal:** Receives the 24V DC signal from a sensor.
2. **Input Conditioning Circuit:** Includes an **RC filter** (debounce ~10 ms) and **over-voltage protection** (Zener diode/TVS).
3. **Optical Isolation:** An **Optocoupler (LED + Phototransistor)** provides **galvanic isolation** (typically **1500 V RMS**).
4. **Threshold Detector (Schmitt Trigger):** Converts the analog isolated signal to a clean **0 V or 5 V** logic level.
5. **Address Decoder:** The CPU uses the module's **rack/slot address** to read the specific bit into the **Input Image Table**.
6. **Input Image Table:** A block of CPU memory (e.g., `I:1/0` to `I:1/15` for 16-point module in slot 1) that mirrors the real-world input status.

---

### 2.4 Output Module Architecture (Deep-Dive)

**Operation of a Typical AC/DC Output Module:**

1. **Output Latch:** The CPU writes the output status into the **Output Image Table** (e.g., `O:2/5`).
2. **Address Decoder:** Routes the bit to the correct module and terminal.
3. **Optical Isolation:** Same galvanic isolation as input modules (**1500 V RMS**).
4. **Driver Stage:** A pre-driver circuit (transistor/Darlington pair) amplifies the logic signal.
5. **Power Switching Element:**
   - **Transistor (DC Output):** For low-voltage DC loads (24V, 0.5 A).
   - **TRIAC (AC Output):** For AC loads (230V, 1 A).
   - **Relay (EMR/SSR):** For universal voltage loads (dry contact, 2 A).
6. **Snubber Circuit:** RC network across the switching device to suppress voltage spikes from inductive loads.

---

### 2.5 The PLC Scan Cycle (Operating Sequence)

Every PLC executes a continuous loop, called the **Scan Cycle**, which consists of **four mandatory steps**:

1. **Input Scan:** Read all inputs into the Input Image Table (~1–5 ms).
2. **Program Scan:** Execute the user program logic sequentially (~5–20 ms).
3. **Output Scan:** Write the Output Image Table to the physical output modules (~1–5 ms).
4. **Housekeeping:** Communication, diagnostics, self-tests (~1–3 ms).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The **Input/Output Image Tables** are crucial. The program **does NOT** read from physical inputs directly during execution — it reads from the **Input Image Table** (a snapshot taken at the start of the scan). This guarantees deterministic, repeatable behavior.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

| Formula | Description | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$ | **Total Scan Time** | ms (milliseconds) | Used to calculate worst-case response time |
| $T_{worst} = T_{scan} + T_{input\,delay} + T_{output\,delay}$ | **Worst-Case Response Time** | ms | Determines if the PLC is fast enough for the process |
| $N_{points} = N_{slots} \times 16$ | **I/O Points per Rack** (16-point modules) | dimensionless | Rack sizing calculation |
| $N_{racks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{N_{total\,I/O}}{N_{points\,per\,rack}} \right\rceil$ | **Number of Racks Required** | integer | System sizing |
| $T_{in} = N_{in\,modules} \times t_{per\,module}$ | **Input Scan Time** | ms | Where $t_{per\,module} \approx 0.1$ to $0.5$ ms |
| $T_{out} = N_{out\,modules} \times t_{per\,module}$ | **Output Scan Time** | ms | Same per-module time |
| $BW_{analog} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}$ | **Analog Resolution (LSB size)** | V/bit | Where $n$ = ADC bits (12, 14, 16) |
| $\%FS = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | **Analog Input as % of Full Scale** | percent | Analog scaling |
| $V_{in\,digital} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in\,analog}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor$ | **ADC Quantization** | bit-count | Converting analog to digital |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Mathematical Notations Used (KTU Exam Friendly):**
> - $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ = **Ceiling function** (round up to next integer)
> - $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ = **Floor function** (round down to next integer)
> - $V_{FS}$ = Full-Scale Voltage (typically **10 V** or **20 mA** for analog)
> - $V_{LSB}$ = Least Significant Bit voltage step

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly Lines:** Modular PLCs with distributed I/O control robotic welding, painting, and assembly stations.
- **Water Treatment Plants:** Analog I/O modules monitor pH, turbidity, and flow rate with 16-bit resolution.
- **Power Plants:** High-speed digital I/O modules monitor circuit breaker status at sub-millisecond response times.
- **Packaging Industry:** Event-based tasks trigger high-speed counters for product counting at 100 kHz.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total PLC Scan Time

The scan time is the sum of the four sequential phases of the PLC operating cycle.

**Step 1 — Define the four phases:**
- $T_{in}$ = Time to scan all input modules
- $T_{prog}$ = Time to execute the user logic
- $T_{out}$ = Time to update all output modules
- $T_{house}$ = Time for housekeeping (communication, diagnostics)

**Step 2 — Express the Total Scan Time as the algebraic sum:**

$$
T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}
$$

**Step 3 — State the assumed typical per-module scan time:**
For a standard 16-point module, the per-module scan time is $t_m = 0.2$ ms.

**Step 4 — Substitute the per-module time expression:**

$$
T_{in} = N_{in} \times t_m \quad \text{and} \quad T_{out} = N_{out} \times t_m
$$

where $N_{in}$ and $N_{out}$ are the number of input and output modules.

**Step 5 — Final Compact Form:**

$$
\boxed{T_{scan} = (N_{in} + N_{out}) \cdot t_m + T_{prog} + T_{house}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Scan Time Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU Standard):**
A PLC has **4 input modules** and **3 output modules**. Each module takes **0.2 ms** to scan. The user program takes **8 ms** to execute, and housekeeping takes **2 ms**. Calculate the **total scan time** and the **worst-case response time** (assume input and output hardware delays of **5 ms** each).

**Step 1 — Identify Given Parameters:**
- $N_{in} = 4$, $N_{out} = 3$, $t_m = 0.2$ ms
- $T_{prog} = 8$ ms, $T_{house} = 2$ ms
- $T_{input\,delay} = 5$ ms, $T_{output\,delay} = 5$ ms

**Step 2 — Calculate Input Scan Time:**

$$
T_{in} = N_{in} \times t_m = 4 \times 0.2 = 0.8 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Output Scan Time:**

$$
T_{out} = N_{out} \times t_m = 3 \times 0.2 = 0.6 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Total Scan Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{scan} &= T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house} \\
&= 0.8 + 8 + 0.6 + 2 \\
&= 11.4 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Worst-Case Response Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{worst} &= T_{scan} + T_{input\,delay} + T_{output\,delay} \\
&= 11.4 + 5 + 5 \\
&= 21.4 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $T_{scan} = 11.4$ ms, $T_{worst} = 21.4$ ms.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 2: I/O Rack Sizing

**Step 1 — Given:** Total I/O requirement = $N_{total}$, Module density = 16 points.

**Step 2 — Points per rack** (assuming 1 module per slot and 16 slots per rack):

$$
N_{points} = 16 \text{ slots} \times 16 \text{ points} = 256 \text{ points}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of racks required (using ceiling function):**

$$
N_{racks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{N_{total}}{256} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example:** $N_{total} = 500$ points.

$$
N_{racks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{500}{256} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 1.953 \right\rceil = 2 \text{ racks}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Analog Input Resolution (LSB Size)

**Step 1 — Given:** ADC resolution $n = 12$ bits, Full-scale voltage $V_{FS} = 10$ V.

**Step 2 — The number of discrete levels (quantization steps) is $2^n$:**

$$
N_{levels} = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ steps}
$$

**Step 3 — The LSB (resolution) is:**

$$
V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \dfrac{10}{4096} = 0.00244 \text{ V} = 2.44 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 4 — For a 16-bit ADC:**

$$
V_{LSB} = \dfrac{10}{2^{16}} = \dfrac{10}{65536} = 0.0001526 \text{ V} = 0.1526 \text{ mV}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 2024 Exam Favorite)

| Parameter | Digital Input Module | Digital Output Module | Analog Input Module | Analog Output Module |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Field → CPU | CPU → Field | Field → CPU | CPU → Field |
| **Signal Type** | ON/OFF (1 bit) | ON/OFF (1 bit) | Continuous (mV/mA) | Continuous (mV/mA) |
| **Resolution** | 1 bit | 1 bit | 12/14/16 bit | 12/14/16 bit |
| **Typical Range** | 24 V DC / 110 V AC | 24 V DC / 230 V AC | 4–20 mA / 0–10 V | 4–20 mA / 0–10 V |
| **Isolation** | Optocoupler (1.5 kV) | Optocoupler (1.5 kV) | Optocoupler + Transformer | Optocoupler + Transformer |
| **Switching Element** | None (read-only) | Transistor / TRIAC / Relay | None (read-only) | DAC + Amplifier |
| **Image Table** | Input Image (I) | Output Image (O) | Input Register (IR) | Output Register (OR) |
| **Debounce/Filter** | RC filter (10 ms) | None | Anti-aliasing filter | Output smoothing |
| **KTU 2-Mark Weightage** | **High** | **High** | **Medium** | **Medium** |

---

### 3.6 Common I/O Addressing Format (Allen-Bradley SLC 500 Convention)

The general format is:

$$
\text{Address} = \underbrace{\text{Type}}_{\text{I/O/S/B/T/C/N}} : \underbrace{\text{Slot}}_{\text{0–30}} / \underbrace{\text{Word}}_{\text{0–255}} / \underbrace{\text{Bit}}_{\text{0–15}}
$$

**Example Addresses:**

| Address | Type | Slot | Word | Bit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `I:1/5` | Input | 1 | — | 5 | Input bit 5 in slot 1 |
| `O:2/3` | Output | 2 | — | 3 | Output bit 3 in slot 2 |
| `B3:0/0` | Bit file | 3 | 0 | 0 | First bit of word 0 in file 3 |
| `N7:10` | Integer | 7 | 10 | — | Integer at word 10 in file 7 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PLC System Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FieldDevices["FIELD DEVICES LAYER"]
        SENSORS["SENSORS\nPush Button\nLimit Switch\nProximity Sensor"]
        ACTUATORS["ACTUATORS\nMotor Contactor\nSolenoid Valve\nIndicator Lamp"]
    end

    subgraph IOModules["I/O MODULE LAYER"]
        INMOD["INPUT MODULE\nOpto-isolated\nFiltered\n24V DC / 110V AC"]
        OUTMOD["OUTPUT MODULE\nOpto-isolated\nDriver Stage\nTransistor / TRIAC / Relay"]
    end

    subgraph CPUBlock["CPU AND MEMORY LAYER"]
        INIMG["INPUT IMAGE TABLE\nSnapshot of all inputs\nUpdated each scan"]
        PROG["PROCESSOR\nExecutes Ladder Logic\nReads Input Image\nWrites Output Image"]
        OUTIMG["OUTPUT IMAGE TABLE\nStatus for all outputs\nUpdated each scan"]
        MEM["DATA MEMORY\nTags\nData Files\nProgram Files"]
    end

    subgraph PGMORG["PROGRAM ORGANIZATION LAYER"]
        TASK["TASK\nContinuous / Periodic / Event"]
        PROGUNIT["PROGRAM\nMachine Function Group"]
        ROUTINE["ROUTINE\nMain / Sub / Fault\nLadder or FBD or ST"]
    end

    SENSORS -->|Field Voltage 24V| INMOD
    INMOD -->|5V Logic Level| INIMG
    INIMG -->|Read by CPU| PROG
    PROG -->|Writes to| OUTIMG
    OUTIMG -->|5V Logic Level| OUTMOD
    OUTMOD -->|Field Voltage 230V| ACTUATORS

    PROG -->|Uses| MEM
    TASK -->|Schedules| PROGUNIT
    PROGUNIT -->|Contains| ROUTINE
    ROUTINE -.->|Compiled into| PROG
```

---

### 4.2 Input Module — Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FIELD TERMINAL\n+24V and 0V"] --> B["PROTECTION STAGE\nTVS Diode\nFuse"]
    B --> C["FILTER STAGE\nRC Debounce\n10 ms Time Constant"]
    C --> D["OPTO-ISOLATOR\nLED + Phototransistor\n1.5 kV Isolation"]
    D --> E["SCHMITT TRIGGER\nThreshold Detector\nHysteresis"]
    E --> F["ADDRESS DECODER\nRack / Slot Logic"]
    F --> G["CPU BACKPLANE\n5V Logic\nInput Image Table"]
```

---

### 4.3 Output Module — Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CPU BACKPLANE\n5V Logic\nOutput Image Table"] --> B["ADDRESS DECODER\nRack / Slot Logic"]
    B --> C["OUTPUT LATCH\nStores bit status"]
    C --> D["OPTO-ISOLATOR\n1.5 kV Isolation"]
    D --> E["PRE-DRIVER\nDarlington Pair"]
    E --> F["POWER SWITCH\nTransistor DC\nTRIAC AC\nRelay Universal"]
    F --> G["SNUBBER CIRCUIT\nRC Network\nVoltage Spike Suppression"]
    G --> H["FIELD TERMINAL\nConnects to Load"]
```

---

### 4.4 Program Organization Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["TASK LAYER\nContinuous Task\nPeriodic Task\nEvent Task"] --> B["PROGRAM LAYER\nConveyor Program\nTank Program\nAlarm Program"]
    B --> C["ROUTINE LAYER\nMain Routine\nSub Routines\nFault Routines"]
    C --> D["TAG AND DATA LAYER\nBoolean Tags\nInteger Tags\nReal Tags\nTimer and Counter Tags"]
```

---

### 4.5 PLC Scan Cycle — Time-Sequence Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as PLC CPU
    participant IN as Input Module
    participant IMG as Input Image Table
    participant MEM as Program Memory
    participant OUT as Output Module

    Note over CPU,OUT: START OF SCAN CYCLE
    CPU->>IN: Read all field inputs
    IN-->>IMG: Update Input Image Table
    Note over IMG: T_in (e.g., 0.8 ms)
    
    CPU->>IMG: Read current input states
    CPU->>MEM: Fetch instructions sequentially
    CPU->>CPU: Execute Ladder Logic rung by rung
    Note over CPU: T_prog (e.g., 8 ms)
    
    CPU->>OUT: Write to Output Image Table
    CPU->>OUT: Update physical output terminals
    Note over CPU,OUT: T_out (e.g., 0.6 ms)
    
    CPU->>CPU: Housekeeping and Communication
    Note over CPU: T_house (e.g., 2 ms)
    
    Note over CPU,OUT: END OF SCAN CYCLE — REPEAT
```

---

### 4.6 I/O Addressing Structure (Rack-Slot-Bit Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["PLC CHASSIS\nLocal Rack 0 and Expansion Racks 1-7"] --> B["RACK 0\nSlots 0 to 15"]
    A --> C["RACK 1\nSlots 0 to 15"]
    B --> D["SLOT 0\nPower Supply"]
    B --> E["SLOT 1\nInput Module\nAddress I:1/0 to I:1/15"]
    B --> F["SLOT 2\nOutput Module\nAddress O:2/0 to O:2/15"]
    B --> G["SLOT 3\nAnalog Input Module\nAddress I:3.0 to I:3.7"]
    B --> H["SLOT 4\nAnalog Output Module\nAddress O:4.0 to O:4.3"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Differentiate between a fixed (compact) PLC and a modular PLC with respect to I/O handling. List two advantages of modular PLCs in industrial applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Fixed (Compact) PLC | Modular PLC |
|---|---|---|
| **I/O Type** | Built-in, fixed number of I/O | Plug-in modules on a rack |
| **Expandability** | Limited or none | Highly expandable (extra racks) |
| **Maintenance** | Replace whole unit | Replace only faulty module |
| **Cost** | Lower for small systems | Higher initial cost, scalable |
| **Application** | Small machines | Large industrial processes |

**Two advantages of modular PLCs:**
1. **Flexibility:** I/O modules can be added, removed, or replaced as per process requirements without changing the CPU.
2. **Hot-Swappable Maintenance:** Faulty I/O modules can be replaced online without shutting down the entire plant, increasing uptime and productivity.

*(Marks Distribution: Definition + 1 mark, Comparison table + 1 mark, Advantages + 1 mark)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Explain the function of the Input Image Table and Output Image Table in a PLC. Why is the program scan executed using these tables and not directly with the physical I/O?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Input Image Table (IIT)** is a dedicated block of CPU memory that stores a **one-bit-per-input snapshot** of all field input states at the start of every scan. Similarly, the **Output Image Table (OIT)** is a memory block holding the desired state of all outputs as set by the program.

**Why use Image Tables:**
1. **Determinism:** The program always reads a **consistent, time-stamped snapshot** of all inputs, avoiding race conditions where inputs might change mid-scan.
2. **Synchronization:** All outputs are updated **simultaneously** at the end of the scan, preventing partial or transient output states that could damage actuators.
3. **Speed:** Reading from high-speed CPU memory is **1000× faster** than scanning physical I/O terminals.

*(Marks Distribution: Definition of IIT + 0.5, Definition of OIT + 0.5, Reason 1 + 1 mark, Reason 2 + 0.5, Reason 3 + 0.5)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **hierarchical program organization** of a modern PLC with reference to **Tasks, Programs, Routines, and Tags**. Use a suitable diagram to illustrate the hierarchy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **internal architecture of a typical AC output module** and explain the role of each block. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 Marks]**

The modern PLC program is organized in a **strict four-level hierarchy**, as defined by the IEC 61131-3 standard:

**Level 1 — Task [2 Marks]:**
A Task is the **highest-level scheduling unit** in the PLC. It dictates *when* a set of programs will execute.
- **Continuous Task:** Runs continuously in the background whenever the CPU has spare time.
- **Periodic Task:** Runs at a fixed, user-defined time interval (e.g., every 50 ms).
- **Event Task:** Triggered by a specific event such as an input change, alarm, or fault condition.

**Level 2 — Program [1.5 Marks]:**
A Program is a **logical container of routines** that are related to a specific machine function. For example, in a bottling plant, separate programs may exist for "Bottle_Filling", "Cap_Sealing", and "Conveyor_Control".

**Level 3 — Routine [2 Marks]:**
A Routine contains the **actual executable code** in any of the five IEC languages (Ladder Diagram, Function Block Diagram, Structured Text, Instruction List, or Sequential Function Chart).
- **Main Routine:** The primary routine, always executed first when the program runs.
- **Sub-Routine:** Called on-demand by the main routine for specific sub-functions.
- **Fault Routine:** Executed only when a designated fault condition occurs.

**Level 4 — Tag [1.5 Marks]:**
A Tag is a **named variable** with a defined data type (BOOL, INT, REAL, TIMER, COUNTER). Tags are referenced by routines to read inputs, store intermediate values, and drive outputs.

*(Total: 7 Marks — Awarded as per above distribution.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 Marks]**

The **AC output module** delivers 110/230 V AC power to field loads such as solenoid valves, motor starters, and indicator lamps. Its internal architecture contains the following blocks:

1. **CPU Backplane Interface (Address Decoder) [1 Mark]:**
   Receives the 5 V logic signal from the CPU backplane and decodes the module's **rack/slot address** to identify which specific bit (terminal) is being updated.

2. **Output Latch (Memory Register) [1 Mark]:**
   Stores the ON/OFF status of each output bit for the **entire scan duration**, ensuring the output does not flicker between program steps.

3. **Optical Isolator [1.5 Marks]:**
   An **optocoupler (LED + Phototransistor)** provides **galvanic isolation of 1500 V RMS**, protecting the low-voltage CPU from high-voltage transients on the field side.

4. **Zero-Crossing Detector (for TRIAC outputs) [1 Mark]:**
   Detects when the AC mains voltage crosses zero volts, and triggers the TRIAC **only at this instant**. This eliminates inrush current and electrical noise (RFI).

5. **Pre-Driver and TRIAC [1.5 Marks]:**
   The TRIAC (Triode for Alternating Current) is the **power switching element** that handles the actual 230 V AC load current. A pre-driver transistor amplifies the logic-level signal to drive the TRIAC gate.

6. **Snubber Circuit (RC Network) [1 Mark]:**
   A resistor-capacitor network connected across the TRIAC to **suppress voltage spikes** generated by switching inductive loads (solenoids, transformers), preventing false triggering.

*(Total: 7 Marks — Awarded as per above distribution. Add the diagram from SECTION 4.3 for full marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **organization of data in a PLC** using the **Allen-Bradley data file structure** (O0, I1, S2, B3, T4, C5, N7, F8). List the function of any **four** of them. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **internal architecture of a typical DC input module** with a block diagram. Discuss the importance of **optical isolation** and **input filtering** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 Marks]**

In Allen-Bradley SLC 500 PLCs, data is organized into **predefined data files** identified by a **single-letter type** and a **file number (0–9)**. Each file can hold up to **256 elements (words)** of 16 bits each.

**Data File Organization Table [3 Marks]:**

| File | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| O0 | Output | Stores status sent to output modules |
| I1 | Input | Stores status from input modules |
| S2 | Status | Processor status, fault bits, mode |
| B3 | Bit | General-purpose binary storage |
| T4 | Timer | Timer PV, preset, control bits |
| C5 | Counter | Counter PV, preset, control bits |
| N7 | Integer | 16-bit signed integer storage |
| F8 | Float | 32-bit floating-point storage |
| R6 | Control | Sequencer & shift-register control |

**Function of Four Selected Files [4 Marks — 1 Mark Each]:**

1. **O0 (Output File):** Holds the **status bits sent to the output modules** at the end of each scan. The address format is `O:s/b` (slot/bit).

2. **I1 (Input File):** Stores the **real-time status of all physical inputs** read during the input scan. Addressed as `I:s/b`.

3. **T4 (Timer File):** Each timer element uses **3 words**: Word 0 = Preset Value (PRE), Word 1 = Accumulated Value (ACC), Word 2 = Control Word (status bits: EN, TT, DN).

4. **N7 (Integer File):** Stores **16-bit signed integers** (-32,768 to +32,767) used for mathematical operations, setpoints, and analog signal scaling.

*(Total: 7 Marks — Awarded as per above distribution.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 Marks]**

A **DC input module** interfaces 24 V DC field devices (push buttons, sensors) with the PLC's 5 V logic CPU. Its internal architecture is:

1. **Field Terminal Block [0.5 Mark]:** Screw terminals where the field wiring (24 V DC source and sensor contacts) is connected.

2. **Protection Stage [1 Mark]:** A **bidirectional TVS (Transient Voltage Suppressor) diode** clamps voltage spikes, and a **fuse** protects against over-current faults.

3. **RC Filter / Debounce Circuit [1.5 Marks]:** An **RC low-pass filter** (typically $\tau = RC \approx 10$ ms) eliminates **contact bounce** from mechanical switches and **high-frequency electrical noise** (EMI/RFI).

4. **Optical Isolator [2 Marks]:** An **optocoupler** (LED on the field side + Phototransistor on the logic side) provides **galvanic isolation of 1500 V RMS**, ensuring that any high-voltage fault on the field side cannot damage the CPU.

5. **Schmitt Trigger [1 Mark]:** A comparator with **hysteresis** that converts the slow, noisy analog signal from the optocoupler into a **clean, sharp 0 V / 5 V logic level** suitable for the CPU.

6. **Address Decoder & Input Register [1 Mark]:** Decodes the **rack/slot address** and writes the 16-bit word into the corresponding location of the **Input Image Table** in CPU memory.

**Importance of Optical Isolation [Bonus +0.5 Mark]:**
- Protects the **delicate CPU electronics** from field-side voltage surges, transients, and ground loops.
- **Breaks ground loops** between the field wiring and the CPU, eliminating noise.

**Importance of Input Filtering [Bonus +0.5 Mark]:**
- Eliminates **contact bounce** (10–50 ms typical) from mechanical switches.
- Rejects **high-frequency EMI** from VFDs, motors, and switching power supplies.

*(Total: 7 Marks — Awarded as per above distribution.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing I/O Image Table addresses with physical I/O:** Students often write "the CPU reads `I:1/5` directly from the field." The **correct** statement is: "The input module writes the status of terminal 5 to `I:1/5` in the Input Image Table, and the CPU reads it from there."
> 2. **Forgetting to include housekeeping time:** Many students compute $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out}$ only. The KTU 2024 marking scheme **explicitly allocates 1 mark for $T_{house}$** in scan time problems.
> 3. **Mixing up sink (PNP) and source (NPN) input wiring:** European/IEC standard uses **PNP (sourcing sensors)**, while Japanese standard uses **NPN (sinking sensors)**. Always mention the wiring convention explicitly.
> 4. **Incorrect addressing format:** Writing `I1/5` instead of the correct `I:1/5` (with the colon) loses 0.5 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram in 7-mark questions:** A 7-mark question on I/O modules **mandates a labelled block diagram**. Without it, expect a **2-mark penalty**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1**

- **PLC Program Organization** has **4 levels:** Task → Program → Routine → Tag.
- **Tasks** are of **3 types:** Continuous, Periodic, Event.
- **Routines** are of **3 types:** Main, Sub-routine, Fault.
- **Scan Cycle** has **4 phases:** Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan → Housekeeping.
- **Input Image Table** is a **memory snapshot** of all inputs, taken at the start of the scan.
- **Output Image Table** holds the desired output states, written to physical modules at the end of the scan.
- **I/O Modules** provide **galvanic isolation** via **optocouplers** (1500 V RMS typical).
- **I/O Module Types:** Digital Input (DI), Digital Output (DO), Analog Input (AI), Analog Output (AO), Specialty (High-Speed Counter, Motion, Communication).
- **Output Switching Elements:** Transistor (DC), TRIAC (AC), Relay (universal).
- **Allen-Bradley Data Files:** O0, I1, S2, B3, T4, C5, N7, F8, R6.
- **Standard Addressing:** `Type:Slot/Word/Bit` (e.g., `I:1/5`, `O:2/3`).
- **Scan Time Formula:** $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$.
- **Worst-Case Response Time:** $T_{worst} = T_{scan} + T_{in\,delay} + T_{out\,delay}$.
- **Rack Sizing:** $N_{racks} = \lceil N_{total} / 256 \rceil$ (for 16-slot racks with 16-point modules).
- **Analog Resolution:** $V_{LSB} = V_{FS} / 2^n$ (12-bit, 14-bit, 16-bit common).
- **Modular PLCs** are preferred over compact PLCs for **scalability, hot-swappable maintenance, and high I/O counts**.
- **IEC 61131-3** is the **international standard** governing PLC programming languages and program organization.
- **Typical Scan Time** for mid-range PLCs: **10–50 ms**.
- **Always draw a block diagram** for 7-mark questions on I/O module architecture.
- **Always mention isolation and filtering** in 7-mark answers about input modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete AC/DC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAET604 - LOGIC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Discrete AC/DC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the architecture of a **Logic and Distributed Control System (LDCS)**, a **Discrete I/O module** is a field-interface subsystem that exchanges binary (ON/OFF, 1/0) electrical signals with industrial actuators and sensors. When classified by the electrical nature of the signal, these modules fall into two families:

- **Discrete DC I/O** — Operates on direct-current voltage levels standardized at **5 V DC**, **12 V DC**, **24 V DC**, **48 V DC**, and **110 V DC**, with **24 V DC** being the dominant industrial standard.
- **Discrete AC I/O** — Operates on alternating-current line levels standardized at **24 V AC**, **120 V AC**, and **230 V AC** at **50 Hz / 60 Hz** mains frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** "Discrete Input/Output modules convert binary field signals (AC or DC) into logic-level signals compatible with the controller, and vice versa, using optical isolation, signal conditioning, and solid-state or electromechanical switching."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **translator sitting between two people who speak different languages**.

- The **field** (sensors, limit switches, solenoid valves) "speaks" in raw electrical — either 230 V AC hum from a conveyor motor contactor, or 24 V DC pulses from a proximity sensor.
- The **controller CPU** (PLC, DCS controller) only "understands" low-voltage logic levels of 0 V or 5 V.
- The **Discrete I/O module is the translator** — it listens to the field's loud language, safely scales it down, and whispers a clean logic 0 or 1 to the CPU. Going the other way, it takes the CPU's tiny command and amplifies it back into the high-power language the field actuator can act upon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two types (AC & DC)?** Industrial history is the reason. Older plants standardized on **110/230 V AC** because AC power distribution is cheaper over long distances. Modern semiconductor-driven plants standardized on **24 V DC** because (a) batteries can back it up, (b) it is intrinsically safer in explosive atmospheres, and (c) sensors are simpler. A modern LDCS engineer must fluently speak **both** languages.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Parameter | Standard Value | Engineering Context |
|---|---|---|
| Industrial DC voltage | **24 V DC** | Most common sensor/actuator supply (IEC 61131-2) |
| Logic-level voltage | **5 V DC / 3.3 V DC** | Internal CPU logic |
| Industrial AC voltage (EU) | **230 V AC, 50 Hz** | Mains-coupled discrete signals |
| Industrial AC voltage (US) | **120 V AC, 60 Hz** | Mains-coupled discrete signals |
| Optical isolation voltage | **1500 V RMS – 2500 V RMS** | Galvanic safety barrier |
| Typical ON threshold (24 V DC) | **≥ 15 V DC** | IEC 61131-2 Type 1/3 |
| Typical OFF threshold (24 V DC) | **≤ 5 V DC** | IEC 61131-2 Type 1/3 |
| Sink current per output | **0.5 A – 2 A** | Module rating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **24 V DC** level is the de-facto industrial standard for two reasons: (1) it is low enough to be considered safe per NFPA 79 / IEC 60204, and (2) it is high enough to reject industrial electromagnetic noise picked up over long cable runs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Discrete I/O Channel

Every discrete I/O channel — whether AC or DC, input or output — is built from the same five functional blocks. Understanding this universality is a high-yield KTU insight.

### Block-by-Block Decomposition

1. **Protection Block (Stage 1)**
   - **Purpose:** Absorbs transient over-voltages caused by switching of inductive loads (relay coils, solenoids, motor contactors) and lightning-induced surges on long field cables.
   - **DC implementation:** Bidirectional TVS (Transient Voltage Suppressor) diode + reverse-polarity protection diode + fuse.
   - **AC implementation:** MOV (Metal-Oxide Varistor) + gas discharge tube + series PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient) resettable fuse.
   - *Why:* Industrial field cables run alongside VFD-driven motor cables and act as antennas for EMI.

2. **Signal Conditioning / Rectification Block (Stage 2)**
   - **DC input:** RC low-pass filter (debouncing) + Schmitt trigger comparator.
   - **AC input:** Bridge rectifier → RC filter → Schmitt trigger. The rectifier converts bipolar AC into unipolar DC for the logic stage.
   - *Why:* Mechanical switch contacts "bounce" for 1–10 ms; without debouncing the CPU would see multiple false edges.

3. **Optical Isolation Block (Stage 3)**
   - **Component:** **Optocoupler** (an LED + a phototransistor in one IC package, e.g., PC817, 4N35).
   - **Function:** Provides galvanic isolation of typically **1500 V RMS – 5000 V RMS** between the field side and the logic side.
   - *Why:* A fault on the field side (e.g., 230 V AC accidentally shorted onto a 24 V DC line) must never propagate into and destroy the CPU. Optical isolation is the **single most important safety feature** of any discrete module.

4. **Logic-Level Translation (Stage 4)**
   - Converts the optocoupler's collector signal into a clean 5 V / 3.3 V CMOS logic level.
   - Uses a pull-up resistor and a Schmitt-trigger inverter (e.g., 74HC14).

5. **Output Driver (Stage 5) — for Output Modules**
   - **DC output driver:** **BJT (NPN sinking), MOSFET (N-channel sinking or P-channel sourcing), or Darlington pair.**
   - **AC output driver:** **Triac** (e.g., BTA16, BTA25) or **Solid-State Relay (SSR)**.
   - **Universal driver:** Electromechanical relay (can switch AC or DC, but slower, ~10 ms operate time).

---

## 2.2 Sinking vs. Sourcing — The Most-Tested KTU Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD CONCEPT:** Sinking and sourcing describe the *direction of conventional current flow* across the output (or input) device. Mis-wiring these is the #1 cause of failed commissioning.

- **Sourcing Output (PNP):** The output device *sources* (pushes) current **OUT** of its terminal into the load. The load's other terminal returns to **0 V (common/negative rail)**. Common in **Europe**.
- **Sinking Output (NPN):** The output device *sinks* (pulls) current **FROM** the load down to **0 V**. The load's other terminal is tied to **+V supply**. Common in **Asia**.

For an **input**, the conventions mirror: a **sourcing sensor** (PNP) sources current into a **sinking input**; a **sinking sensor** (NPN) sinks current from a **sourcing input**.

**Compatibility Rule:** Sourcing-output ↔ Sinking-input ; Sinking-output ↔ Sourcing-input. **Never** connect two sourcing outputs in parallel without a diode-OR.

---

## 2.3 AC vs DC Discrete Output Devices — Comparison

| Property | DC Transistor (MOSFET/BJT) | AC Triac / SSR | Electromechanical Relay |
|---|---|---|---|
| Switching speed | **< 1 µs** (PWM capable) | **< 10 ms** (zero-cross turn-on) | **5 – 15 ms** |
| Lifetime | **> 10⁹ operations** (no contact) | **> 10⁸ operations** | **10⁵ – 10⁷ operations** |
| Leakage current (OFF) | **< 1 µA** | **1 – 10 mA** (snubber) | **0** (open contact) |
| Voltage rating | Up to **60 V DC** typical | Up to **600 V AC** | **250 V AC / 30 V DC** |
| Audible noise | None | None | **Yes (click)** |
| Inductive load handling | Needs flyback diode | Needs snubber (RC or MOV) | Built-in (contact gap) |
| Cost per channel | Lowest | Medium | Higher |

> [!NOTE]
> **Triac** is a "bidirectional thyristor" — effectively two SCRs in anti-parallel. It latches ON when gate-triggered, and naturally turns OFF only at the next **AC zero-cross**. This is why a 50 Hz triac switches at most every **10 ms** (half-cycle).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{\text{ON}} \geq 0.6 \cdot V_{\text{rated}}$ | IEC 61131-2 guaranteed ON threshold | V |
| 2 | $V_{\text{OFF}} \leq 0.4 \cdot V_{\text{rated}}$ | IEC 61131-2 guaranteed OFF threshold | V |
| 3 | $V_{\text{HYS}} = V_{\text{ON}} - V_{\text{OFF}}$ | Hysteresis voltage (noise immunity margin) | V |
| 4 | $R_{\text{LED}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{supply}} - V_{\text{F}}}{I_{\text{F}}}$ | Optocoupler LED current-limiting resistor | Ω |
| 5 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | RC debounce / snubber time constant | s |
| 6 | $f_{\text{debounce}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | RC low-pass corner frequency for debounce | Hz |
| 7 | $P_{\text{diss}} = V_{\text{CE(sat)}} \cdot I_{\text{load}}$ | Power dissipation in saturated DC output transistor | W |
| 8 | $I_{\text{RMS}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS load current for AC triac | A |
| 9 | $E_{\text{coil}} = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$ | Energy stored in an inductive DC load (needs flyback) | J |
| 10 | $V_{\text{peak(inv)}} \geq 1.4 \cdot V_{\text{RMS}}$ | Reverse-bridge DC link for AC-DC rectifier | V |
| 11 | $I_{\text{hold}} < I_{\text{load,min}}$ | Triac latch-current check (must stay latched) | A |
| 12 | $R_{\text{snub}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{supply}}}{I_{\text{peak,surge}}}$ | Snubber resistor sizing for triac | Ω |
| 13 | $C_{\text{snub}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{load}}}{2\pi f \cdot V_{\text{spike}}}$ | Snubber capacitor sizing | F |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU PITFALL:** When writing absolute value or magnitude in a markdown table, never use the pipe character `\vert` directly. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the table parser.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Discrete AC/DC I/O is the **workhorse interface** of every industrial control system. Application examples:

- **24 V DC sinking input:** Reading a PNP proximity sensor on a bottling line.
- **24 V DC sourcing output:** Energizing a 24 V DC solenoid valve on a pneumatic actuator.
- **230 V AC input:** Detecting the auxiliary contact of a motor contactor (run feedback).
- **230 V AC triac output:** Switching a 230 V AC heater band on a plastic extruder.
- **Relay output:** Driving a mixed AC/DC indicator lamp on a control panel.

In a modern DCS, a typical I/O rack may contain **32 channels of universal discrete I/O** per module, with each channel individually configurable as DI or DO, AC or DC, via software — a direct consequence of the standardized architecture described in §2.1.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Optocoupler LED Current-Limiting Resistor

**Problem:** A 24 V DC discrete input module uses a PC817 optocoupler. The optocoupler LED has a forward voltage $V_{\text{F}} = 1.2 \text{ V}$ at the recommended forward current $I_{\text{F}} = 10 \text{ mA}$. Determine the series current-limiting resistor $R_{\text{LED}}$ and verify power dissipation.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the circuit.**
The LED of the optocoupler is in series with a current-limiting resistor $R_{\text{LED}}$, connected to a 24 V DC field supply. The LED acts as the input indicator that triggers the phototransistor on the logic side.

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop.**

$$V_{\text{supply}} - V_{R_{\text{LED}}} - V_{\text{F}} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the voltage drop across the resistor.**

$$V_{R_{\text{LED}}} = V_{\text{supply}} - V_{\text{F}}$$

Substituting $V_{\text{supply}} = 24 \text{ V}$ and $V_{\text{F}} = 1.2 \text{ V}$:

$$V_{R_{\text{LED}}} = 24 \text{ V} - 1.2 \text{ V} = 22.8 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Ohm's Law to find $R_{\text{LED}}$.**

$$R_{\text{LED}} = \dfrac{V_{R_{\text{LED}}}}{I_{\text{F}}}$$

$$R_{\text{LED}} = \dfrac{22.8 \text{ V}}{10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}} = 2280 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Choose the nearest standard E24 value.**
The nearest standard E24 resistor is **2.2 kΩ** (2200 Ω).

**Step 6 — Verify the actual LED current with the chosen resistor.**

$$I_{\text{F,actual}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{supply}} - V_{\text{F}}}{R_{\text{LED,chosen}}} = \dfrac{22.8 \text{ V}}{2200 \text{ }\Omega} = 10.36 \text{ mA}$$

This is within ±5% of the design value of 10 mA — **acceptable**.

**Step 7 — Compute power dissipation in the resistor.**

$$P_{R_{\text{LED}}} = V_{R_{\text{LED}}} \cdot I_{\text{F,actual}}$$

$$P_{R_{\text{LED}}} = 22.8 \text{ V} \times 10.36 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} = 0.236 \text{ W}$$

Therefore the resistor must be rated for at least **0.5 W** (with a 2× safety derating, typical engineering practice).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RC Debounce Time Constant for a Mechanical Input

**Problem:** A mechanical limit switch on a conveyor has a worst-case contact bounce duration of **8 ms**. Design an RC low-pass filter such that the output settles to a clean logic level within the bounce window. The downstream Schmitt trigger has a threshold of 2.5 V and the input voltage is 24 V DC.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Establish the settling criterion.**
A first-order RC low-pass filter reaches approximately **95% of its final value** after **3 time constants** ($3\tau$). To suppress the 8 ms bounce:

$$3\tau \geq t_{\text{bounce}} = 8 \text{ ms}$$

$$\tau \geq \dfrac{8 \text{ ms}}{3} \geq 2.67 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Choose a convenient capacitor.**
Pick $C = 0.1 \text{ μF}$ (a standard value, non-polarized, film type).

**Step 3 — Compute the required resistor.**

$$R = \dfrac{\tau}{C} = \dfrac{2.67 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}{0.1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}} = 26.7 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Select the nearest standard E12 value.**
The nearest standard resistor is **27 kΩ**.

**Step 5 — Verify the actual settling time.**

$$\tau_{\text{actual}} = R \cdot C = 27{,}000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 2.7 \text{ ms}$$

$$t_{\text{settle}} = 3 \cdot \tau_{\text{actual}} = 3 \times 2.7 \text{ ms} = 8.1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6 — Verify that the time constant is faster than the system response requirement.**
The PLC scan time is typically **10 ms**; the filter settling time of 8.1 ms is shorter, so the bounce will be filtered out before the PLC reads the input. **Design valid.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Snubber Design for an AC Triac Driving a Solenoid

**Problem:** A BTA16 triac switches a 230 V AC, 50 Hz solenoid coil (highly inductive, $L = 2 \text{ H}$, $I = 0.5 \text{ A RMS}$). Design an RC snubber to limit the rate of rise of voltage ($dV/dt$) across the triac to below the specified **200 V/μs**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the $dV/dt$ requirement.**
At 50 Hz, the peak voltage is:

$$V_{\text{peak}} = V_{\text{RMS}} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 230 \times 1.414 = 325.3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Relate the $dV/dt$ to the snubber RC.**
When the triac turns off at zero-cross, the inductive load forces a residual current to flow, charging the snubber capacitor. The approximate $dV/dt$ is:

$$\dfrac{dV}{dt} \approx \dfrac{I_{\text{load}}}{C_{\text{snub}}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $C_{\text{snub}}$.**

$$C_{\text{snub}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{load}}}{(dV/dt)_{\text{allowed}}}$$

$$C_{\text{snub}} = \dfrac{0.5 \text{ A}}{200 \times 10^{6} \text{ V/s}} = 2.5 \text{ nF}$$

**Step 4 — Choose the next standard capacitor value.**
Standard value: **$C_{\text{snub}} = 4.7 \text{ nF}$** (safety margin).

**Step 5 — Size the snubber resistor to dissipate the stored energy.**
The resistor must discharge the capacitor between half-cycles. Energy stored per cycle:

$$E_{\text{cap}} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{\text{snub}} V_{\text{peak}}^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 4.7 \times 10^{-9} \times (325.3)^{2}$$

$$E_{\text{cap}} = 0.248 \text{ mJ per half-cycle}$$

The time between zero-crosses is **10 ms** (half of 20 ms). Required average power:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{cap}}}{T_{\text{half}}} = \dfrac{0.248 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0248 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Choose $R_{\text{snub}}$ for damping (typically 39 Ω – 100 Ω).**
Using $R_{\text{snub}} = 47 \text{ }\Omega$ (standard, 0.5 W rating). Power dissipation:

$$P_{R_{\text{snub}}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{peak}}^{2} \cdot C_{\text{snub}}}{2 \cdot R_{\text{snub}} \cdot T_{\text{half}}}$$

$$P_{R_{\text{snub}}} = \dfrac{(325.3)^2 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 47 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0528 \text{ W}$$

A **0.25 W resistor** is sufficient.

**Final Snubber Design:** $R_{\text{snub}} = 47 \text{ }\Omega / 0.25 \text{ W}$ and $C_{\text{snub}} = 4.7 \text{ nF / 630 V}$ in series, connected across the triac's MT1–MT2 terminals.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Channel-Type Decision Table

The following matrix should be memorized for the KTU exam — it is the single most useful summary for picking the right discrete I/O channel type.
