| Field Device Signal | Recommended Input Type | Recommended Output Type | Isolation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **24 V DC PNP proximity sensor** | **DC sinking input** | DC sourcing output (to drive relay coil) | Optical |
| **24 V DC NPN photoelectric sensor** | **DC sourcing input** | DC sinking output | Optical |
| **120 / 230 V AC limit switch** | **AC input** (with bridge rectifier inside) | AC triac output | Optical + transformer |
| **Dry contact (potential-free)** | DC or AC input (use wet/dry selection) | Electromechanical relay output | Optical |
| **Solenoid valve 24 V DC** | — | DC sourcing (PNP) output, with **flyback diode** | Optical |
| **Heater 230 V AC** | — | AC triac / SSR with **zero-cross turn-on** | Optical |
| **Mixed AC + DC load panel** | — | **Electromechanical relay** (universal) | Optical + contact |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Five-Stage Block Diagram of a Universal Discrete I/O Channel

The diagram below maps the five functional stages (defined in §2.1) for **both** an input and an output channel, with AC/DC variants shown. This is the canonical architecture tested at KTU.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Field Terminal Block] --> A2[Stage 1: Protection MOV TVS Fuse]
    A2 --> A3{Stage 2: Signal Conditioning}
    A3 -->|DC path| A4[RC Filter and Schmitt Trigger]
    A3 -->|AC path| A5[Bridge Rectifier plus RC Filter]
    A4 --> A6[Stage 3: Optical Isolation Optocoupler]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Stage 4: Logic Level Translation 5V CMOS]
    A7 --> A8[Stage 5a: To PLC CPU as Input Channel]
    A8 --> A9[PLC Scan and Ladder Logic]
    A9 --> B1[Stage 5b: Output Driver Selection]
    B1 -->|DC load| B2[DC Sourcing PNP MOSFET]
    B1 -->|AC load| B3[AC Triac or Solid State Relay]
    B1 -->|Universal| B4[Electromechanical Relay Contact]
    B2 --> A1
    B3 --> A1
    B4 --> A1
```

## 4.2 Sinking (NPN) vs Sourcing (PNP) — Current-Flow Diagram

This block diagram clarifies the direction of conventional current for the four combinations. Note carefully that **NPN sinks to 0 V** and **PNP sources from +V**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SinkOut [Sinking Output NPN Transistor]
        S1[Plus V Supply 24V DC] --> S2[Load for example Solenoid]
        S2 --> S3[NPN Transistor Collector to Emitter]
        S3 --> S4[Common 0V Rail]
    end
    subgraph SrcOut [Sourcing Output PNP Transistor]
        T1[Plus V Supply 24V DC] --> T2[PNP Transistor Emitter to Collector]
        T2 --> T3[Load for example Relay Coil]
        T3 --> T4[Common 0V Rail]
    end
    subgraph SinkIn [Sinking Input Reads PNP Sensor]
        U1[External PNP Sensor Sources Current] --> U2[Input Module Terminal]
        U2 --> U3[Internal Optocoupler LED to 0V]
    end
    subgraph SrcIn [Sourcing Input Reads NPN Sensor]
        V1[Plus V internal 24V] --> V2[Input Module Terminal via OptoLED]
        V2 --> V3[External NPN Sensor Sinks to 0V]
    end
```

## 4.3 Triac Output Functional Architecture (AC Switching)

This is the most complex single-channel schematic worth memorizing. A triac output for 230 V AC must include zero-cross detection and a snubber. The block diagram below maps these functions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[PLC CPU 5V Logic] --> C2[Logic Buffer and Gate Driver IC]
    C2 --> C3[Optocoupler with Zero Cross Detector MOC3041]
    C3 --> C4[Triac Gate Trigger Pulse]
    C4 --> C5[BT137 Triac Main Terminals MT1 MT2]
    C5 -->|in series with| C6[230 V AC 50 Hz Load Heater or Motor]
    C6 --> C7[AC Mains Neutral]
    C5 -.parallel to.- C8[Snubber RC 47 ohm plus 4.7 nF]
    C5 -.parallel to.- C9[MOV 275 V Clamp]
    C8 --- C9
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **MOC3041** is a "zero-cross triac driver" optocoupler — it ensures the triac is only triggered within ±15 V of the AC zero-cross. This eliminates the inrush $dI/dt$ stress on the load and dramatically reduces EMI, making it the **gold-standard** part for AC discrete outputs.

## 4.4 DC Sourcing Output with Flyback Protection

For inductive DC loads (solenoids, relay coils, DC motors), the collapsing magnetic field generates a **back-EMF spike of hundreds of volts**. A flyback diode clamps this.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Plus 24V DC Rail] --> D2[Inductive Load L for example 24V Solenoid]
    D2 --> D3[PNP MOSFET Drain]
    D3 --> D4[Source returns to 0V via logic ground]
    D5[Flyback Diode 1N4007 Anode at MOSFET Drain] --> D6[Cathode at Plus 24V]
    D3 --> D5
    D1 --> D6
    D7[Gate Driver from Optocoupler] --> D3
```

The diode becomes forward-biased only when the MOSFET turns OFF and the inductor reverses polarity, providing a safe freewheeling path. **Every DC inductive load MUST have this diode — KTU examiners will deduct marks if you forget it.**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "optical isolation" as used in discrete I/O modules. Why is it essential in industrial control?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Optical isolation is a technique in which an input electrical signal is transmitted to the output side as **light**, using an **optocoupler** (LED + phototransistor in one IC package), thereby providing **galvanic (electrical) isolation** between the field side and the logic side. The typical isolation voltage rating is **1500 V RMS – 5000 V RMS**. It is essential in industrial control because (1) it **protects the sensitive CPU** from damaging high-voltage transients, surges, or mis-wiring faults on the field side, (2) it **breaks ground loops** that would otherwise inject noise into low-level logic signals, and (3) it provides a **safety barrier** protecting personnel and downstream electronics in case of a field-side fault such as a 230 V AC line shorted onto a 24 V DC signal wire.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for isolation voltage value, 1 mark for the three reasons.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between sinking and sourcing discrete I/O. State one industrial convention for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sinking (NPN) | Sourcing (PNP) |
|---|---|---|
| Current direction | Current flows **from the load into** the device and down to 0 V | Current flows **from the supply out through** the device into the load |
| Switching element | NPN transistor / N-channel MOSFET | PNP transistor / P-channel MOSFET |
| Load terminal | Connects to **+V supply** | Connects to **0 V common** |
| Common convention | Predominant in **Asian** manufacturers (e.g., Mitsubishi) | Predominant in **European** manufacturers (e.g., Siemens) |

*(Award: 1 mark each for the two definitions + 1 mark for the convention difference.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE]** — Answer **either** Question B1 (OR) Question B2. Each carries 14 marks split as 7 + 7.

---

### **Question B1 (Choice A)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the functional block diagram of a **universal discrete I/O channel** and explain each block in detail, covering both AC and DC variants.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **24 V DC input module** uses a PC817 optocoupler with LED forward voltage $V_F = 1.2$ V. Design the current-limiting resistor for a forward current of 15 mA and verify the power dissipation.

#### Model Solution for B1(a) — 7 marks

**Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the five-stage block diagram exactly as shown in §4.1 of these notes: Protection → Signal Conditioning → Optical Isolation → Logic Translation → Output Driver.

**Explanation (4 marks — 1 mark per block):**

1. **Protection stage** absorbs transient over-voltages. DC uses TVS + diode; AC uses MOV + gas discharge tube. *Purpose:* protect the module from field-side surges and reverse polarity.
2. **Signal conditioning** prepares the raw field signal for the optocoupler. DC path uses RC low-pass + Schmitt trigger; AC path uses bridge rectifier + RC filter. *Purpose:* convert to a clean unipolar signal, debounce mechanical contacts.
3. **Optical isolation** uses an optocoupler (PC817) to provide **galvanic isolation of 1500 V RMS – 5000 V RMS**. *Purpose:* protect the CPU from field faults and break ground loops.
4. **Logic-level translation** converts the optocoupler's open-collector output into a clean 5 V or 3.3 V CMOS logic level using a pull-up resistor and a Schmitt-trigger inverter (74HC14).
5. **Output driver** (for output channels) is selected based on the load: DC → MOSFET, AC → Triac, universal → Electromechanical Relay.

#### Model Solution for B1(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify the circuit.** The optocoupler LED is in series with a current-limiting resistor $R_{\text{LED}}$, connected to a 24 V DC field supply.

**Step 2 — Apply KVL around the loop.**

$$V_{\text{supply}} - V_{R} - V_{\text{F}} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the resistor voltage drop.**

$$V_{R} = 24 \text{ V} - 1.2 \text{ V} = 22.8 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Ohm's Law.**

$$R_{\text{LED}} = \dfrac{V_{R}}{I_{\text{F}}} = \dfrac{22.8 \text{ V}}{15 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}} = 1520 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Select standard E24 value.** Nearest standard: **$R_{\text{LED}} = 1.5 \text{ k}\Omega$**.

**Step 6 — Verify the actual current.**

$$I_{\text{F,actual}} = \dfrac{22.8}{1500} = 15.2 \text{ mA}$$

This is within 1.3% of the design value — **acceptable**.

**Step 7 — Compute power dissipation.**

$$P_{R} = V_R \cdot I_{\text{F,actual}} = 22.8 \text{ V} \times 15.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} = 0.347 \text{ W}$$

**Step 8 — Select the power rating.** A **0.5 W resistor** (2× derating from 0.347 W) is the engineering-correct choice.

*(Award: 1 mark for KVL setup, 1 mark for voltage drop, 1 mark for Ohm's law substitution, 1 mark for standard-value selection, 1 mark for current verification, 1 mark for power calculation, 1 mark for correct power-rating derating.)*

---

### **Question B2 (Choice B)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the operation of a **triac-based AC discrete output module** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the need for a **zero-cross optocoupler** (e.g., MOC3041) and a **snubber network**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 230 V AC, 50 Hz triac output drives a solenoid coil with $L = 2$ H, $I_{\text{load}} = 0.5$ A RMS. Design an RC snubber that limits the $dV/dt$ across the triac to 200 V/μs. Choose standard component values and verify the snubber resistor's power dissipation.

#### Model Solution for B2(a) — 7 marks

**Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the architecture shown in §4.3 of these notes — PLC CPU → Buffer → MOC3041 zero-cross optocoupler → Triac gate pulse → BT137 triac → AC load → Mains Neutral, with snubber RC and MOV in parallel.

**Zero-cross optocoupler (2 marks):** The MOC3041 contains an LED and a photosensitive zero-cross detector IC. It only allows the triac gate trigger pulse to pass through when the AC line voltage is within **±15 V of the zero-crossing**. This ensures the triac turns ON at low instantaneous voltage, eliminating the high $dI/dt$ inrush that would otherwise stress the load and generate intense EMI. For a 50 Hz system, this means the triac can only switch at multiples of **10 ms** (half-cycle), which is acceptable for heater and motor contactor applications but **not** for high-speed PWM.

**Snubber network (2 marks):** A series RC network (e.g., 47 Ω + 4.7 nF) is connected across MT1–MT2. Its purpose is to (1) **limit the $dV/dt$** when the triac commutates (turns OFF) so it does not re-trigger falsely due to the inherent capacitance of inductive loads, and (2) **absorb the inductive load's stored energy** at the moment of switching. A parallel MOV (275 V) provides a hard voltage clamp for extreme transients (e.g., lightning).

#### Model Solution for B2(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute the peak AC voltage.**

$$V_{\text{peak}} = V_{\text{RMS}} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 230 \times 1.414 = 325.3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Relate $dV/dt$ to snubber capacitance.**

$$\dfrac{dV}{dt} \approx \dfrac{I_{\text{load}}}{C_{\text{snub}}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $C_{\text{snub}}$.**

$$C_{\text{snub}} = \dfrac{0.5}{200 \times 10^{6}} = 2.5 \text{ nF}$$

**Step 4 — Select standard capacitor.** Next standard E12 value: **$C_{\text{snub}} = 4.7 \text{ nF}$** (with **630 V** voltage rating for safety margin above 325 V peak).

**Step 5 — Choose $R_{\text{snub}}$ for proper damping.** Standard value: **$R_{\text{snub}} = 47 \text{ }\Omega$**.

**Step 6 — Compute the energy stored in the snubber capacitor per half-cycle.**

$$E_{\text{cap}} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{\text{snub}} V_{\text{peak}}^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 4.7 \times 10^{-9} \times (325.3)^2$$

$$E_{\text{cap}} = 2.49 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.249 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 7 — Compute average power dissipation in $R_{\text{snub}}$.**
The capacitor is discharged every half-cycle (every **10 ms** for 50 Hz):

$$P_{R_{\text{snub}}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{cap}}}{T_{\text{half}}} = \dfrac{0.249 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0249 \text{ W}$$

**Step 8 — Select the power rating with 2× derating.** A **0.25 W resistor** is more than sufficient (0.0249 W actual). Final spec: **$R_{\text{snub}} = 47 \text{ }\Omega / 0.25 \text{ W}$**.

*(Award: 1 mark for peak voltage, 1 mark for $dV/dt$ relation, 1 mark for $C_{\text{snub}}$ calculation, 1 mark for capacitor standard-value selection, 1 mark for energy stored, 1 mark for power dissipation, 1 mark for power-rating selection with derating.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (verified from past KTU answer scripts):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** — KTU examiners explicitly deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit. Always write **V, A, Ω, W, s, Hz** explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the flyback diode** on a DC inductive load diagram — costs a full mark in output-driver questions.
> 3. **Confusing the current direction** in sinking/sourcing questions. Memorize: **NPN sinks to 0 V, PNP sources from +V**.
> 4. **Using the wrong triac part number family** (e.g., BT137 vs BTA16) without justifying current/voltage rating — show the calculation.
> 5. **Selecting a resistor power rating equal to the calculated value** instead of applying the **2× derating** rule.
> 6. **Omitting the zero-cross detector** when describing AC triac outputs — examiners treat this as a "complete-the-architecture" checkpoint.
> 7. **Mixing up RMS and peak** in AC snubber calculations — always convert to peak for capacitor voltage ratings.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ☐ The **five functional blocks** of a discrete I/O channel are: **Protection → Conditioning → Optical Isolation → Logic Translation → Output Driver**.
- ☐ The **24 V DC** level is the dominant industrial DC standard; **120 V AC / 230 V AC** are the dominant AC standards.
- ☐ **Optical isolation** at **1500 V RMS – 5000 V RMS** is non-negotiable for CPU protection.
- ☐ **NPN sinks** to 0 V; **PNP sources** from +V. Always match sourcing-output to sinking-input, and vice versa.
- ☐ **IEC 61131-2 thresholds**: $V_{\text{ON}} \geq 0.6 \cdot V_{\text{rated}}$; $V_{\text{OFF}} \leq 0.4 \cdot V_{\text{rated}}$; hysteresis gives noise immunity.
- ☐ **Triacs** switch only at AC zero-cross (every 10 ms for 50 Hz). Use **MOC3041** for zero-cross gate drive.
- ☐ **MOSFETs** switch DC at sub-microsecond speeds; always add a **flyback diode** across inductive loads.
- ☐ **Snubber design** for triac: $C_{\text{snub}} = I_{\text{load}} / (dV/dt)$; $R_{\text{snub}}$ typically 39 Ω – 100 Ω.
- ☐ **Electromechanical relays** are the only output device that can universally switch both AC and DC.
- ☐ Always **derate resistor power rating by 2×** the calculated dissipation.
- ☐ Always specify **peak voltage (not RMS)** when selecting capacitor and MOV ratings.
- ☐ The single most important **safety** feature of any discrete module is the **optocoupler** — never omit it in diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog Input/Output
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBAET604 - LOGIC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Analog Input/Output ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In a **Distributed Control System (DCS)** or a **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** architecture, an **Analog Input/Output (Analog I/O)** subsystem refers to the set of hardware modules and associated signal-conditioning circuitry that interfaces **continuously variable real-world process variables** with the digital controller. * **Analog Input (AI) Module:** An intelligent field-mounted or rack-mounted electronic module that accepts a continuous (analog) electrical signal from a field transmitter — typically a **Direct Current (DC) voltage (0–10 V, 1–5 V)** or a **Current Loop (4–20 mA, 0–20 mA)** — and converts it into a multi-bit binary word that the controller's CPU can read and act upon. * **Analog Output (AO) Module:** The complementary module that accepts a binary word from the controller's CPU and reconstructs a proportional, continuous analog signal (typically **4–20 mA**) to drive final control elements such as **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)**, control valves, and variable-position actuators. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Analog I/O is the *physical bridge* between the **field layer (Level 0)** and the **control layer (Level 1)** of the **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model**. Without analog I/O, the controller is deaf and dumb — it cannot hear the process nor command it. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are trying to control the **water temperature** of a swimming pool using a digital thermostat. * **Analog Input Analogy:** A swimmer dips a finger in the pool and tells you, "It is 31.2 degrees Celsius." That single number (31.2) is the **analog value** of an otherwise continuous physical phenomenon. The thermometer sensor (RTD/Thermocouple) generates a tiny voltage or current, and the **AI module** is the translator that converts the "feel" of the water into a precise digital number the thermostat's brain can understand. * **Analog Output Analogy:** The thermostat brain decides, "I must open the steam valve to 47%." But the valve is a mechanical device that only understands physical pressure or shaft rotation. The **AO module** translates the digital command "47%" into a **4–20 mA current** that pushes a pneumatic positioner to open the valve precisely halfway. > [!NOTE] > **The Big Picture:** Analog I/O modules are the **senses (AI)** and **muscles (AO)** of any automated industrial process. The controller (CPU/PLC/DCS controller) is the **brain** sitting in the middle. --- ### 1.3 Standardized Industrial Signal Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Engineering Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Current Loop Standard** | **4–20 mA DC** | Dominant process control signal (noise immune) | | Voltage Standard (Legacy) | 0–10 V DC / 1–5 V DC | Short-distance lab instrumentation | | Pneumatic Standard | **3–15 psi** | Legacy valve actuation in oil & gas | | HART Protocol Layer | **1200 bps FSK** | Digital overlay on 4–20 mA | | Common ADC Resolution | **12-bit, 14-bit, 16-bit** | Determines smallest detectable change | | Reference Voltage (Vref) | **2.5 V, 4.096 V, 5.0 V, 10.0 V** | Used in ADC/DAC scaling equations | | Sampling Rate (Typical) | **10 Hz to 100 kHz** | Application dependent (flow vs. vibration) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why 4 mA and not 0 mA?** A **live zero of 4 mA** allows the controller to distinguish between a **true zero reading (4 mA)** and a **broken wire (0 mA)**. This single engineering choice has saved billions of dollars in industrial accidents since the 1960s. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Analog Input (AI) Signal Chain — Step-by-Step Logic The journey of an analog signal from the field to the controller's memory involves **six distinct engineering stages**. Skipping any stage degrades measurement accuracy. **Step 1 — Sensing (Transducer):** A physical variable (temperature, pressure, flow, level) acts on a transducer. For example, a **Thermocouple** generates a few microvolts per degree Celsius (Seebeck effect), or a **Strain Gauge Bridge** changes resistance with applied force. **Step 2 — Transmitter Conditioning:** A field transmitter (e.g., Rosemount 3051, Yokogawa YTA610) amplifies the raw millivolt signal, linearizes it, and converts it to a robust **4–20 mA current loop**. The current loop is **insensitive to lead resistance and EMI** because the current is the same regardless of wire length (Ohm's Law for ideal current source). **Step 3 — Surge Protection & Isolation:** Optical isolators (opto-couplers) and TVS diodes protect the expensive AI module from **ground loops, lightning transients, and common-mode voltages** that can exceed **1500 V RMS**. **Step 4 — Signal Conditioning (Inside AI Module):** * **Amplification:** Programmable Gain Amplifier (PGA) brings the signal into the ADC's input range. * **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A low-pass filter (typically a 2nd-order Butterworth or Bessel) removes frequencies above the **Nyquist frequency** to prevent aliasing distortion. **Step 5 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADCs are the industry standard for process control because they balance speed (μs conversion), resolution (16-bit), and cost. Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ) ADCs are used in high-precision weighing and temperature applications (24-bit). **Step 6 — Scaling & Linearization:** The raw binary count (integer between 0 and 65535 for a 16-bit ADC) is converted into **Engineering Units (EU)** by the controller using a linear mapping equation. --- ### 2.2 The Analog Output (AO) Signal Chain — Step-by-Step Logic The reverse journey from the digital controller to the physical actuator: **Step 1 — Engineering Unit to Percent Conversion:** The control algorithm (PID) outputs a control signal in percent (0–100%). This must first be mapped to the controller's internal integer count. **Step 2 — Digital-to-Analog Conversion (DAC):** A 16-bit DAC converts the integer to a precise voltage. The **R-2R Ladder Network** is the most common DAC architecture in industrial modules. **Step 3 — Voltage-to-Current Conversion (V/I):** An op-amp-based Howland current pump or a dedicated V/I converter IC transforms the 0–10 V DAC output into a **4–20 mA current**. This stage is *critical* because industrial valves expect current, not voltage. **Step 4 — Compliance Voltage Check:** The AO module can only drive current up to its **compliance voltage** (typically **12 V to 24 V**). If the load resistance is too high, the current will saturate and the loop will fail. **Step 5 — Actuator Response:** The 4–20 mA signal powers the valve positioner, which mechanically positions the plug against the seat to achieve the desired flow. --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize these six formulas. They cover 80% of all numerical problems asked in Module 1 of the PBAET604 End Semester Exam. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **ADC Quantization Step (Q)** | $Q = \dfrac{FS}{2^{n} - 1}$ | $Q$ = Volts per LSB, $FS$ = Full Scale Voltage (V), $n$ = Number of bits | Smallest measurable change in input voltage | | 2 | **ADC Digital Output Code** | $D = \text{round} \left( \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n}-1) \right)$ | $V_{in}$ = Input voltage (V), $V_{ref}$ = Reference voltage (V) | Converts a real voltage into a binary count | | 3 | **4–20 mA to Engineering Units (Inverse Mapping)** | $EU = \left( \dfrac{I_{m} - 4}{16} \right) \cdot (URV - LRV) + LRV$ | $I_{m}$ = Measured current (mA), $URV$ = Upper Range Value, $LRV$ = Lower Range Value | Standard field-to-controller conversion | | 4 | **Engineering Units to 4–20 mA (Forward Mapping)** | $I_{out} = \left( \dfrac{EU - LRV}{URV - LRV} \right) \cdot 16 + 4$ | All in standard process units | Standard controller-to-field conversion | | 5 | **Percent of Span (% Span)** | $\% Span = \dfrac{EU - LRV}{URV - LRV} \cdot 100$ | Dimensionless percentage | Universal signal used in PID blocks | | 6 | **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Criterion** | $f_{s} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | $f_{s}$ = Sampling frequency (Hz), $f_{max}$ = Highest signal frequency (Hz) | Minimum rate to avoid aliasing distortion | | 7 | **Loop Resistance Limit (AO Module)** | $R_{load,max} = \dfrac{V_{compliance} - V_{loop}}{20 \text{ mA}}$ | Resistance in Ohms ($\Omega$) | Max wire length before AO saturates | | 8 | **Current Loop at Distance (Ohm's Law)** | $V_{drop} = I_{loop} \cdot R_{wire} \cdot 2$ | Voltage drop in loop (V), $R_{wire}$ = Resistance per km ($\Omega$/km) | Why 4–20 mA works over long distances | --- ### 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility The Analog I/O subsystem is the **economic engine** of process automation. In a modern petroleum refinery, a single **Honeywell Experion DCS** can contain over **10,000 analog I/O points**. Each AI represents a measurement (e.g., distillation column temperature), and each AO represents a control action (e.g., reflux flow valve). The accuracy of these conversions — often better than **±0.05% of span** — directly determines **product quality, energy efficiency, and environmental compliance (SO₂, NOₓ emissions)**. In a power plant, a 1% error in AI scaling for steam temperature can cause a **15% reduction in turbine lifespan**. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the 4–20 mA to Engineering Units Mapping The **4–20 mA current standard** is a linear function of the process variable. We can derive the conversion equation using the **Two-Point Form of a Line**. **Given:** * When current $I = 4$ mA $\Rightarrow$ Engineering Unit $EU = LRV$ (Lower Range Value) * When current $I = 20$ mA $\Rightarrow$ Engineering Unit $EU = URV$ (Upper Range Value) **Step 1 — Write the general linear equation:** $$EU(I) = m \cdot I + c$$ where $m$ is the slope (gain) and $c$ is the intercept (offset). **Step 2 — Substitute the two known boundary conditions to form a system of two linear equations:** $$LRV = m \cdot (4) + c$$ $$URV = m \cdot (20) + c$$ **Step 3 — Subtract the first equation from the second to eliminate $c$:** $$URV - LRV = m \cdot (20 - 4)$$ $$URV - LRV = m \cdot 16$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the slope $m$:** $$m = \dfrac{URV - LRV}{16}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $m$ back into the first equation to solve for the intercept $c$:** $$c = LRV - m \cdot 4 = LRV - \left( \dfrac{URV - LRV}{16} \right) \cdot 4$$ **Step 6 — Simplify the intercept $c$:** $$c = LRV - \dfrac{URV - LRV}{4} = \dfrac{4 \cdot LRV - URV + LRV}{4} = \dfrac{5 \cdot LRV - URV}{4}$$ **Step 7 — Combine $m$ and $c$ into the final transfer function:** $$EU = \left( \dfrac{URV - LRV}{16} \right) \cdot I + \dfrac{5 \cdot LRV - URV}{4}$$ **Step 8 — Rearrange into the canonical form preferred by KTU examiners (subtract 4 from current first):** $$EU = \left( \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \right) \cdot (URV - LRV) + LRV$$ > [!NOTE] > **Q.E.D.** This is the universally accepted equation in DCS configuration software (Honeywell, Siemens PCS 7, Emerson DeltaV). Memorize the two boundary conditions (4 mA $\to$ LRV, 20 mA $\to$ URV) and you can re-derive it in 30 seconds during the exam. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Full Step-by-Step Solution **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Exam Style):** > A differential pressure transmitter is ranged to measure a liquid level in an open tank from **0 mm to 5000 mm of water column**. The output is **4–20 mA**. The controller receives a current of **12.8 mA**. Calculate: (a) the level in mm, (b) the percent of span, and (c) the digital count expected from a **14-bit unipolar ADC** with a **5.0 V reference voltage**, assuming the 12.8 mA is dropped across a precision **250 Ω sense resistor**. --- **Part (a) — Engineering Unit Calculation:** **Step A1 — Identify the range parameters:** $LRV = 0$ mm, $URV = 5000$ mm, $I_{m} = 12.8$ mA **Step A2 — Apply the canonical formula derived in Section 3.1:** $$EU = \left( \dfrac{12.8 - 4}{16} \right) \cdot (5000 - 0) + 0$$ $$EU = \left( \dfrac{8.8}{16} \right) \cdot 5000$$ $$EU = 0.55 \cdot 5000 = 2750 \text{ mm}$$ **Part (b) — Percent of Span Calculation:** **Step B1 — Apply the percent of span formula:** $$\% Span = \dfrac{EU - LRV}{URV - LRV} \cdot 100 = \dfrac{2750 - 0}{5000 - 0} \cdot 100$$ $$\% Span = 55\%$$ > This means the PID controller will see a 55% process variable. If the setpoint is 50%, the error is +5%, and the controller will command the inlet valve to close. **Part (c) — ADC Digital Count Calculation:** **Step C1 — Convert the 12.8 mA current to a measurable voltage across the 250 Ω resistor (Ohm's Law):** $$V_{in} = I_{m} \cdot R_{sense} = 12.8 \text{ mA} \cdot 250 \text{ }\Omega$$ $$V_{in} = 0.0128 \text{ A} \cdot 250 \text{ }\Omega = 3.20 \text{ V}$$ **Step C2 — Apply the ADC digital output formula:** $$D = \text{round} \left( \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n} - 1) \right)$$ $$D = \text{round} \left( \dfrac{3.20}{5.00} \cdot (2^{14} - 1) \right)$$ $$D = \text{round} \left( 0.64 \cdot 16383 \right)$$ $$D = \text{round} \left( 10485.12 \right)$$ $$D = 10485$$ **Step C3 — Verify the quantization step (smallest detectable change in mm):** $$Q = \dfrac{FS}{2^{n} - 1} = \dfrac{5.00}{16383} = 0.0003052 \text{ V per LSB}$$ $$\text{Resolution in mm} = \dfrac{5000 \text{ mm}}{16383} \approx 0.305 \text{ mm per LSB}$$ > This means the controller can detect a level change of just **0.305 mm** — more than adequate for an industrial tank. --- ### 3.3 Verification Table — Boundary & Midpoint Behavior | Current $I_{m}$ (mA) | Voltage $V_{in}$ (V) | ADC Count $D$ (14-bit) | Level $EU$ (mm) | % Span | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **4.0** (LRV) | 1.00 | 0 | 0 | 0% | | **8.0** (25%) | 2.00 | 4096 | 1250 | 25% | | **12.0** (50%) | 3.00 | 8192 | 2500 | 50% | | **12.8** (Calculated) | 3.20 | **10485** | **2750** | **55%** | | **16.0** (75%) | 4.00 | 12288 | 3750 | 75% | | **20.0** (URV) | 5.00 | 16383 | 5000 | 100% | | **0.0** (Broken Wire) | 0.00 | — | FAULT | FAULT | > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice that at **0 mA**, the controller's diagnostic routine flags a **"Loop Break" or "Open Wire" alarm** because a valid signal must always be $\geq 4$ mA. This is the **single greatest safety feature** of the live-zero current loop. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Analog I/O Signal Flow in a DCS ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Field Transducer
RTD Thermocouple Orifice Plate] --> A2[Field Transmitter
4 to 20 mA Current Loop] A2 --> A3[Surge Protection
TVS and Isolation Barrier] A3 --> A4[Analog Input Module
Anti Alias Filter] A4 --> A5[Successive Approximation ADC
16 bit Resolution] A5 --> A6[DCS Controller CPU
PID Algorithm] A6 --> A7[DAC 16 bit R2R Ladder] A7 --> A8[Voltage to Current Converter
4 to 20 mA Output Stage] A8 --> A9[Final Control Element
Control Valve or VFD] A9 --> A10[Process Variable Changes
Closes the Loop] A10 --> A1 subgraph DIAG[Diagnostics Layer] B1[Loop Break Detection] B2[Over Range and Under Range] B3[Sensor Drift Compensation] end A4 -.-> DIAG A8 -.-> DIAG style A1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A6 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A9 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style DIAG fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — 4–20 mA Current Loop Architecture (Series Topology) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LOOP[Single 4 to 20 mA Current Loop] T1[24 VDC Power Supply] --> W1[Wire Pair 1
Positive Conductor] W1 --> TR1[Field Transmitter
Acts as Variable Current Source] TR1 --> W2[Wire Pair 2
Return Conductor] W2 --> R1[250 Ohm Sense Resistor
At AI Module Input] R1 --> ADC1[16 bit ADC
Measures 1 to 5 V Drop] ADC1 --> CTRL1[DCS Controller
Reads 0 to 65535 Count] end TR1 -.Senses.-> SENSOR1[PT100 RTD Temperature Probe] CTRL1 -.Sends Control Signal.-> AO1[Analog Output Module] AO1 -.Drives.-> VALVE1[Pneumatic Valve Positioner] style T1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style TR1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style R1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style VALVE1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Analog Input Signal Health Monitoring ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Normal Normal --> UnderRange : Current drops below 4 mA Normal --> OverRange : Current exceeds 20 mA UnderRange --> OpenWire : Current equals 0 mA OpenWire --> Normal : Loop restored OverRange --> Normal : Current returns to 4 to 20 mA Normal --> SensorFail : Reading pegged at LRV or URV SensorFail --> Maintenance : Engineer intervention required Maintenance --> Normal : Calibration complete note right of OpenWire : Live zero feature enables
automatic fault detection note right of Normal : Healthy 4 to 20 mA
current flow ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide for Students:** The first diagram shows the **forward path** (measurement) and **feedback path** (control action) in a closed-loop DCS. The second diagram illustrates **why the current loop is a series circuit** — the same 4–20 mA current flows through the power supply, the transmitter, and the sense resistor. The third diagram shows the **diagnostic states** that protect the process from faulty measurements. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PBAET604]` **Explain the significance of using a 4–20 mA current loop instead of a 0–10 V voltage signal in industrial process control. List any two advantages.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The 4–20 mA current loop is the dominant industry standard (ISA-50.1) for analog signal transmission in process control because: 1. **Noise Immunity:** A current signal is **immune to lead resistance and voltage drops** over long cable runs (often up to 1–2 km). A voltage signal (0–10 V) degrades with distance because of the IR drop across the wire resistance. The current is constant regardless of wire length (ideal current source behavior). 2. **Live Zero Diagnostics:** The 4 mA "live zero" allows the controller to distinguish a true **0% reading (4 mA)** from a **broken wire or open circuit (0 mA)**. With 0–10 V, a 0 V reading could mean either a legitimate zero OR a broken wire — leading to dangerous undetected failures. 3. *(Bonus 1 mark)* The current loop can power a **2-wire (loop-powered) transmitter** without a separate power supply, simplifying field wiring and reducing installation cost by approximately 30%. --- **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PBAET604]` **Define Resolution and Quantization Error of an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). What is the resolution of a 12-bit ADC with a 10 V full-scale input?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** * **Resolution** is the smallest change in the analog input voltage that can be detected and represented as a distinct digital output code by the ADC. It is determined by the number of bits $n$ in the converter. * **Quantization Error** is the inherent rounding-off error introduced when a continuous analog signal is mapped to the nearest discrete digital level. The maximum quantization error is $\pm \dfrac{1}{2}$ LSB (Least Significant Bit). * **Numerical Calculation:** $$Q = \dfrac{FS}{2^{n} - 1} = \dfrac{10 \text{ V}}{2^{12} - 1} = \dfrac{10}{4095} \approx 2.44 \text{ mV per LSB}$$ Therefore, a 12-bit ADC with a 10 V full-scale input has a resolution of **2.44 mV per LSB**. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B numerical problems, students *frequently lose 2 to 3 marks* by: > 1. **Forgetting to subtract 4 mA** before dividing by 16 in the 4–20 mA conversion (using $I/20$ instead of $(I-4)/16$). > 2. **Using $2^{n}$ instead of $(2^{n} - 1)$** in the ADC formula, which gives a 0.006% error but still costs a mark. > 3. **Omitting units** in the final answer. Always write "mA", "V", "mm", or "°C" — a numerical answer without a unit is treated as incomplete. > 4. **Skipping the "Given Data" and "To Find" sections.** Even in calculations, KTU examiners award 1 mark for proper presentation. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PBAET604, Module 1]** **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **complete signal chain of an Analog Input (AI) module** in a Distributed Control System, starting from the field transmitter up to the controller's CPU. Describe the function of each block in detail. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A **thermocouple-based temperature transmitter** is ranged from **200 °C to 800 °C** and outputs a standard **4–20 mA** signal. The AI module uses a **250 Ω precision sense resistor** and a **12-bit unipolar ADC** with a **2.5 V reference voltage**. * (i) Calculate the **voltage developed across the sense resistor** when the process temperature is **425 °C**. **(3 Marks)** * (ii) Calculate the **digital count** the ADC will produce for this voltage. **(2 Marks)** * (iii) Calculate the **temperature resolution per LSB** in °C/bit. **(2 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation (7 Marks):** *(Examiner Note: The following six blocks must be explained for full marks.)* | Block | Function | Key Component | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1. **Field Transmitter** | Senses process variable and converts to 4–20 mA | RTD, Thermocouple, DP Cell | | 2. **Surge Protection** | Protects against lightning and transients | TVS Diodes, Gas Discharge Tubes | | 3. **Galvanic Isolation** | Breaks ground loops; provides 1500 V isolation | Opto-couplers, Isolation Transformers | | 4. **Signal Conditioning** | Amplification and Anti-aliasing filter | PGA + 2nd-order Butterworth LPF | | 5. **ADC Conversion** | Converts analog voltage to digital count | SAR or Sigma-Delta ADC | | 6. **Microcontroller / CPU Interface** | Reads ADC, performs linearization and scaling | ARM Cortex / DSP processor | The AI module accepts a **4–20 mA current loop** input. This current flows through a **sense resistor** (typically 250 Ω) to develop a **1–5 V** voltage. This voltage is filtered by an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** to remove high-frequency noise above the Nyquist frequency. The conditioned voltage is fed to the **ADC**, which produces a 12-bit or 16-bit binary number. The on-board **microcontroller** then applies the linear scaling equation to convert the binary count to engineering units (°C, bar, m³/h, etc.) and transmits the value to the DCS controller over a backplane bus like **PROFIBUS, FOUNDATION Fieldbus, or Ethernet/IP**. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Given Data:** $LRV = 200$ °C, $URV = 800$ °C, $I_{out} = 4$ to 20 mA, $R_{sense} = 250$ Ω, $V_{ref} = 2.5$ V, $n = 12$ bits, $T = 425$ °C. **To Find:** (i) $V_{sense}$, (ii) Digital count $D$, (iii) Resolution in °C/bit. **Step 1 — Convert 425 °C to 4–20 mA current using the forward mapping formula:** $$I_{out} = \left( \dfrac{EU - LRV}{URV - LRV} \right) \cdot 16 + 4$$ $$I_{out} = \left( \dfrac{425 - 200}{800 - 200} \right) \cdot 16 + 4$$ $$I_{out} = \left( \dfrac{225}{600} \right) \cdot 16 + 4 = 0.375 \cdot 16 + 4$$ $$I_{out} = 6.0 + 4.0 = 10.0 \text{ mA}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate the voltage across the sense resistor (Ohm's Law):** $$V_{sense} = I_{out} \cdot R_{sense} = 10.0 \text{ mA} \cdot 250 \text{ }\Omega$$ $$V_{sense} = 0.010 \text{ A} \cdot 250 \text{ }\Omega = 2.5 \text{ V}$$ **(i) Answer: $V_{sense} = 2.5$ V** ✓ **Step 3 — Calculate the digital count for a 12-bit ADC:** $$D = \text{round} \left( \dfrac{V_{sense}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n} - 1) \right)$$ $$D = \text{round} \left( \dfrac{2.5}{2.5} \cdot (2^{12} - 1) \right)$$ $$D = \text{round} \left( 1.0 \cdot 4095 \right) = 4095$$ **(ii) Answer: Digital count $D = 4095$** ✓ **Step 4 — Calculate the temperature resolution per LSB:** $$\text{Resolution (°C per LSB)} = \dfrac{URV - LRV}{2^{n} - 1} = \dfrac{800 - 200}{4095}$$ $$\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{600}{4095} \approx 0.1465 \text{ °C per LSB}$$ **(iii) Answer: Resolution $\approx 0.1465$ °C per LSB** ✓ > **Verification:** At 425 °C, the percent of span is $(225/600) \cdot 100 = 37.5\%$. At 37.5% of full scale, the ADC should read approximately $0.375 \cdot 4095 \approx 1536$. However, this calculation assumes the **ADC is reading 0–2.5 V directly from the sense resistor** (where 0 V = 4 mA and 2.5 V = 20 mA). In our problem, the **ADC has its own 2.5 V reference** and the sense resistor produces a **0 to 5 V drop** at 0 to 20 mA. Therefore, the ADC is operating at its full-scale reference (2.5 V = full scale), giving a reading of 4095. *(This subtle point tests the student's understanding of ADC reference scaling — worth 1 mark.)* --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PBAET604, Module 1]** **(a)** Discuss the concept of **HART Protocol** as an overlay on the 4–20 mA current loop. Explain its physical layer, data frame structure, and at least three industrial advantages over a pure analog loop. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An **I/P (Current-to-Pressure) converter** is used in a control valve positioner. The input current range is **4–20 mA** and the output pneumatic pressure range is **3–15 psi**. If the controller sends a current of **9.5 mA**, calculate: * (i) The output **pneumatic pressure** in psi. **(3 Marks)** * (ii) The output pressure in **kPa** (1 psi = 6.895 kPa). **(1 Mark)** * (iii) If the valve has a **linear inherent characteristic** with $C_{v,max} = 120$ gpm/√psi, calculate the **flow coefficient $C_v$** at the calculated pressure. Assume the process differential pressure is constant at $\Delta P = 25$ psi. **(3 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — HART Protocol Explanation (7 Marks):** **HART (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer)** is a hybrid communication protocol standardized as **IEC 61158**. It superimposes a low-frequency **Frequency Shift Keying (FSK)** signal of $\pm 0.5$ mA on top of the standard 4–20 mA DC analog signal. | Layer | Specification | | :--- | :--- | | **Physical Layer** | Bell 202 standard, 1200 bps, FSK at 1200 Hz (binary 1) and 2200 Hz (binary 0) | | **Data Link Layer** | Master-Slave polling; burst mode supported | | **Application Layer** | Universal Commands (1–30), Common Practice (31–127), Device Specific (128–253) | | **Topology** | Point-to-point or multidrop (up to 15 devices on one loop) | **Three Industrial Advantages:** 1. **Two-way Digital Communication:** Field instruments can report calibration date, diagnostics, and multiple process variables (e.g., a Coriolis flow meter can send density and temperature along with flow). 2. **Remote Configuration:** Engineers can re-range, re-tag, and troubleshoot instruments from the control room using a HART handheld communicator (e.g., Emerson 475), eliminating the need to climb tanks and towers. 3. **Asset Management:** Predictive maintenance becomes possible because the device can self-report health conditions (e.g., "sensor nearing end of life", "blocked impulse line"). --- **Part (b) — I/P Converter Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Given Data:** $I_{in}$ range = 4–20 mA, $P_{out}$ range = 3–15 psi, $I_{in} = 9.5$ mA, $C_{v,max} = 120$, $\Delta P = 25$ psi. **Step 1 — Apply the linear mapping formula for I/P converter (4 mA $\to$ 3 psi, 20 mA $\to$ 15 psi):** $$P_{out} = \left( \dfrac{I_{in} - 4}{16} \right) \cdot (URV - LRV) + LRV$$ $$P_{out} = \left( \dfrac{9.5 - 4}{16} \right) \cdot (15 - 3) + 3$$ $$P_{out} = \left( \dfrac{5.5}{16} \right) \cdot 12 + 3$$ $$P_{out} = 0.34375 \cdot 12 + 3 = 4.125 + 3$$ $$\boxed{P_{out} = 7.125 \text{ psi}}$$ **(i) Answer: $P_{out} = 7.125$ psi** ✓ (3 Marks) **Step 2 — Convert to kPa:** $$P_{out} = 7.125 \cdot 6.895 = 49.13 \text{ kPa}$$ **(ii) Answer: $P_{out} \approx 49.13$ kPa** ✓ (1 Mark) **Step 3 — For a linear valve characteristic, the fraction of travel equals the fraction of input signal:** $$\tau = \dfrac{P_{out} - LRV}{URV - LRV} = \dfrac{7.125 - 3}{15 - 3} = \dfrac{4.125}{12} = 0.34375$$ **Step 4 — Calculate the actual $C_v$ using the linear inherent flow characteristic equation:** $$C_v(\tau) = C_{v,max} \cdot \tau$$ $$C_v = 120 \cdot 0.34375 = 41.25 \text{ gpm}/\sqrt{\text{psi}}$$ **(iii) Answer: $C_v \approx 41.25$ gpm/√psi** ✓ (3 Marks) > **Verification using flow formula:** $Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P} = 41.25 \cdot \sqrt{25} = 41.25 \cdot 5 = 206.25$ gpm. The maximum possible flow (at full opening, 100%) would be $Q_{max} = 120 \cdot \sqrt{25} = 600$ gpm. The ratio $206.25 / 600 = 0.34375 = 34.375\%$ of max flow, which exactly matches the input signal percent. ✓ --- ### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Guide for KTU PBAET604 Module 1 — Analog I/O** * ✅ **Current Loop Standard:** Always **4–20 mA** (live zero). Remember: 4 mA = 0%, 20 mA = 100%, 0 mA = fault. * ✅ **Forward Mapping Formula:** $I = \left( \dfrac{EU - LRV}{URV - LRV} \right) \cdot 16 + 4$ * ✅ **Inverse Mapping Formula:** $EU = \left( \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \right) \cdot (URV - LRV) + LRV$ * ✅ **ADC Resolution:** $Q = \dfrac{FS}{2^{n} - 1}$ — never use $2^{n}$; the "-1" is mandatory for a **unipolar ADC**. * ✅ **Nyquist Theorem:** Sample at least **twice** the highest input frequency to avoid aliasing. * ✅ **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Must be a **low-pass filter** with cutoff below $f_{s}/2$. * ✅ **HART Protocol:** **1200 bps, FSK, $\pm 0.5$ mA** overlay on 4–20 mA. Digital signal does not affect the analog average. * ✅ **Sense Resistor Value:** Standard industrial value is **250 Ω** (gives 1–5 V drop from 4–20 mA). Always check that the ADC's reference voltage is compatible (1–5 V needs $V_{ref} \geq 5$ V). * ✅ **I/P Converter:** Maps 4–20 mA $\to$ 3–15 psi. **P/I Converter** does the reverse (3–15 psi $\to$ 4–20 mA). * ✅ **Hazardous Area:** Use **Intrinsic Safety (IS) barriers** (e.g., Pepperl+Fuchs K-Series) when connecting AI/AO modules to field devices in Zone 1 or Zone 0 areas (oil refineries, chemical plants). * ✅ **Diagnostics:** Always configure **HH (High-High), H (High), L (Low), LL (Low-Low)** alarm limits at the AI block. These protect the process from running away. * ✅ **Redundancy:** Critical loops use **1-out-of-2 or 2-out-of-3 voted redundant AI/AO modules** for SIL-2 or SIL-3 safety integrity levels (per IEC 61508/61511). > **Good luck with your KTU PBAET604 End Semester Examination. Master these six formulas and the live-zero concept, and you are guaranteed to score above 90% in any Analog I/O question.**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAET604 - LOGIC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modular PLC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Modular PLC** (also called a Rack-Mounted or Chassis-Based PLC) is a programmable logic controller in which the major functional subsystems — namely the **power supply, central processing unit (CPU), input modules, output modules, and communication/interface modules** — are constructed as **physically separate, plug-in cards** that are inserted into a common **rack (backplane/chassis)**. Each module performs a dedicated function and can be independently added, removed, replaced, or upgraded without disturbing the other modules in the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Snapshot:** A Modular PLC is a controller whose **CPU, I/O, and Power Supply are housed in separate, interchangeable modules** that share a common backplane. The defining feature is **modularity** — meaning the system grows by *adding cards*, not by replacing the controller.

In strict KTU terminology, a Modular PLC is the **high-end, large-scale variant** of the PLC family, sitting at the top of the hierarchy shown below:

| PLC Type | I/O Capacity | Typical Use | Architecture |
|----------|--------------|-------------|--------------|
| **Fixed / Compact PLC** | < 128 I/O | Small machines, OEM skid control | CPU + I/O in one brick |
| **Modular (Rack-Mounted) PLC** | 128 to > 8192 I/O | Plant-wide process control, DCS interfacing | Discrete cards in a backplane |
| **Soft PLC / PC-based** | Variable | High-speed motion, complex data logging | Software on industrial PC |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Building vs. Studio Apartment" Mental Model

Imagine you are choosing a place to live:

- A **Fixed (Compact) PLC** is like a **studio apartment**. The kitchen, bedroom, and bathroom are all *built into one room*. If you need more space, you must *move out* and find a bigger apartment. You cannot add a new room.

- A **Modular PLC** is like an **office building with removable walls**. The structure (the *rack/backplane*) is fixed, but you can *slide in* a new floor with extra cubicles (an I/O module) whenever your company grows. If a cubicle's wiring shorts, you pull *that single cubicle* out and replace it — without closing the entire office.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** If a student remembers only one sentence about Modular PLC, it should be: *"A Modular PLC is a LEGO® set of automation — you snap in only the cards your plant needs, and you can keep snapping more in later."*

## 1.3 Distinguishing Features of a Modular PLC (KTU High-Yield Bullet List)

- **Physical Separation:** Power supply, CPU, I/O, and communication are **discrete, removable cards**.
- **Backplane / Rack:** All modules plug into a **common chassis** that carries the data bus, power rails, and address lines.
- **Local I/O (in-rack):** Modules seated in the CPU's own rack communicate at the **highest speed** via the backplane.
- **Remote I/O (over fieldbus):** Additional I/O racks can be placed hundreds of metres away and connected via **serial, Ethernet, or proprietary fieldbus links** (e.g., Modbus, Profibus, Ethernet/IP).
- **Hot-Swappable I/O (in premium models):** Defective modules can be replaced while the PLC is *running*, without a full shutdown.
- **High I/O Density:** Modular systems can scale from **a few hundred to several thousand I/O points**, suiting them for **plant-level / DCS** applications.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** Any question that mentions *"more than 256 I/O"*, *"future expansion"*, *"plant-wide control"*, or *"redundancy"* is almost certainly describing a **Modular PLC** scenario.

## 1.4 Typical Specifications (Industry Standard Ranges)

The following metrics are the **benchmarks** examiners expect a student to quote for a "typical" Modular PLC (Allen-Bradley ControlLogix, Siemens S7-400, Schneider Modicon M580, ABB AC500):

- **I/O Capacity:** 128 to **8 192 digital points**, with analogue channels stacked on top.
- **Backplane Bus Speed:** **1 Mbps to 12 Mbps** (and beyond with modern Ethernet backplanes).
- **I/O Addressing:** **Slot-based**, e.g., `I:4.0/5` (input rack 4, slot 0, terminal 5) or `Slot 3, Channel 2`.
- **Program Memory:** **4 MB to 64 MB** RAM with battery-backed or flash-backed retention.
- **Scan Time:** **0.1 ms to 20 ms per 1k of logic** (varies with processor class).
- **Operating Temperature:** **0 °C to 60 °C** (industrial grade).
- **Redundancy:** Premium models support **redundant power supplies** and **redundant CPUs** (hot-standby).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of a Modular PLC — Component-by-Component Breakdown

A Modular PLC is built from a stack of specialised cards. Below is the rigorous, KTU-aligned enumeration of every functional block.

### 2.1.1 The Rack / Backplane / Chassis

The rack is the **mechanical and electrical skeleton** of a Modular PLC. It is a passive (or, in newer designs, an intelligent backplane) printed-circuit motherboard that:

- **Mechanically** holds each module in place via a card-edge connector.
- **Distributes DC power** (+5 V logic, +24 V field-side) from the power supply to every slot.
- **Carries the parallel or serial data bus** between the CPU and each I/O slot.
- **Implements the addressing lines** that tell the CPU *which slot* it is currently reading/writing.

Common rack form factors in KTU-style problems:

- **4-slot rack** — small sub-system, or remote I/O drop.
- **7-slot, 10-slot, 13-slot, 16-slot racks** — main CPU racks.
- **Local extension racks** — connected to the CPU rack via a short parallel cable (typically < 15 m).
- **Remote I/O racks** — connected via fibre or shielded twisted pair, often up to **3 km**.

### 2.1.2 The Power Supply Module (PS)

The PS module is the **first slot on the rack** (in Allen-Bradley PLC-5/SLC-500 conventions) or any dedicated slot. It accepts **120/230 V AC** or **24/48/125 V DC** from the field and produces the regulated voltages required by the logic cards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module-Specific vs. Field-Side Power:** In Modular PLCs, the *logic-side* power is the regulated output of the PS module. The *field-side* power (the 24 V that actually energises a sensor or a contactor coil) is often supplied **separately** by a user-provided DC bus, not by the PS module. This isolation is critical for noise immunity and for *hot-swapping* I/O cards.

Redundant power-supply slots (e.g., two PS modules feeding the same rack in a diode-OR configuration) are common in high-availability systems.

### 2.1.3 The CPU / Processor Module

The CPU is the **brain**. It contains:

- **A microprocessor** (often a 32-bit or 64-bit RISC chip such as ARM Cortex or PowerPC).
- **A co-processor / ASIC** that handles the backplane bus arbitration and I/O image updates.
- **Volatile RAM** (user program + data table) — battery-backed.
- **Non-volatile flash/EEPROM** (for program storage and firmware).
- **Communication ports** (RS-232/485, Ethernet, USB, sometimes CAN).
- **Status LEDs** (RUN, FAULT, FORCE, BATT, CPU active).

In redundant systems, **two identical CPU modules** are installed side-by-side, and the standby CPU tracks the primary via a high-speed **synchronisation link** (a fibre-optic cable). If the primary fails, switchover occurs in **one to four scan cycles** (sub-100 ms).

### 2.1.4 The Input Modules

Input modules convert **field-level electrical signals** into **logic-level signals** the CPU can read. They are the *senses* of the PLC.

Common modular input types:

- **Digital (Discrete) Inputs:** 8, 16, or 32 channels per card. Senses **24 V DC** (sourcing), **120 V AC**, **230 V AC**, or contact-closure.
  - *Typical isolation:* **1500 V RMS** optical isolation between field and logic.
- **Analogue Inputs:** 4, 8, or 16 channels. Accepts **4–20 mA**, **0–10 V**, **±10 V**, **RTD**, **thermocouple** (J, K, T, etc.).
  - *Resolution:* **12-bit, 14-bit, 16-bit** (1 part in 65 536 for 16-bit).
- **Specialty Inputs:** High-speed counter modules (e.g., encoder inputs at 1 MHz), motion modules, weighing modules, etc.

### 2.1.5 The Output Modules

Output modules convert the CPU's **logic-level decisions** into **field-level signals** capable of driving actuators.

- **Digital Outputs:** Relays, transistors (PNP/NPN), or triacs.
  - *Relay outputs:* up to **2 A at 230 V AC** per point — slow, universal.
  - *Transistor outputs:* up to **0.5 A at 24 V DC** — fast, long life, for DC loads.
  - *Triac outputs:* up to **1 A at 230 V AC** — for AC loads, but sensitive to leakage.
- **Analogue Outputs:** 4–20 mA or 0–10 V signals for control valves, variable-speed drives, chart recorders.
- **Intelligent Output Modules:** Stepper-motor, servo, pneumatic valve-manifold interfaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bussed vs. Modular I/O:** In some textbooks, you may see the term *"bussed I/O"* — this refers to a pre-wired, fixed block of I/O that occupies one slot of a modular rack (e.g., an 8-in / 8-out block on a single PCB). It is *cheaper* than 16 individual point-modules, but the I/O count per slot is fixed. Bussed modules are a *halfway house* between fixed and fully modular PLCs.

### 2.1.6 Communication / Interface / Specialty Modules

These do not touch I/O directly but extend the PLC's reach:

- **Serial communication modules** (RS-232, RS-422, RS-485) for Modbus RTU, DF-1, ASCII.
- **Fieldbus modules** (Profibus-DP, Foundation Fieldbus, DeviceNet, CANopen) for connecting remote I/O islands and intelligent field devices.
- **Ethernet/IP modules** for upper-level SCADA, MES, and ERP integration.
- **Peer-to-peer and distributed I/O networking modules.**

## 2.2 The I/O Image Tables and the Scan Cycle — *How a Modular PLC Actually Thinks*

The defining operational concept for any PLC exam is the **Scan Cycle**. In a Modular PLC, the scan is identical in principle to a compact PLC, but the **I/O update phase is now physically distributed** across multiple cards.

The canonical KTU four-step scan cycle is:

1. **Input Scan:** The CPU reads the *Input Image Table* (a region of RAM). The image was last updated by the input modules during the **previous** scan.
2. **Program Scan:** The CPU executes the user logic (Ladder, FBD, ST, SFC) from top to bottom, reading inputs **from the image table** and writing outputs **to a temporary buffer**.
3. **Output Scan:** At the end of the program, the CPU copies the output buffer into the *Output Image Table*, which the output modules then drive onto the field.
4. **Housekeeping:** Communication servicing, force-status updates, battery check, fault logging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Modular-Architecture Twist:** In a Modular PLC, the I/O scan is **distributed**. The CPU does *not* sequentially read slot 1, then slot 2, then slot 3 — instead, the **backplane bus** is arbitrated by the CPU's I/O co-processor, which uses a **block-transfer or cyclic-I/O protocol** to exchange data with each card. From the CPU's perspective, it simply reads a *contiguous block of memory* (the input image); the backplane is responsible for keeping that memory up to date.

## 2.3 I/O Addressing — The KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

Different vendors use different notation, but the underlying **slot-based scheme** is universal.

| Vendor | Address Format | Example | Meaning |
|--------|----------------|---------|---------|
| Allen-Bradley (PLC-5 / SLC-500) | `Type : Rack . Slot / Bit` | `I:013/05` | Input, rack 13, slot 0, terminal 5 |
| Allen-Bradley (Logix) | `Tag-based` | `MyInput.5` | Tag-driven, slot-agnostic |
| Siemens S7-300/400 | `I Byte.Bit` (local) | `I 5.3` | Input byte 5, bit 3 |
| Schneider Modicon | `Slot.Channel` | `40023` | Register-style addressing |

A KTU question often asks: *"How does the CPU locate a particular input?"* — the answer must mention **rack number, slot number, and terminal number**, optionally followed by a **bit number** for digital points.

## 2.4 Advantages and Disadvantages of Modular PLCs

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite 7-Mark Question:** *"Compare modular and fixed PLCs. Justify the choice of a modular PLC for a process plant."*

| Criterion | Modular PLC | Fixed (Compact) PLC |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| Initial Cost | **High** | Low |
| Expandability | **Excellent** — add cards | Limited or none |
| Maintenance | **Easy** — swap one card | Replace whole unit |
| Redundancy | **Supported** (PS, CPU) | Not supported |
| I/O Capacity | **Thousands** | < 128 |
| Hot-Swapping | **Supported** in premium models | No |
| Physical Footprint | Larger, needs cabinet | Small, often DIN-rail |
| Wiring | Per-channel on cards | Pre-wired terminal strip |
| Field Use | **Plant / process / DCS** | OEM machines, sub-systems |

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet

The Modular PLC is more of an *architectural* topic than a calculation-heavy one, but the following expressions still appear in KTU papers (especially for scan-time and I/O-update-rate problems).

| Symbol / Term | Equation / Definition | Units | Remarks |
|---------------|----------------------|-------|---------|
| $T_{scan}$ | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$ | ms | Total scan time |
| $T_{in}$ | $\dfrac{N_{in}}{f_{bus}} \times k_{overhead}$ | ms | Input-update time |
| $T_{prog}$ | $N_{inst} \times t_{inst}$ | ms | Per-instruction cost |
| $T_{house}$ | $T_{comm} + T_{diag} + T_{force}$ | ms | Overhead |
| $I/O_{total}$ | $\sum_{k=1}^{N_{slots}} I/O_k$ | points | Total capacity |
| $R_{adc}$ | $\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}$ | V / LSB | Analogue resolution |
| $t_{pulse}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2 f_{max}}$ | s | High-speed-counter limit |
| $N_{scan/sec}$ | $\dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$ | Hz | Scan rate |

> [!WARNING]
> **Latex Isolation Rule:** All absolute-value or divisibility signs in KTU exam answers must be written as $\vert x \vert$ or $x \mid y$ — never as bare `|x|` — to avoid markdown table breakage in scanned answer sheets.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical refineries:** Modular PLCs form the lower level of a **DCS**, interfacing to SCADA/HMI for the operator to monitor thousands of analogue loops.
- **Automotive body-in-white welding lines:** Modular PLCs synchronize **dozens of robots** through fieldbus remote I/O drops.
- **Power plant boiler control:** Redundant modular PLCs run burner management, FD/ID fan control, and drum-level trip logic with **SIL-2 / SIL-3** safety certification.
- **Water and wastewater treatment:** Modular PLCs manage **SCADA-tied distributed pumping stations**, with remote I/O over fibre.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Signal Flow in a Modular PLC

The following walk-through is the **canonical KTU derivation** style: a 7-slot Modular PLC is to be configured for a small bottling line. We will walk through **physical slotting, addressing, and scan-cycle calculation**.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Designing a 7-Slot Modular PLC Configuration

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A bottling-plant control system requires:
- 24 digital inputs (24 V DC) from limit switches and pushbuttons,
- 8 analogue inputs (4–20 mA) from level and flow transmitters,
- 16 digital outputs (24 V DC) to solenoid valves and conveyors,
- 4 analogue outputs (4–20 mA) to variable-speed drives,
- One Ethernet/IP port for SCADA,
- One Profibus-DP port for a remote I/O island.

Design the **slot-by-slot layout** of a 7-slot Modular PLC rack, assign **addresses** in Allen-Bradley PLC-5 convention, and compute the **total I/O count**.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Choose the modules and count the I/O points

We must minimise the number of slots used while satisfying every requirement.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Digital Inputs (24 V DC)} &= 24 \text{ points} \\
\text{Analogue Inputs (4–20 mA)} &= 8 \text{ points} \\
\text{Digital Outputs (24 V DC)} &= 16 \text{ points} \\
\text{Analogue Outputs (4–20 mA)} &= 4 \text{ points} \\
\text{Communication modules} &= 2 \text{ (Eth/IP + Profibus)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Pack the I/O onto the available card types

For a 7-slot rack, the standard slotting convention (Allen-Bradley PLC-5 / SLC-500 style) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slot 0} &\rightarrow \text{Power Supply (PS)} \\
\text{Slot 1} &\rightarrow \text{CPU (Processor)} \\
\text{Slots 2 to 6} &\rightarrow \text{I/O and Communication modules} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

We have **5 free slots** for 4 I/O card groups and 2 communication modules. We can group I/O as follows:

- **Slot 2 — 32-point DC Input Module** (covers the 24 digital inputs in one card, leaving 8 spare points for future expansion).
- **Slot 3 — 8-channel Analogue Input Module** (exactly matches the 8 analogue inputs).
- **Slot 4 — 16-point DC Output Module** (matches the 16 digital outputs).
- **Slot 5 — 4-channel Analogue Output Module** (matches the 4 analogue outputs).
- **Slot 6 — Communication Interface Module** (combines Ethernet/IP and Profibus-DP on a single card, as many modern interface modules support multiple protocols).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world trade-off:** A 32-point input module has a *higher per-point cost* than a 16-point module, but it saves a slot. In a 7-slot rack this is often the right call. In a 16-slot rack, you might prefer 16-point modules for *granular maintenance* (one card failure affects fewer field devices).

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Assign the addresses

Using the Allen-Bradley PLC-5 convention `I:Rack.Slot/Word/Terminal`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input Image, Rack 0, Slot 2 (32-pt DC input)}: &\quad I:0.2/00 \text{ to } I:0.2/15 \text{ (words 0, 1)} \\
\text{Input Image, Rack 0, Slot 3 (8-ch AI)}: &\quad I:0.3/00 \text{ to } I:0.3/07 \\
\text{Output Image, Rack 0, Slot 4 (16-pt DC output)}: &\quad O:0.4/00 \text{ to } O:0.4/15 \\
\text{Output Image, Rack 0, Slot 5 (4-ch AO)}: &\quad O:0.5/00 \text{ to } O:0.5/03 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Compute the total I/O count

$$
\begin{aligned}
I/O_{total} &= DI + DO + AI + AO \\
&= 24 + 16 + 8 + 4 \\
&= 52 \text{ points} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

With the 32-point input card overspecified, the rack can actually accept up to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I/O_{installed} &= 32 + 8 + 16 + 4 = 60 \text{ points} \\
\text{Spare points available} &= 60 - 52 = 8 \text{ points (8 spare digital inputs)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Justify the choice of a modular over a fixed PLC

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Required I/O} &= 52 \text{ points} \\
\text{Typical fixed-PLC I/O limit} &\approx 128 \text{ points} \\
\text{Modular I/O capacity} &\gg 128 \text{ points} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

A fixed PLC could in principle handle 52 points, so the **modular choice is justified on the basis of**:
- *Future expansion* (8 spare points already wired and addressable, plus spare rack slots for new card types).
- *Maintainability* (a single damaged card can be replaced in minutes without rewiring).
- *Communication flexibility* (the Ethernet/IP and Profibus-DP modules are not available in a fixed PLC at all).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Scan-Time Computation

**Problem Statement:**
A Modular PLC executes 5 000 ladder instructions per scan. The processor is rated at an *average instruction execution time* of **0.8 µs per instruction**. The I/O update phase for 60 points over a backplane rated at **2 M words/second** takes a fixed **0.6 ms** regardless of point count (block-transfer protocol). The housekeeping overhead is **2 ms**. Communication servicing adds **1.5 ms**. Compute the total scan time and the scan rate.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Compute the program-scan time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{prog} &= N_{inst} \times t_{inst} \\
T_{prog} &= 5\,000 \times 0.8 \,\mu s \\
T_{prog} &= 4\,000 \,\mu s \\
T_{prog} &= 4 \,ms \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Identify the input-scan and output-scan contributions

In this problem, the I/O update is *consolidated* into a single 0.6 ms block-transfer. The I/O scan time is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{in} + T_{out} &= 0.6 \,ms \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Identify the housekeeping time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{house} &= T_{comm} + T_{diag} + T_{force} \\
T_{house} &= 1.5 \,ms \quad \text{(given as a single block)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Sum all four scan phases

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{scan} &= T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house} \\
T_{scan} &= 0.6 \,ms + 4.0 \,ms + 0.6 \,ms + 1.5 \,ms \quad \\
&\quad \text{(re-checking: the 0.6 ms is a single block covering both input and output updates)} \\
T_{scan} &= 0.6 + 4.0 + 1.5 \\
T_{scan} &= 6.1 \,ms \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caveat:** Be careful with the wording of the I/O block. In this problem the 0.6 ms is *all I/O* (input + output together), so we add it *once*, not twice. In other problems, the 0.6 ms is *per direction*, in which case we add it twice. Always re-read the sentence.

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Compute the scan rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{scan/sec} &= \frac{1}{T_{scan}} \\
N_{scan/sec} &= \frac{1}{6.1 \times 10^{-3} \,s} \\
N_{scan/sec} &\approx 163.93 \,Hz \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.6 Step 6 — Sanity-check the result

A typical KTU expectation is that a *good* modular PLC achieves **a few milliseconds per 1 000 instructions**. Here we have **4 ms per 5 000 instructions** = **0.8 ms per 1 000 instructions**, which is excellent. The total scan of **6.1 ms** is well within the 10–20 ms range expected of a *mid-range* modular processor.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Analogue-Input Resolution Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A 16-bit analogue input module is used to read a 4–20 mA signal from a level transmitter. Compute the **resolution** in mA per LSB, and the **current value** corresponding to a binary count of **27 500**.

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — State the resolution formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{adc} &= \frac{\Delta_{FS}}{2^{n}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Delta_{FS} = (20 - 4) \,mA = 16 \,mA$ is the full-scale span and $n = 16$ bits.

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Compute the resolution

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{adc} &= \frac{16 \,mA}{2^{16}} \\
R_{adc} &= \frac{16 \,mA}{65\,536} \\
R_{adc} &\approx 2.44 \times 10^{-4} \,mA / LSB \\
R_{adc} &\approx 0.244 \,\mu A / LSB \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Compute the current at the given binary count

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= 4 \,mA + (\text{count} \times R_{adc}) \\
I &= 4 \,mA + (27\,500 \times 2.44 \times 10^{-4} \,mA) \\
I &= 4 \,mA + 6.71 \,mA \\
I &\approx 10.71 \,mA \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.4 Step 4 — Convert to engineering units (level in percent)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ level} &= \frac{I - 4}{16} \times 100 \\
\% \text{ level} &= \frac{10.71 - 4}{16} \times 100 \\
\% \text{ level} &= \frac{6.71}{16} \times 100 \\
\% \text{ level} &\approx 41.94 \,\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Pro Tip:** Always show the units in *every* line of a derivation. Examiners explicitly award partial credit for unit consistency, and a missing unit can cost you 0.5 to 1 mark on a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — Modular PLC Specifications vs. Application Class

| Application Class | Typical I/O | Modular PLC Slot Count | Backplane Speed | Redundancy | Vendor Example |
|-------------------|-------------|-------------------------|-----------------|------------|----------------|
| **OEM machine (small)** | 32–128 | 7–10 | 1–4 M words/s | None | AB MicroLogix 1400, Siemens S7-1200 |
| **Process skid (medium)** | 128–512 | 10–13 | 4–12 M words/s | Optional PS | AB CompactLogix, Siemens S7-1500 |
| **Plant-wide control (large)** | 512–4 096 | 13–16 + remote I/O | 12 M words/s + Eth | PS + CPU redundant | AB ControlLogix, Siemens S7-400, Modicon M580 |
| **DCS front-end (extra large)** | > 4 096 | Distributed | 100 Mbit Eth | Full | Siemens PCS 7 AS, Honeywell C300 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Reference to LDCS:** The PBAET604 course also covers Distributed Control Systems. The Modular PLC is the *building block* of a DCS — multiple Modular PLCs, each governing a process unit, are networked over industrial Ethernet to a supervisory HMI. This is the bridge between the *logic* (PLC) and the *distributed* (DCS) halves of the course title.
