# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*List any six criteria that must be considered while selecting a PLC for an industrial application.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Number of I/O points** — including future expansion (20–25% spare).
2. **Memory size** — adequate for program storage and data tables.
3. **Scan time** — fast enough to meet the response requirements of the process.
4. **Communication protocols** — Modbus, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP for SCADA/DCS integration.
5. **Environmental specifications** — operating temperature, humidity, vibration, IP rating (per **IEC 61131-2**).
6. **Redundancy and reliability** — Hot-Standby Redundancy (HSR) for critical applications; MTBF and MTTR figures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 0.5 marks per correct point. Listing only 4 criteria will fetch only 2/3. Always write **at least six** for full marks.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Differentiate between fixed (compact) PLC and modular PLC. Give one industrial application of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Fixed (Compact) PLC | Modular PLC |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------|
| Construction | CPU, I/O, power supply in single unit | Separate plug-in modules in racks |
| I/O Count | Limited (typically 16–128) | Expandable (up to thousands) |
| Flexibility | Low — cannot change card types | High — mix DI/DO/AI/AO modules |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Typical Use | Small machines, vending, lifts | Refineries, power plants, automotive plants |
| **Example** | Siemens LOGO!, Allen-Bradley MicroLogix 1100 | Siemens S7-1500, Allen-Bradley ControlLogix |

- **Compact PLC Application:** Small packaging machine, automatic door, water pump control.
- **Modular PLC Application:** Oil refinery DCS interface, automotive assembly line, paper mill.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A [14 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat block diagram the **PLC-based control of a three-tank water-level monitoring system** used in a chemical plant. List the inputs and outputs and explain the ladder logic sequence.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Explain the **role of PLC in a SCADA-based distributed control architecture** with a suitable block diagram. Discuss how the PLC interfaces with field devices, HMI, and the plant database.

#### Model Solution — (a) Three-Tank Level Control

**System Description:**
A chemical plant has three storage tanks (Tank 1, Tank 2, Tank 3) that must be monitored and controlled automatically. The PLC manages:
- Automatic filling when level falls below 25%.
- Automatic emptying when level rises above 90%.
- High-high alarm at 95%.
- Low-low alarm at 10%.

**Inputs:**
- 3 × Float level switches (high, low) per tank → **6 Digital Inputs**
- 1 × Manual/auto selector switch
- 1 × Emergency stop pushbutton
- Total: **8 DI**

**Outputs:**
- 3 × Inlet solenoid valves (one per tank)
- 3 × Outlet motorized valves (one per tank)
- 1 × Common alarm siren
- Total: **7 DO**

**Working Logic:**

$$
\text{If } L < 25\% \text{ for Tank i: energize Inlet Valve } V_{in,i}
$$

$$
\text{If } L > 90\% \text{ for Tank i: energize Outlet Valve } V_{out,i}
$$

$$
\text{If } L > 95\% \text{ OR } L < 10\% \text{: activate ALARM}
$$

```
    |       START       INLET1      OUTLET1     |
    |       |           |            |           |
    |       |           L1_LOW   L1_HIGH         |
    |       |           |        |               |
    |       -----] [------+] [------+            |
    |                                            |
    |       ALARM                               |
    |       |    L1_HH   OR  L1_LL               |
    |       |    |       |   |                   |
    |       -----] [---+] [------+] [------------|
```

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Tank 1 Float Switches]
    T2[Tank 2 Float Switches]
    T3[Tank 3 Float Switches]
    PB[Manual Pushbuttons]

    T1 --> PLC[PLC CPU]
    T2 --> PLC
    T3 --> PLC
    PB --> PLC

    PLC --> V1[Inlet Valve 1]
    PLC --> V2[Inlet Valve 2]
    PLC --> V3[Inlet Valve 3]
    PLC --> V4[Outlet Valve 1]
    PLC --> V5[Outlet Valve 2]
    PLC --> V6[Outlet Valve 3]
    PLC --> ALM[Siren and Beacon]
```

**Marking Breakdown (7 Marks):**
- Block Diagram (2 marks)
- I/O list (2 marks)
- Working logic with ladder snippet (3 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — (b) PLC in SCADA/DCS Architecture

**Layered Hierarchy (ISA-95 / Purdue Model):**

| Level | Function | PLC Role |
|-------|----------|----------|
| Level 0 — Field | Sensors, actuators | **Interface** via I/O modules |
| Level 1 — Control | Local control loops | **Core execution** of ladder/FBD logic |
| Level 2 — Supervisory | HMI, SCADA alarms | **Data exchange** via Ethernet |
| Level 3 — Operations | MES, production tracking | Reports throughput, downtime |
| Level 4 — Enterprise | ERP, business planning | Receives finished production data |

**Communication Flow:**
1. Field sensors (4–20 mA, 24 V DC) → AI/DI module of PLC.
2. PLC executes logic in real-time (scan cycle).
3. PLC publishes process variables to **SCADA server** via **OPC-UA / Modbus TCP** on Industrial Ethernet.
4. HMI subscribes to SCADA tags; operator can override setpoints.
5. SCADA historian stores long-term data accessible to MES.

**Role Summary:**
- The PLC is the **real-time workhorse** at Level 1.
- SCADA provides **supervisory visibility** at Level 2.
- PLC cannot replace SCADA, but SCADA cannot operate without a controller (PLC/DCS) below it.

**Marking Breakdown (7 Marks):**
- Purdue/ISA-95 layered diagram (3 marks)
- Communication flow and protocols (2 marks)
- PLC vs SCADA responsibility distinction (2 marks)

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **PLC-based control of an elevator system** for a 5-floor building. List all inputs and outputs and describe the safety interlocks.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **selection of PLCs for hazardous (explosive) environments** with reference to ATEX, IECEx, and IP ratings.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes in Module 4 Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing SCADA with PLC.** SCADA is a *software system* for visualization; PLC is a *hardware controller*. Students often swap their functions — **lose 2 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Missing the I/O list.** Any application question without a properly tabulated I/O list attracts 1–2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting scan-time justification.** When asked to "select a PLC," never just name a model. Always justify with **scan time, I/O count, communication, and redundancy**.
> 4. **Ignoring safety standards.** Elevator, hazardous area, and medical applications require **specific safety certifications**. Writing only generic points loses marks.

---

## 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Industrial Applications of PLC | Quick Revision Checklist**

- A PLC is a **solid-state, industrially-hardened digital computer** for real-time sequential control, conforming to **NEMA** and **IEC 61131-3** standards.
- The standard PLC scan cycle is: **Read Inputs → Execute Program → Update Outputs → Housekeeping**.
- Industrial applications fall into **four paradigms**: discrete, process, motion, and safety control.
- **Selection criteria** always include: I/O count (with 20–25% margin), scan time, memory, communication, environment, redundancy.
- The **ISA-95/Purdue model** has 5 levels; PLC operates at **Level 1 (Basic Control)**, SCADA at **Level 2 (Supervisory)**.
- Major industrial sectors: **automotive, bottling, water/wastewater, oil & gas, power, elevators, traffic, packaging, building automation**.
- Communication protocols in modern PLC systems: **Modbus, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, DeviceNet, OPC-UA**.
- Hazardous area PLCs must comply with **ATEX (Europe) / IECEx (global)** standards with **IP65/IP66** enclosures.
- Key formulas to memorize:
    - $I_{design} = 1.25 \times I_{total}$
    - $T_{scan} \le T_{cycle} / 5$
    - $\eta = T_{scan}/T_{cycle} \times 100\%$
    - $A = MTBF/(MTBF + MTTR)$
- In exam answers, **always include**: (i) I/O list, (ii) Block diagram, (iii) Ladder/FBD logic snippet, (iv) Justification of PLC selection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SCADA, DCS and Open Systems for Industrial Plants - Thermal Power Plant and Steel Plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAET604 - LOGIC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SCADA, DCS and Open Systems for Industrial Plants - Thermal Power Plant and Steel Plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 SCADA — Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **SCADA** is a centralized industrial control system that uses computers, networked data communications, and graphical Human-Machine Interfaces (HMIs) to monitor, gather, and process real-time data from geographically dispersed Remote Terminal Units (RTUs) or Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs), enabling supervisory control over industrial processes, infrastructure, and facility-based operations.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Air Traffic Control Tower

Imagine an **air traffic control (ATC) tower** at a busy international airport. The controller cannot physically move each aircraft on the runway. Instead, the controller:

- **Watches radar screens** (HMIs) showing the live position of every aircraft in their sector.
- **Receives telemetry** (pressure, pressure-altitude, heading) from the aircraft's onboard systems (analogous to **field instruments**).
- **Sends high-level commands** ("climb to 30,000 ft", "turn left 20°") that the aircraft's **autopilot** (RTU/PLC) executes locally.
- **Logs every event** for post-flight analysis and regulatory compliance (historical data archiving).

The ATC tower is **NOT** flying the plane. It is **supervising**. SCADA works exactly the same way. The central SCADA server tells a substation breaker to "OPEN" — the substation's local PLC physically actuates the breaker coil. SCADA is **event-driven**, **data-centric**, and **geographically distributed** (hence "Supervisory Control").

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** SCADA is typically used for **geographically spread** assets (pipelines, power transmission, water distribution), whereas DCS is used for **process-intensive, plant-floors** like refineries and power stations.

### SCADA Hardware Taxonomy (The Four Pillars)

| Tier | Component | Function |
|------|-----------|----------|
| Field | **Sensors & Actuators** | Convert physical variables (T, P, Flow) to electrical signals (4-20 mA / 0-10 V) |
| Control | **RTUs / PLCs** | Local automation, logic execution, telemetry uplink |
| Communication | **Communication Infrastructure** | Radio, fiber, GSM, leased lines, Ethernet |
| Supervisory | **SCADA Master Station / MTU** | HMI, historians, alarms, control commands |

The **MTU (Master Terminal Unit)** is the brain. RTUs are the remote hands. The standard RTU scan time is **2 seconds to 10 seconds** (slower than DCS loop time of **100-500 ms**).

---

## 1.2 DCS — Distributed Control System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Distributed Control System (DCS)** is a computerized control system for a process or plant, wherein autonomous controllers are distributed throughout the system, but all operations, including control, data acquisition, display, and communications, are supervised and coordinated through a central operator interface. DCS uses a proprietary, highly reliable, redundant, and deterministic communication bus (e.g., FOUNDATION Fieldbus, Profibus) for high-speed loop control.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital ICU Central Station

Consider a **Hospital Intensive Care Unit (ICU)**:

- Each **patient bedside monitor** (DCS **Controller**) continuously measures the patient's ECG, BP, SpO₂ (analogous to **process loops** — temperature, pressure, flow).
- These bedside monitors act **autonomously**: if BP drops, the monitor triggers the infusion pump **without waiting** for the central station.
- However, **all patient data** is also streamed to the **Central Nursing Station** (DCS **Operator Station / Engineering Station**) where a senior doctor oversees trends, adjusts alarm limits, and writes prescriptions (setpoints).
- If a bedside monitor fails, another **redundant monitor** takes over (1:1 redundancy).
- The system uses a **dedicated medical-grade network** (DCS Control Network) — no internet, no Wi-Fi interference, deterministic.

A DCS is the **ICU of the process industry**. The control is **distributed** for safety and speed, but **supervision is centralized** for unified operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** DCS is characterized by:
> - **Loop integrity** (each control loop is closed at the controller level).
> - **Determinism** (guaranteed message delivery within bounded time).
> - **Redundancy** (1:1 hot standby for controllers and network).
> - **Continuous process orientation** (refineries, petrochemicals, power plants).

---

## 1.3 Open Systems for Industrial Plants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> An **Open System** in industrial automation refers to a control architecture built upon **non-proprietary, standards-based, interoperable** technologies that allow equipment from multiple vendors to exchange data seamlessly using well-documented protocols (e.g., OPC UA, FOUNDATION Fieldbus, Profinet, Modbus TCP, HART, IEC 61850).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Universal Power Socket (Type G Universal Adapter)

Imagine the old days where every country had a unique electrical socket. An Indian appliance (BS 546) could not plug into a German socket (Schuko Type F). Then came the **Universal Travel Adapter** — a single physical interface that accepts multiple plug standards and **translates** them to the local grid.

**OPC (OLE for Process Control)** and modern open protocols are exactly that — a **"Universal Adapter"** for industrial data:

- A **Siemens PLC** (speaking Profinet) needs to send data to an **Emerson DCS** (speaking FOUNDATION Fieldbus) and to an **ABB HMI** (speaking Modbus).
- Without open systems, this requires **expensive custom gateways** and protocol converters.
- With **OPC UA (Unified Architecture)**, all three devices publish their data to a common **OPC UA Server** using a standardized information model (address space), and the consumer reads it in a **vendor-neutral, platform-independent** manner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Open Standards in the KTU 2024 Syllabus:**
> - **OPC Classic / OPC UA** — Data exchange between control systems and enterprise/SCADA
> - **FOUNDATION Fieldbus & Profibus PA** — Field-level digital communication
> - **Profinet & EtherNet/IP** — Real-time Ethernet at control level
> - **HART** — Hybrid (analog + digital) smart instrumentation
> - **IEC 61850** — Substation automation (relevant for power plant switchyards)
> - **ISA-95 / IEC 62264** — Enterprise-Control System Integration (B2B/MES to Level 3)

---

## 1.4 Industry-Specific Plant Contexts (Thermal Power Plant & Steel Plant)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires a comparison of how SCADA, DCS, and open systems are deployed in two contrasting industries: **Thermal Power Plant (process-continuous, energy)** and **Steel Plant (process-batch + continuous, metallurgical)**.

### 1.4.1 Thermal Power Plant — Snapshot

A typical **500 MW subcritical thermal power plant** has these functional zones:

- **Coal Handling Plant (CHP)** — Wagon tipplers, conveyors, stackers, crushers
- **Coal Milling & Boiler** — Pulverizers, furnace, superheaters, reheaters, economizer, air-preheater
- **Steam Turbine & Generator** — HP/IP/LP turbines, generator, excitation, governor
- **Condensate & Feedwater** — Condenser, BFP, deaerator, HP heaters
- **Switchyard** — Generator transformer, bus bars, transmission lines
- **Ash Handling & Water Treatment** — ESP, ash slurry, DM plant, cooling towers

### 1.4.2 Steel Plant — Snapshot

An **integrated steel plant (ISP)** based on the **BF-BOF route** has:

- **Coke Oven & By-Products** — Coke batteries, gas cleaning
- **Sinter Plant** — Iron ore agglomeration
- **Blast Furnace (BF)** — Iron making (shaft furnace, 2000°C tuyère zone)
- **Steel Melting Shop (BOF/LD Converter)** — Primary steel making
- **Secondary Metallurgy (LF, RH, VD)** — Ladle refining, degassing
- **Continuous Casting** — Billets, blooms, slabs
- **Hot Strip Mill / Cold Rolling Mill** — Finishing

> [!TIP]
> **Pedagogical Hook:** A thermal power plant has **~2000-3000 I/O points** with very tight thermal loops. A modern integrated steel plant has **~30,000-50,000 I/O points** across vastly different process regimes. Both are **mission-critical 24×7** operations — this is why DCS dominates, with SCADA on the periphery (e.g., switchyard, CHP conveyors, stockyards).

---

## 1.5 Why These Three Concepts Are Co-Taught (The Big Picture)

The KTU Module 4 stitches three threads:

1. **SCADA** = Wide-area supervision (kilometers of plant).
2. **DCS** = Tight, plant-floor, continuous control (millisecond loops).
3. **Open Systems** = The "glue" that lets them talk to each other AND to the **ERP/MES** layer above.

In a modern smart factory, the hierarchy is:

$$\text{Level 0 (Field)} \rightarrow \text{Level 1 (Control)} \rightarrow \text{Level 2 (Supervisory)} \rightarrow \text{Level 3 (MES)} \rightarrow \text{Level 4 (ERP)}$$

This is the **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model** — the **gold standard** of industrial automation architecture.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SCADA — Architecture & Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 The Four-Layer SCADA Architecture

SCADA systems are best understood in **four logical layers**:

**Layer 1 — Field Instrumentation Layer**
- **Sensors:** RTD/TC for temperature, orifice/DPT for flow, strain gauge for pressure.
- **Actuators:** Motor starters, solenoid valves, VFD-driven pumps.
- **Signal Standardization:** Industrial signals are **4-20 mA current loops** (preferred over 0-10 V because current loops are **immune to line voltage drop** over long distances).
- **Smart instruments** additionally overlay **HART (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer)** digital data on the 4-20 mA signal.

**Layer 2 — Remote Terminal Unit (RTU) / PLC Layer**
- RTUs are ruggedized for outdoor, substation-grade deployment.
- They perform **local logic** (e.g., auto-reclosing of breakers).
- They **store-and-forward** telemetry to the master.
- Modern RTUs also support **IEC 61850** for substation automation.

**Layer 3 — Communication Network**
- Wired: **Leased lines, OFC, power-line carrier (PLC)**.
- Wireless: **VHF/UHF radio, GSM/GPRS, satellite, 4G/5G private networks**.
- Protocols: **DNP3, IEC 60870-5-101/104, Modbus RTU/TCP**.

**Layer 4 — Master Station / Control Center**
- HMI workstations (SCADA Server + Operator Console).
- **Historian database** for time-series archival.
- Alarm management, trending, report generation.
- **Redundancy**: Hot-standby server pairs for high availability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Event-Driven vs. Polled Reporting:**
> - **Polled (Report-by-Exception disabled):** Master queries each RTU cyclically. High bandwidth consumption.
> - **Report-by-Exception (RBE):** RTU transmits ONLY when a value crosses a dead-band. Reduces bandwidth by **~80%** in steady-state operation. Standard in modern DNP3 / IEC 60870-5-104.

---

### 2.1.2 SCADA Communication Protocols (Most Examined in KTU)

| Protocol | Origin | Domain | Key Feature |
|----------|--------|--------|-------------|
| **Modbus RTU/TCP** | Modicon (1979) | Generic industrial | Master-slave, 255 slaves, RS-485 / TCP |
| **DNP3** | IEEE 1815 | SCADA / Substation | Report-by-exception, time-stamping, file transfer |
| **IEC 60870-5-101** | IEC | European SCADA | Balanced/asymmetric, supervisory |
| **IEC 60870-5-104** | IEC | SCADA over TCP/IP | Networked version of 101 |
| **IEC 61850** | IEC | Substation automation | Object-oriented data model, GOOSE messaging |
| **OPC UA** | OPC Foundation | Open architecture | Platform-independent, secure, information modelling |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **DNP3** with **Modbus**. Key distinction — DNP3 supports **time-stamped events, file transfer, and report-by-exception**, while Modbus is a **flat register map** with no native time-stamping.

---

## 2.2 DCS — Architecture & Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 The Three-Level DCS Architecture (Honeywell, Yokogawa, ABB model)

A DCS is built as **three hierarchical levels**:

**Level 0 — Field Devices**
- 4-20 mA / HART / Fieldbus sensors and actuators.
- **Smart valves** with digital valve controllers (e.g., Fisher FIELDVUE).

**Level 1 — Continuous Control / Direct Control Level**
- **Distributed Process Controllers (DPCs)** — e.g., Honeywell C300, Yokogawa CENTUM VP, ABB 800xA AC 800M.
- They execute **PID, cascade, ratio, split-range, override, batch** control strategies.
- **Execution cycle (loop scan time): 50 ms - 500 ms** depending on loop criticality.
- **Redundancy:** 1:1 hot-standby (primary runs, secondary is in sync via "shadow memory").

**Level 2 — Supervisory / Operations Level**
- **Operator Stations** (HMIs) with plant mimics, trends, alarm summaries.
- **Engineering Stations** for configuration, downloading control strategies.
- **Historian** for compliance reporting and process analytics.
- **Asset Management** (e.g., Emerson AMS) for instrument calibration.

**Level 3 (and above) — Manufacturing Execution System (MES) / Enterprise**
- Production scheduling, OEE, lot tracking, recipe management.
- Connected via **OPC UA, ISA-95, or proprietary bridges**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Foundation:**
> A DCS is **not** a single computer. It is a **networked federation** of autonomous controllers that share a common database, common alarm philosophy, and common engineering environment. The "D" in DCS stands for "Distributed" — meaning **control function** is distributed, NOT the hardware alone.

---

### 2.2.2 Key DCS Control Strategies (Exam-Relevant)

| Strategy | Purpose | Typical Use |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| **Single-loop PID** | Track a setpoint with one PV | Tank level, drum pressure |
| **Cascade Control** | Inner loop faster than outer | Reactor T controlled by jacket T |
| **Ratio Control** | Maintain A:B proportion | Air-Fuel ratio in boiler |
| **Split-Range** | One output drives 2+ actuators | Heating + cooling valve |
| **Override / Auctioneering** | Select max/min of multiple controllers | Compressor surge protection |
| **Feedforward + Feedback** | Anticipate + correct | Disturbance rejection in furnace |
| **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** | Multi-variable constrained optimization | Distillation column, power plant deaerator |

The **PID control law** is the most fundamental block in any DCS:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where the three terms are **Proportional**, **Integral**, **Derivative** actions. The **KTU exam** almost always asks the student to identify which term is responsible for **offset elimination** (the Integral term, because it integrates the steady-state error until it is forced to zero).

---

### 2.2.3 DCS Redundancy & Determinism (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DCS is "high availability":**
> - **Controller Redundancy:** 1:1 hot standby. Switchover time < 1 second, bumpless transfer via signal tracking.
> - **I/O Redundancy:** Critical analog inputs come in pairs. Voting logic (1-out-of-2 or 2-out-of-3) rejects faulty signals.
> - **Network Redundancy:** Dual-redundant COTS Ethernet (or proprietary token-passing bus) with **< 100 ms switchover**.
> - **Power Redundancy:** Dual feeds from independent UPS and battery banks (typically 30 min autonomy).

**Determinism** is the ability to guarantee that a message is delivered within a **bounded time**. DCS networks (FOUNDATION Fieldbus, Profibus) guarantee **deterministic** behavior, while standard Ethernet is **non-deterministic** by default. This is why **Profinet IRT (Isochronous Real-Time)** and **EtherCAT** were invented — to bring Ethernet's bandwidth with deterministic timing.

---

## 2.3 Open Systems — Why "Open" Matters

### 2.3.1 The Problem of Proprietary Systems

In the 1980s, every DCS vendor used a **closed protocol**. Connecting a **Honeywell TDC 3000** to a **Fisher PROVOX** required an expensive custom gateway. The user was **locked-in** ("vendor lock-in"). Maintenance, training, and spare parts all flowed through the vendor.

### 2.3.2 The Open Systems Revolution

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** model (ISO/IEC 7498-1) was adapted to industrial networks. The key milestones:

- **1990s:** **Fieldbus Foundation (FF)** and **Profibus** standardized field-level communication.
- **1996:** **OPC (OLE for Process Control)** Classic DA / AE / HDA standardized data exchange on Windows.
- **2008:** **OPC UA (Unified Architecture)** — platform-independent, secure, scalable, and information-modelling-aware.
- **2010s:** **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — MQTT, REST APIs, and cloud connectors.
- **2020s:** **OPC UA over TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking)** — bringing real-time control over standard Ethernet.

### 2.3.3 The ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model

The **Purdue Model** is the **canonical reference architecture** for industrial cybersecurity and control hierarchy:

$$\text{Levels 0-2: OT (Operations Technology)} \quad \vert \quad \text{Levels 3-5: IT (Information Technology)}$$

The boundary at **Level 3.5 (DMZ)** is the **Industrial Demilitarized Zone** — a buffer zone for industrial cybersecurity that separates enterprise IT from plant-floor OT. KTU examiners love to ask about this **Purdue model** and the role of firewalls at Level 3.5.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for KTU 2024 University Exam.**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---------|----------------------|--------------|
| 4-20 mA current loop | $I = 4\,\text{mA} + 16\,\text{mA} \cdot \dfrac{V - V_{LRV}}{V_{URV} - V_{LRV}}$ | mA; $V_{LRV}$, $V_{URV}$ are range limits |
| HART signal | $\omega_{HART} = 1200\,\text{Hz}$ (1) and $2200\,\text{Hz}$ (0) | FSK on top of 4-20 mA |
| PID controller | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | $K_p$ dimensionless, $K_i$ in s$^{-1}$, $K_d$ in s |
| Loop scan time | $T_{scan} \le 0.1 \cdot T_{process}$ | Rule of thumb: scan $\le$ 1/10 of process time constant |
| Network latency budget | $T_{lat} = \sum_{i} T_{transmit,i} + T_{prop,i} + T_{queue,i}$ | Must be $\le T_{deadline}$ for determinism |
| RTU scan time | $T_{RTU} = 2 - 10$ seconds | SCADA standard |
| DCS control loop time | $T_{loop} = 50 - 500$ ms | DCS standard |
| Cascade inner/outer time | $T_{inner} \le \dfrac{1}{3} T_{outer}$ | Tuning rule |
| Master-to-RTU poll | $N_{RTUs} \cdot T_{poll} \le T_{deadline}$ | Bandwidth constraint |
| OPC UA Information Model | Tree of nodes defined in **AddressSpace** | Objects, Variables, Methods, References |
| Purdue Model Level 3.5 | Industrial DMZ (Firewall) | OT/IT boundary |

**Important:** Avoid using the vertical bar `|` symbol directly inside markdown table cells — KTU PDF rendering breaks. Always use `\vert` for math-mode absolute values and pipe-like notation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | SCADA Use | DCS Use | Open Systems |
|--------|-----------|---------|--------------|
| **Thermal Power** | Switchyard, CHP conveyors, ash dyke | Boiler-Turbine-Generator (B-T-G) coordinated control | IEC 61850 for switchyard, OPC UA to plant ERP |
| **Steel Plant** | Stockyard, slag handling, dispatch | Blast furnace stove control, BOF, caster | Profinet, OPC UA, ISA-95 for MES/ERP |
| **Oil & Gas (Pipeline)** | Primary (SCADA) | Compressor stations (DCS) | DNP3, OPC UA |
| **Water Distribution** | Primary (SCADA) | Treatment plant (DCS) | Modbus, OPC UA |
| **Refinery** | Tank farm, terminals | CDU/VDU (DCS) | ISA-95, OPC UA, IEC 62443 cyber |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** A common 14-mark question is *"Compare SCADA and DCS along 8 parameters."* Memorize the comparison axes:
> 1. Geographic scope (SCADA = wide, DCS = plant)
> 2. Loop scan time (SCADA = seconds, DCS = ms)
> 3. Control philosophy (SCADA = supervisory, DCS = regulatory)
> 4. Architecture (SCADA = central + remote, DCS = distributed)
> 5. Data volume (SCADA = events, DCS = continuous)
> 6. Redundancy (DCS = extensive 1:1, SCADA = server pair)
> 7. Vendor lock-in (DCS traditionally proprietary, SCADA more open)
> 8. Typical application (SCADA = utility, DCS = process plant)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Why derivations matter for SCADA/DCS:** Unlike purely physics topics, the "derivations" here are **architectural justification derivations** — proving WHY a certain scan time, control law, or protocol parameter is chosen, and walking through the trade-off equations. The KTU 2024 examiner rewards step-by-step logical reasoning, not memorization.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Justification of RTU Scan Time and Bandwidth

**Problem (KTU Exam Style, 7 marks):**
A SCADA system polls 50 RTUs. Each RTU contains 200 measured points. Each point is reported as a 16-bit integer with a 16-bit time-stamp and 8-bit status byte. The communication channel is a 9600 bps leased line using 1 start bit, 8 data bits, 1 parity, 1 stop bit (10 bits per byte). Find the **minimum time** to complete a full scan of the plant.

### Step 1 — Compute total bits per RTU

Each point requires: $2 \text{ bytes (value)} + 2 \text{ bytes (timestamp)} + 1 \text{ byte (status)} = 5 \text{ bytes}$

For 200 points: $200 \times 5 = 1000 \text{ bytes per RTU}$

### Step 2 — Convert to total bits on the wire

With 10 bits per byte (start + 8 data + parity + stop): $1000 \times 10 = 10{,}000 \text{ bits per RTU}$

### Step 3 — Add framing and protocol overhead

DNP3 / Modbus framing adds approximately 25 bytes of overhead (header, CRC, addressing): $25 \times 10 = 250 \text{ bits}$

Total bits per RTU: $10{,}000 + 250 = 10{,}250 \text{ bits}$

### Step 4 — Compute time per RTU

$$T_{RTU} = \frac{\text{bits per RTU}}{\text{bit rate}} = \frac{10{,}250 \text{ bits}}{9600 \text{ bps}} \approx 1.068 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 5 — Total scan time for 50 RTUs

$$T_{total} = N_{RTU} \cdot T_{RTU} = 50 \cdot 1.068 = 53.4 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 6 — Check against SCADA deadline

A typical SCADA scan deadline is **10 seconds** for critical data. Our system takes **53.4 seconds**, exceeding the deadline by a factor of **5.34**. The bandwidth is **undersized**.

### Step 7 — Engineered solutions

- **Upgrade to 56,600 bps:** $T_{total} = 53.4 \times 9600 / 56600 \approx 9.06 \text{ s}$ — borderline.
- **Upgrade to 64,000 bps:** $T_{total} \approx 8.0 \text{ s}$ — meets deadline.
- **Use Report-by-Exception (RBE):** If only 5% of points change between scans, report only those 10 points per RTU: $T_{RTU,RBE} = (10 \times 10) + 250 = 350 \text{ bits} \approx 0.0364 \text{ s}$, and $T_{total} = 1.82 \text{ s}$ — well within deadline.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Answer Framework:** Always end such derivations with a **practical recommendation** (RBE, higher bandwidth, or compression) — this is what earns the "engineering judgment" marks in the 14-mark question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PID Controller Output and Anti-Windup

**Problem (KTU Exam Style, 7 marks):**
A steam drum level controller has $K_p = 2.5$, $K_i = 0.5\,\text{s}^{-1}$, $K_d = 0.1\,\text{s}$. The setpoint is $r = 50\%$, the current measurement is $y = 42\%$. The error has been persisting for $t = 8$ seconds. The error rate of change is $\dfrac{de}{dt} = 0.2\,\%/\text{s}$. Compute the controller output $u(t)$ assuming the controller is in **direct-acting** mode and the output is normalized to 0-100%.

### Step 1 — Compute the instantaneous error

$$e(t) = r - y = 50 - 42 = 8\%$$

### Step 2 — Proportional term

$$u_P = K_p \cdot e = 2.5 \times 8 = 20\%$$

### Step 3 — Integral term (assuming the error is roughly constant over 8 s)

$$u_I = K_i \int_0^{8} e(\tau) \, d\tau = 0.5 \times 8 \times 8 = 32\%$$

(Note: integral is area under the error-time curve, which is a triangle/rectangle depending on the error profile. For a constant error over 8 s, the area is $8 \times 8 = 64\%\cdot\text{s}$.)

### Step 4 — Derivative term

$$u_D = K_d \cdot \frac{de}{dt} = 0.1 \times 0.2 = 0.02\%$$

### Step 5 — Total controller output

$$u(t) = u_P + u_I + u_D = 20 + 32 + 0.02 = 52.02\%$$

### Step 6 — Anti-windup check

If the actuator saturates at 100% (valve fully open), the integral term would continue to grow ("integrator windup") until the error reverses. This causes a large **overshoot** when the disturbance clears. The DCS applies **anti-windup**: when the output is saturated, integration is **frozen** (or **back-calculated**).

Anti-windup (back-calculation) rule:

$$\frac{d I}{dt} = K_i \cdot e + \frac{1}{T_t}(u_{sat} - u)$$

where $T_t$ is the tracking time constant (typically $\sqrt{T_i \cdot T_d}$ or $T_t = 0.1 T_i$).

With $T_i = 1/K_i = 2$ s, $T_t = 0.2$ s. If $u_{sat} = 100\%$ and $u = 52.02\%$, the back-calculation term is $(100 - 52.02) / 0.2 = 239.9\,\%/s$ which would rapidly **bleed off** the integrator to keep $u$ within bounds. This is automatic in every modern DCS (e.g., Honeywell C300, Yokogawa CENTUM VP).

### Step 7 — Final answer (clamped)

$$u_{final} = \min(u, u_{max}) = \min(52.02, 100) = 52.02\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's View:** The student is expected to (a) state the three-term PID equation, (b) plug in numerical values with correct units, (c) discuss anti-windup. A full-mark answer must explicitly mention **derivative filter** (since pure derivative is noisy) — add a first-order lag $u_D = K_d \cdot s / (1 + T_f \cdot s)$ with $T_f \approx 0.1 T_d$ in real implementations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cascade Control Tuning (Justification of Inner/Outer Loop Time)

**Problem (KTU Exam Style, 7 marks):**
A cascade control loop has an outer (master) loop with process time constant $T_{outer} = 60$ s and an inner (slave) loop with time constant $T_{inner} = 10$ s. Justify the **selection of inner loop scan time** such that the inner loop settles at least 3-5 times faster than the outer.

### Step 1 — State the cascade rule of thumb

The inner loop must be at least **3 to 5 times faster** than the outer loop:

$$T_{inner,closed} \le \frac{T_{outer,open}}{3} \quad \text{to} \quad \frac{T_{outer,open}}{5}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the inner loop bandwidth

Closed-loop time constant of a well-tuned loop is roughly $T_{cl} \approx T_{open} / 3$.

So the inner loop's closed-loop time constant should be:

$$T_{inner,cl} \le \frac{T_{outer,open}}{3 \times 3} = \frac{60}{9} \approx 6.67 \text{ s}$$

Or using the 5× rule:

$$T_{inner,cl} \le \frac{60}{15} = 4 \text{ s}$$

### Step 3 — Inner loop scan time

A practical rule is that the controller scan time should be $\le T_{process}/10$:

$$T_{scan,inner} \le \frac{T_{inner,open}}{10} = \frac{10}{10} = 1 \text{ s}$$

### Step 4 — Justification in words

The inner loop (e.g., fuel flow) responds quickly to disturbances (e.g., fuel pressure fluctuation). The outer loop (e.g., furnace temperature) is slow. If the inner loop were as slow as the outer, the cascade would be useless — the inner loop would lag the outer and **fight** the master's commands. By making the inner loop 3-5× faster, the inner loop appears "instantaneous" to the outer loop, and the outer loop only sees a well-controlled secondary variable.

### Step 5 — Quantitative check

If the outer loop's PI controller has $K_p^{outer} = 0.8$, $T_i^{outer} = 30$ s, the recommended outer loop scan time is $T_{scan,outer} \le 60/10 = 6$ s. So the cascade is realized as:
- **Inner loop:** $T_{scan} = 1$ s, $K_p = 1.5$, $T_i = 5$ s
- **Outer loop:** $T_{scan} = 6$ s, $K_p = 0.8$, $T_i = 30$ s

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The inner loop's setpoint is the output of the outer loop's PID — so the inner loop must be able to **track** a moving setpoint without lag. If the inner loop is too slow, the outer loop's "anticipatory" command is delayed, and the cascade acts like a sluggish single loop.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Report-by-Exception Bandwidth Savings

**Problem (KTU Exam Style, 7 marks):**
A SCADA system polls 100 RTUs, each with 500 points. Polled mode: every RTU is queried cyclically. RBE mode: only points that change by more than a configured dead-band (0.5% of span) are reported. In a typical **steady-state** operation, only 3% of points change per scan cycle. Compute the **bandwidth reduction** achieved by RBE.

### Step 1 — Total points in the system

$$N_{points} = 100 \times 500 = 50{,}000 \text{ points}$$

### Step 2 — Bits per point (assuming 16-bit value + 16-bit timestamp + 8-bit status = 40 bits per point)

In **polled mode**, the master reads every point every cycle:

$$\text{Polled bits per cycle} = 50{,}000 \times 40 = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ bits} = 2 \text{ Mbits}$$

### Step 3 — In RBE mode, only 3% of points are reported

$$\text{RBE bits per cycle} = 0.03 \times 2{,}000{,}000 = 60{,}000 \text{ bits} = 60 \text{ kbits}$$

### Step 4 — Compute bandwidth reduction

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000 - 60{,}000}{2{,}000{,}000} \times 100\% = 97\%$$

### Step 5 — Engineered commentary

RBE achieves a **97% bandwidth reduction** in steady state. However:
- **Burst handling:** During plant upsets, the 3% can rise to 30-50%, and the channel must handle this burst.
- **Heartbeat messages:** Even in RBE, RTUs send a "still alive" heartbeat (typically every 5-15 min) so the master knows the RTU is healthy.
- **Dead-band selection:** Too tight → excessive reports. Too loose → loss of visibility. A typical dead-band is 0.25% to 1% of span for analog points.

---

The **switchyard** is on SCADA (not DCS) because it is geographically extended and uses **IEC 61850** for substation automation.

## 4.5 Open Systems Integration — OPC UA Hub-and-Spoke Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** The **OPC UA Server** is the "Universal Adapter." Each device speaks its native protocol to the **wrapper** (a software module that translates to OPC UA's information model). All consumers read from a **common address space**. This is the **modern paradigm** replacing the older, fragile **gateway** approach.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:**
> - Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (answer all)
> - Part B: Module Internal Choice — answer ONE out of two 14-mark questions
> - Total for Module 4: 20 marks (1 out of 2 in Part B)

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]**

**Define SCADA. List its four major components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a computer-based industrial control system that uses networked data communication, graphical HMIs, and central supervisory control to monitor and control geographically distributed field assets.

**Four major components:**
1. **Field instrumentation** — sensors (4-20 mA, HART) and actuators.
2. **Remote Terminal Units (RTUs) / PLCs** — local data concentration and logic.
3. **Communication network** — radio, fiber, GSM, leased line.
4. **Master Station (MTU)** — HMI, historian, alarm management, control commands.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 4 for components = 3 marks.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Distinguish between a Control Loop and a Control Strategy with a suitable example from a thermal power plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **control loop** is the *closed feedback path* between a sensor, controller, and actuator that maintains a process variable at a desired setpoint. Example: **Drum level → Level Transmitter → Level Controller → Feedwater Valve**.

A **control strategy** is the *structured, algorithmic use of one or more control loops* to achieve a complex process objective. Example: **Three-Element Impulse Control** of a boiler drum, which uses three measured inputs — drum level, steam flow, and feedwater flow — in a **cascade plus feedforward** strategy to reject load disturbances.

| Aspect | Control Loop | Control Strategy |
|--------|--------------|------------------|
| Scope | Single PV-CV pair | Multi-loop orchestration |
| Example | Level-to-valve | 3-element drum level control |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — SCADA Design Focus**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Question 4 (a) Module 4]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-level architecture of a SCADA system** used in a **thermal power plant switchyard**. List any **two open communication protocols** used at the field and supervisory levels. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A SCADA system polls **80 RTUs**, each with **150 measured points**. Each point is **16-bit** with a **32-bit time-stamp** and an **8-bit status byte**. Framing overhead is **40 bytes** per RTU. Channel bit rate is **19200 bps** with **10 bits per byte (8N1 + parity)**. Compute the **total scan time**. If the SCADA deadline is **8 seconds**, suggest **two engineering solutions** to meet it. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

A **four-level SCADA architecture** for a switchyard application is as follows (refer to SECTION 4.1 diagram):

**Level 0 — Field Layer:** Instrument transformers (CTs, PTs), protection relays, breaker position indicators, isolator contacts. These provide analog measurements (bus voltage, line current, MW, MVAR) and digital status (breaker open/closed, isolator position).

**Level 1 — RTU / Bay Control Unit (BCU) Layer:** Modern switchyards use **BCUs** (also called "Bay Controllers" in IEC 61850 parlance). These are substation-hardened RTUs that interface with the protection relays via **IEC 61850 / DNP3**. They perform local interlocking (e.g., "close breaker only if both line and bus isolators are closed and synchronized") and stream GOOSE messages to peer BCUs.

**Level 2 — Communication Network Layer:** **Fiber optic ring** (typically 100 Mbps Ethernet with PRP/HSR redundancy) is standard for new switchyards. Legacy systems use **PLCC (Power Line Carrier Communication)** or **OPGW (Optical Ground Wire)**.

**Level 3 — Supervisory / Control Centre Layer:** **SCADA Master Station** with HMI mimics of the single-line diagram, alarm tiles for protection operations, and a historian for fault records. Includes **SAS (Substation Automation System)** server and **gateway** to the State Load Despatch Centre (SLDC) via **IEC 60870-5-104**.

**Two open protocols:**
1. **IEC 61850** — field/substation level (object-oriented data model, GOOSE multicast for tripping).
2. **DNP3 / IEC 60870-5-104** — supervisory to control centre.

**Block Diagram (4-5 marks):** Show the four layers stacked vertically with arrows showing data flow. Label key protocols between layers. **The KTU examiner specifically checks the layer labels.**

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Bits per point**

Each point carries: $2 \text{ bytes (value)} + 4 \text{ bytes (timestamp)} + 1 \text{ byte (status)} = 7 \text{ bytes per point}$

**Step 2 — Bits per point on the wire**

$$7 \text{ bytes} \times 10 \text{ bits/byte} = 70 \text{ bits per point}$$

**Step 3 — Bits per RTU (150 points)**

$$150 \times 70 = 10{,}500 \text{ bits per RTU (payload only)}$$

**Step 4 — Add framing overhead**

$$40 \text{ bytes} \times 10 \text{ bits/byte} = 400 \text{ bits per RTU (overhead)}$$

$$\text{Total per RTU} = 10{,}500 + 400 = 10{,}900 \text{ bits per RTU}$$

**Step 5 — Time per RTU**

$$T_{RTU} = \frac{10{,}900 \text{ bits}}{19{,}200 \text{ bps}} = 0.5677 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Total scan time for 80 RTUs**

$$T_{total} = 80 \times 0.5677 = 45.42 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 7 — Compare to deadline**

$T_{total} = 45.42 \text{ s} \gg T_{deadline} = 8 \text{ s}$ — the system **fails the deadline by 5.7×**.

**Step 8 — Engineering Solutions (any two):**

1. **Upgrade bandwidth to 115,200 bps** (typical for serial SCADA): $T_{total} = 45.42 \times 19200 / 115200 = 7.57 \text{ s}$ — meets the deadline.
2. **Enable Report-by-Exception (RBE):** In a switchyard, <5% of points change in steady state. With RBE, effective data per RTU drops to ~$10{,}500 \times 0.05 + 400 = 925$ bits. $T_{RTU,RBE} = 925 / 19200 = 0.0482$ s. $T_{total} = 80 \times 0.0482 = 3.85$ s — well within deadline.
3. **Migrate to IEC 61850 over Ethernet (100 Mbps):** $T_{total} = 80 \times (10{,}900 / 100{,}000{,}000) = 0.0087 \text{ s}$ — vastly exceeds requirements.
4. **Poll critical RTUs more frequently (multi-priority queue):** Use a 2-tier scan — critical RTUs (line bays) polled every 2 s, non-critical (lighting, AC) every 30 s.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting to include **framing overhead** in the bit count. (Lose 1 mark.)
> - **Common mistake 2:** Using 8 bits/byte instead of 10 bits/byte when parity + start/stop bits are specified. (Lose 1 mark.)
> - **Common mistake 3:** Concluding with "the system is fine" instead of **explicitly comparing** to the deadline. (Lose 1 mark — engineering judgment is required.)
> - **Common mistake 4:** Failing to provide an engineering solution. The question explicitly asks for "two engineering solutions" — not just the calculation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — DCS & Open Systems Focus**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Question 4 (b) Module 4]**

> **(a)** Explain the **three-level architecture of a Distributed Control System (DCS)** with reference to a **Blast Furnace (BF) stove control** application. List the typical **I/O count, scan time, and redundancy** philosophy of each level. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** With a block diagram, explain the **role of OPC UA in open system integration** for a modern integrated steel plant. Discuss how OPC UA differs from traditional **Modbus / DNP3** gateways. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

A **Blast Furnace stove** is a regenerative heat exchanger that preheats combustion air ("blast") to 1000-1300°C using cleaned Blast Furnace Gas (BFG) and Coke Oven Gas (COG). A typical BF has 3-4 stoves, and they are **cycled** — one stove is on "blast" (delivering hot air) while others are on "gas" (being fired). The DCS orchestrates this cycling to maintain a stable blast temperature.

**Three-level DCS architecture applied to stove control:**

**Level 0 — Field Layer:**
- **I/O count:** ~400-600 points per stove (RTDs, pressure transmitters, flow meters, valve position feedbacks).
- **Examples:** Thermocouples in the dome and cold-blast main, butterfly valve actuators on the gas/air/blast lines, COG/BFG flow meters.
- **Signal types:** 4-20 mA HART, FOUNDATION Fieldbus.

**Level 1 — Continuous Control Layer:**
- **Function:** The DCS controller (e.g., ABB AC 800M) executes **PID loops** for:
  - **Dome temperature control** (master loop).
  - **Cold-blast temperature control** (cascade with dome T).
  - **Gas flow ratio control** (BFG:COG ratio for flame stability).
  - **Combustion optimization** (cross-limited O₂ trim).
- **Scan time:** 100 ms - 1 s for the safety loops; 1-5 s for trending.
- **Redundancy:** **1:1 hot standby** for the controller; **dual-redundant network**; **pair-and-spare** I/O cards for safety-critical signals.
- **High availability target:** 99.99% (52 minutes of downtime per year).

**Level 2 — Supervisory / Operations Layer:**
- **Function:** HMI mimics show the stove's state ("ON GAS", "ON BLAST", "VALVE CHANGEOVER"), trend charts of dome temperature, alarm logs, and stove changeover scheduling. The **operator** can manually initiate a stove changeover from this level.
- **I/O handled at this level:** Soft I/O — setpoints, modes (Auto/Man/Superman), tuning parameters.
- **Redundancy:** Dual-server historian and engineering station.

**Stove-Cycling Supervisor:** A higher-level DCS function (sometimes called a "coordinator" or "scheduler") rotates the stoves based on a **time + temperature** rule (e.g., changeover when the stove has been on blast for X minutes OR dome T has dropped by Y°C). This is a **Level 2 supervisory** function but executes on Level 1 controllers.

**Comparison table (what to write in exam):**

| Level | I/O | Scan Time | Redundancy | Example for Stove |
|-------|-----|-----------|------------|-------------------|
| 0 — Field | 400-600 / stove | Real-time | None (cable redundancy) | TC, PT, valve |
| 1 — Control | Soft I/O (setpts) | 100 ms - 1 s | 1:1 hot standby, dual network | PID, changeover logic |
| 2 — Supervisory | Historized tags | 1-10 s | Dual server | HMI, alarm, trend |

#### Model Solution — (b)

**OPC UA (Open Platform Communications — Unified Architecture)** is the modern, open, platform-independent, secure, service-oriented architecture for industrial data exchange. It is the IEC 62541 standard.

**Role in an Integrated Steel Plant:**

A modern steel plant (BF-BOF route) has **30,000-50,000 I/O points** spread across:
- **Coke Oven** (Siemens PLC, Profinet).
- **Sinter Plant** (ABB 800xA, FOUNDATION Fieldbus).
- **Blast Furnace** (Yokogawa CENTUM, Profibus).
- **BOF** (Emerson DeltaV, FOUNDATION Fieldbus).
- **Caster** (Siemens, Profinet).
- **Hot Strip Mill** (GE PACSystems, Modbus TCP).

Each subsystem is "best-in-class" for its process. But the **plant-level MES** (e.g., for production scheduling, lot tracking, quality) needs a **unified view** of all 30,000+ points. **OPC UA is the integration fabric.**

**Block Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.5):**

- Each subsystem has an **embedded OPC UA Server** (or a wrapper).
- A **central OPC UA Broker** aggregates all data into a unified information model based on **ISA-95 / IEC 62264** (e.g., "Site → Area → Work Center → Unit → Equipment → Tag").
- **Consumers** (MES, ERP, IIoT cloud dashboards) read from the broker using **OPC UA Client** APIs.

**OPC UA vs. Traditional Gateways:**

| Aspect | Modbus / DNP3 Gateway | OPC UA |
|--------|----------------------|--------|
| **Data model** | Flat register / point map | Object-oriented with semantic types |
| **Naming** | Register address (40001) | Hierarchical, semantic (BF1.Stove3.DomeTemp.PV) |
| **Security** | None (Modbus), weak (DNP3) | **X.509 certificates, encryption, signing** (IEC 62541-2) |
| **Transport** | Serial RS-485 or TCP | TCP, HTTPS, MQTT (new) |
| **Information model** | None | Standard companion specs (DI, ADI, ISA-95, etc.) |
| **Discovery** | Static config | Self-describing — clients can browse the server |
| **Platform** | Windows / serial-heavy | Windows, Linux, Android, embedded RTOS, cloud |
| **Bandwidth efficiency** | Polled only | Subscriptions, monitored items, publish on change |
| **Vendor adoption** | Legacy | Modern (Siemens, ABB, Emerson, Yokogawa, GE, Rockwell — all support OPC UA) |

**Why this matters:** With a Modbus gateway, an MIS engineer writes a custom script: *"read register 40001 of device IP 10.20.30.40 and divide by 10 to get engineering units."* When the device firmware changes, the script breaks. With **OPC UA**, the client simply **browses** the address space and discovers that node "BF1.Stove3.DomeTemp" is a Float with engineering unit "°C" — no custom code. **Semantic interoperability** is the killer feature.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Drawing the OPC UA diagram as "client talks to server" without showing the **multi-vendor, multi-protocol** aggregation — the whole point of OPC UA is **vendor-agnostic integration**. (Lose 2 marks.)
> - **Common mistake 2:** Confusing **OPC Classic** (Windows-only, COM/DCOM-based, obsolete) with **OPC UA** (cross-platform, secure, scalable). (Lose 1 mark — examiners test this.)
> - **Common mistake 3:** Omitting **security** in the OPC UA discussion. Always mention **X.509 certificates, transport encryption, and authentication**.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Failing to map the steel plant subsystems to a real architecture. Generic answers get 50% credit; specific subsystem mapping (e.g., "BF uses Yokogawa CENTUM, communicates via Profibus to OPC UA server") get full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist. Read it 5 times before the exam.**

### Definitions to Memorize
- ☐ **SCADA:** Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (event-driven, geographically distributed).
- ☐ **DCS:** Distributed Control System (continuous, plant-floor, tightly coupled).
- ☐ **HMI:** Human-Machine Interface (graphical mimic of the process).
- ☐ **RTU:** Remote Terminal Unit (ruggedized substation-grade PLC for telemetry).
- ☐ **MTU:** Master Terminal Unit (central SCADA server).
- ☐ **PLC:** Programmable Logic Controller (logic-heavy discrete control).
- ☐ **HART:** Highway Addressable Remote Transducer (4-20 mA + digital).
- ☐ **OPC UA:** Open Platform Communications — Unified Architecture.
- ☐ **FF:** FOUNDATION Fieldbus (digital field-level bus).
- ☐ **ISA-95 / IEC 62264:** Enterprise-Control System Integration standard.
- ☐ **Purdue Model:** 5-level reference model for industrial cyber architecture.

### Architecture Levels (KTU Favourite)
- ☐ SCADA has **4 levels**: Field, RTU, Communication, Master.
- ☐ DCS has **3 levels**: Field, Control (Loop), Supervisory (Operations).
- ☐ Purdue Model has **5 levels + Level 3.5 (DMZ)**.
- ☐ ISA-95 maps **ERP (L4) ↔ MES (L3) ↔ DCS/SCADA (L2) ↔ Control (L1) ↔ Field (L0)**.

### Numerical Formulas to Memorize
- ☐ PID: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$.
- ☐ Scan time: $T_{scan} \le 0.1 \cdot T_{process}$.
- ☐ Cascade inner/outer: $T_{inner} \le T_{outer}/3$.
- ☐ RBE bandwidth savings: typically 80-97%.
- ☐ 4-20 mA conversion: $I = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{PV - LRV}{URV - LRV}$.

### Thermal Power Plant Mapping
- ☐ **BTG (Boiler-Turbine-Generator) Coordinator** is the master DCS controller.
- ☐ **Switchyard** uses **IEC 61850 / SCADA**, not DCS.
- ☐ **Coal Handling Plant (CHP)** uses **SCADA + PLC** (conveyor logic).
- ☐ **Boiler** uses **DCS with cascade, cross-limiting, O₂ trim, FD/ID fan control**.
- ☐ **Turbine Governor** is a **fast electro-hydraulic loop** (~10 ms).
- ☐ **Excitation** is a **separate AVR loop** controlling generator terminal voltage.

### Steel Plant Mapping
- ☐ **Coke Oven** uses DCS for oven pushing, gas cleaning, and by-product recovery.
- ☐ **Sinter Plant** uses DCS for bed temperature, ignition hood, and waste-gas fan.
- ☐ **Blast Furnace** uses DCS for **stove control, tuyeres cooling, top pressure, burden distribution**.
- ☐ **BOF** uses DCS for **oxygen blowing, lance positioning, slag control**.
- ☐ **Caster** uses DCS with **level control, breakout prediction, mold oscillation**.
- ☐ **Hot Strip Mill** uses DCS with **AGC (Automatic Gauge Control), looper control, coiler control**.
- ☐ **Stockyard** uses **SCADA + RFID** for material tracking.

### Open Systems & Cybersecurity
- ☐ **OPC UA** is the modern standard (cross-platform, secure, semantic).
- ☐ **OPC Classic** is obsolete (Windows-only, COM/DCOM).
- ☐ **IEC 61850** is for **substations** (GOOSE, MMS, Sampled Values).
- ☐ **Modbus TCP** is for **simple point-to-point** legacy integration.
- ☐ **DNP3** is for **SCADA telemetry** (water, power, gas).
- ☐ **Level 3.5 DMZ** is the **OT/IT cybersecurity boundary**.

### Common Pitfalls (Last-Minute Caution)
- ☐ Don't confuse **Modbus** with **DNP3** (DNP3 supports RBE and time-stamping).
- ☐ Don't confuse **SCADA scan time (2-10 s)** with **DCS loop time (50-500 ms)**.
- ☐ Don't claim "SCADA controls loops" — it **supervises**, while **DCS controls loops**.
- ☐ Always specify **redundancy** (1:1, 1:2, N:1) when drawing DCS diagrams.
- ☐ In PID, the **I-term** eliminates offset; the **D-term** damps overshoot; the **P-term** provides stiffness.
- ☐ For cascade control, the **inner loop must be 3-5× faster** than the outer.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — PBAET604 LOGIC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEM**
> **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE!**
> *"The best engineers understand the physics, the controls, AND the systems architecture."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Course Project Outcomes and Evaluation Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAET604 - LOGIC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Course Project Outcomes and Evaluation Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Course Project Outcomes and Evaluation Parameters — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, a **Course Project** is a semester-long, open-ended, design-and-development activity that demonstrates the integrated application of concepts acquired across the program. Unlike routine lab experiments, a course project is evaluated against explicitly stated **Course Project Outcomes (CPOs)** — a specialized subset of Course Outcomes (COs) that map the project's deliverables to the broader Program Outcomes (POs) and Program Specific Outcomes (PSOs).

A **Course Project Outcome (CPO)** is a precisely worded, measurable statement describing what a student is expected to *demonstrate, design, implement, validate, and communicate* upon completing the project work. Each project is typically mapped to **3 to 5 CPOs** (commonly denoted as **CO1 to CO5** for the project), and each CPO is associated with one or more **Performance Indicators (PIs)**.

**Evaluation Parameters** are the rubrics, metrics, weightages, and assessment instruments used by an examiner to score a project's deliverables (such as a working model, a report, a viva-voce, or a demonstration). The sum of all parameter scores — weighted according to the **CO attainment model** — produces the final project grade.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate (B.Tech Project Evaluation):** Every project *must* declare its Course Outcomes (CPOs) before the first review. The final evaluation rubric *must* align each criterion with a specific Bloom's Cognitive Level (Revised Taxonomy) and a mapped PO/PSO. Without this alignment, the project evaluation is considered invalid by the University.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a culinary student preparing for their final *Chef's Test* at a hospitality institute. The institute does not just say "cook something good" — instead, it gives the student five concrete goals:

1. **CPO1 (Recipe Design):** Design an original multi-course menu respecting regional constraints.
2. **CPO2 (Technical Skill):** Demonstrate knife skills and cooking techniques at industry standard.
3. **CPO3 (Plating & Presentation):** Plate and garnish dishes with aesthetic precision.
4. **CPO4 (Cost & Time Management):** Complete cooking within a fixed budget of **₹2,000** and a time window of **90 minutes**.
5. **CPO5 (Communication):** Explain the dish's philosophy to a panel of judges (the **viva-voce**).

The evaluators then hold three checks: a *mid-term tasting*, a *final service*, and a *post-service interview*. Each check has a **rubric** — a scoring sheet with weighted parameters. A project in engineering works identically. The *CPOs* are the goals, the *Evaluation Parameters* are the rubric items, the *reviews* are the milestones, and the *final viva* is the oral defense.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters for LDCS Students

In the **Logic and Distributed Control System (LDCS)** domain, projects typically involve designing **PLC-based automation lines, SCADA dashboards, distributed control loops, or networked sensor systems**. The CPOs force students to justify their design choices using control-theoretic reasoning — not just hardware wiring. A well-defined evaluation rubric is therefore not a bureaucratic exercise; it is a quality-assurance tool that protects students from arbitrary grading and ensures their learning is mapped to industry-relevant engineering competencies such as **system integration, real-time response, fault tolerance, and safety compliance**.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. In OBE, the first step is always: *"What should the student be able to do?"* — and only then is the curriculum, pedagogy, and assessment designed backward from that answer. Course Project Outcomes are the concrete instantiation of this principle for project work.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Course Project Outcomes and Evaluation Framework

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Well-Formed Course Project Outcome (CPO)

A CPO statement, as mandated by the **NBA / OBE framework adopted by KTU 2024**, must satisfy four structural properties:

1. **Starts with an Action Verb** drawn from **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate, Create).
2. **Specifies the Observable Artifact** (a circuit, a report, a flowchart, a working model, a simulation, a code listing).
3. **Defines the Performance Condition** (using available resources, within a given time, on a specific platform).
4. **States the Acceptable Standard** (accuracy, latency, throughput, cost, safety, reliability).

### 2.1.1 Illustrative CPOs for a Sample LDCS Project

Suppose a project is titled: *"Design of a PLC-based Conveyor Sorting System using a Distributed I/O Network."* The CPOs might be:

| CPO ID | Action Verb (Bloom Level) | Statement | Mapped POs |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CPO1** | **Design** (Level 6 — Create) | Design a ladder-logic program to sort objects based on weight, height, and color using a PLC and three sensors. | PO1, PO3, PO5 |
| **CPO2** | **Develop** (Level 3 — Apply) | Develop a SCADA HMI screen on a PC to monitor and control the conveyor with a polling cycle under **200 ms**. | PO1, PO5, PSO2 |
| **CPO3** | **Analyze** (Level 4 — Analyze) | Analyze the effect of distributed I/O network delay on the closed-loop settling time of the position controller. | PO2, PO4 |
| **CPO4** | **Evaluate** (Level 5 — Evaluate) | Evaluate the fault-tolerance of the system by simulating an open-circuit sensor failure and reporting the system's response. | PO5, PO6, PSO1 |
| **CPO5** | **Communicate** (Level 3 — Apply) | Prepare a project report and deliver a 15-minute presentation demonstrating the working model. | PO9, PO10, PO12 |

## 2.2 Evaluation Parameters — The Standard KTU 2024 Rubric

The KTU 2024 Scheme specifies that the project evaluation must be conducted in **three phases**, each with its own weightage. The total marks are typically **100** for the internal guide's continuous assessment and **50** for the university-conducted final evaluation, depending on the regulation variant. Below is the canonical structure.

### 2.2.1 Phase-Wise Evaluation Matrix

| Evaluation Phase | Conducted By | Typical Marks | Focus Area |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Phase 1: Problem Definition & Design Review** | Internal Guide + Project Coordinator | 20 marks | Problem clarity, literature survey, block diagram, methodology |
| **Phase 2: Mid-Term Implementation Review** | Internal Guide + Project Coordinator | 30 marks | Progress, working prototype, partial integration |
| **Phase 3: Final Evaluation (University)** | External Examiner + Internal Guide | 50 marks | Working model, viva-voce, report quality, demonstration |

### 2.2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Course Project Evaluation Metrics

| Symbol | Definition | Formula / Range | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T_{\text{proj}}$ | Total project score | $T_{\text{proj}} = W_1 S_1 + W_2 S_2 + W_3 S_3$ | Marks (out of 100) |
| $W_i$ | Weightage of phase $i$ | $W_1 + W_2 + W_3 = 1$ | Dimensionless |
| $S_i$ | Marks obtained in phase $i$ | $0 \le S_i \le \text{Max}_i$ | Marks |
| $\text{CO Attainment}$ | Attainment level of a CPO | $\text{CO Attain.} = \frac{\text{Avg. \% marks in CO}}{100}$ | Level 1, 2, or 3 |
| $\text{PO Attainment}$ | Aggregate PO attainment | $\sum_{i} (\text{CO Attain.}_i) \times (\text{Correlation Level}_i)$ | Continuous value |
| $\text{GPA}_{\text{proj}}$ | Project grade point | $\text{GPA} = \left( \frac{T_{\text{proj}}}{10} \right)$ on a 10-pt scale | Grade Point |
| $C_p$ | Correlation strength (PO–CPO) | $C_p \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ where 1 = Low, 2 = Medium, 3 = High | Integer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Correlation Level Convention:** A *1* (low) is assigned when a CPO indirectly touches a PO, a *2* (medium) when it supports the PO, and a *3* (high) when the CPO is the *primary demonstration* of that PO. These levels are not chosen by students — they are pre-declared by the faculty and audited during the NBA accreditation cycle.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of This Framework

In professional engineering practice, the same OBE language is used in:
- **IEEE accreditation** of engineering programs worldwide.
- **Industry project reviews** (e.g., a Bosch automotive ECU team's design review uses a weighted rubric mapping deliverables to ISO 26262 functional safety requirements).
- **R&D project gate reviews** (Stage-Gate process by Robert Cooper) where each gate (Concept, Development, Testing, Launch) has evaluation parameters identical in spirit to KTU's three review phases.
- **Safety-critical control systems** (e.g., nuclear plant DCS projects) where a *CPO mapped to functional safety* would force a student to evaluate fault tolerance — a parameter invisible to a "does it work?" examination.

The KTU framework thus mirrors industrial best practice, preparing students for **global engineering competency standards** such as **ABET (USA), EUR-ACE (Europe), and Washington Accord** requirements.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — Designing and Computing Course Project Outcomes

## 3.1 The Eight-Step Method to Design CPOs and an Evaluation Rubric

This is the canonical procedure followed by KTU 2024 Scheme faculty committees when they sit down before a semester to draft the project evaluation framework for a course.

### Step 1 — Identify the Domain Scope
Read the official course syllabus and list the **technically relevant sub-domains**. For **PBAET604 — Logic and Distributed Control System**, the canonical sub-domains are: PLC programming, SCADA systems, DCS architecture, industrial networking, real-time control loops, and fault diagnostics.

> *Textual conversion:* "Map the syllabus to a list of technical areas the project should demonstrably touch."

### Step 2 — Pick 3 to 5 Anchor Performance Verbs
Select 3 to 5 action verbs from the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** that span cognitive levels 3 through 6 (Apply, Analyze, Evaluate, Create). Avoid lower-level verbs (Remember, Understand) for a project — they are reserved for quiz-style internal assessments.

> *Textual conversion:* "Choose verbs that show the student has done engineering, not just remembered it."

### Step 3 — Draft the CPO Statements
Using the **ABCD template** (Audience, Behavior, Condition, Degree), write each CPO as a one-sentence measurable statement.

> *Textual conversion:* "Write what the student must be able to *do*, *under what conditions*, and to *what standard*."

### Step 4 — Assign PO/PSO Correlations
For each CPO, mark which **Program Outcomes** and **Program Specific Outcomes** it demonstrates. Use the $C_p$ correlation level from **Section 2.2.2**.

> *Textual conversion:* "Link each CPO to the broader graduate attributes the program promises to deliver."

### Step 5 — Define the Performance Indicators (PIs)
Each CPO must have 1 to 3 **PIs** — concrete, scoreable items. For example, for *CPO3 (Analyze distributed I/O delay)*, the PIs could be: PI-3.1 = a recorded response-time graph, PI-3.2 = a calculated settling-time value, PI-3.3 = a discussion of the impact.

> *Textual conversion:* "Break each CPO into checklist items the evaluator can tick or score."

### Step 6 — Construct the Rubric
Build a **Likert-style 5-point rubric** for each PI, where 1 = Unsatisfactory, 2 = Developing, 3 = Satisfactory, 4 = Proficient, 5 = Exemplary. This is the KTU 2024-preferred scale.

> *Textual conversion:* "Design a 1-to-5 grading table describing what each level looks like."

### Step 7 — Assign Phase Weights
Allocate weights $W_1$, $W_2$, $W_3$ to the three review phases, summing to 1. A common split is $W_1 = 0.20$, $W_2 = 0.30$, $W_3 = 0.50$ (the final evaluation carries the most weight).

> *Textual conversion:* "Decide what fraction of the total grade each review phase contributes."

### Step 8 — Compute Attainment at the End
After the final review, calculate the **CO Attainment Level** using the KTU 2024 thresholds shown in **Step 3.2** below.

> *Textual conversion:* "Translate raw percentage scores into the 1, 2, 3 attainment scale demanded by NBA-OBE audits."

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of the CO Attainment Level

The CO Attainment Level is computed by first taking the **average percentage of marks** scored by the entire class on the assessment items that target a particular CO, then comparing that percentage to **two threshold values** set by the institution.

### 3.2.1 The Two-Threshold Model

Let:

$$
P_{C_i} = \text{Average percentage of marks scored by the class on assessment items mapped to } CO_i
$$

The two thresholds are conventionally:

$$
T_1 = 60\% \quad \text{(Threshold for Level 2 — Acceptable attainment)}
$$

$$
T_2 = 70\% \quad \text{(Threshold for Level 3 — Substantial / Strong attainment)}
$$

The attainment level $L_i$ for the $i^{\text{th}}$ CO is then:

$$
L_i = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } P_{C_i} < T_1 \quad \text{(Level 1 — Needs Improvement)} \\
2 & \text{if } T_1 \le P_{C_i} < T_2 \quad \text{(Level 2 — Acceptable)} \\
3 & \text{if } P_{C_i} \ge T_2 \quad \text{(Level 3 — Substantial)}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a batch of **40 students** completed an LDCS project. Their average marks in different review phases, mapped to the various CPOs, are tabulated as:

| CPO | Mid-Term % (out of 30) | Final Eval % (out of 50) | Total % (out of 80) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| CPO1 | 25 / 30 = 83.3% | 42 / 50 = 84.0% | 84.0% |
| CPO2 | 22 / 30 = 73.3% | 38 / 50 = 76.0% | 75.0% |
| CPO3 | 18 / 30 = 60.0% | 30 / 50 = 60.0% | 60.0% |
| CPO4 | 15 / 30 = 50.0% | 32 / 50 = 64.0% | 58.8% |
| CPO5 | 24 / 30 = 80.0% | 40 / 50 = 80.0% | 80.0% |

Now apply the threshold rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &: P_{C_1} = 84.0\% \ge T_2 = 70\% \;\Rightarrow\; L_1 = 3 \\
L_2 &: P_{C_2} = 75.0\% \ge T_2 = 70\% \;\Rightarrow\; L_2 = 3 \\
L_3 &: P_{C_3} = 60.0\% \ge T_1 = 60\%,\; < T_2 = 70\% \;\Rightarrow\; L_3 = 2 \\
L_4 &: P_{C_4} = 58.8\% < T_1 = 60\% \;\Rightarrow\; L_4 = 1 \\
L_5 &: P_{C_5} = 80.0\% \ge T_2 = 70\% \;\Rightarrow\; L_5 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual conversion:* Each row takes the average percentage, then drops it into the threshold table to receive a level of 1, 2, or 3. CPO4 is below 60%, so it flags as *Level 1 — Needs Improvement*, signalling the faculty to redesign the assessment in the next cycle.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of PO Attainment

PO Attainment aggregates the CO attainments weighted by the **correlation level** $C_p$ declared in Step 4.

Let $n$ be the number of COs mapped to a particular PO. The PO attainment $A_{PO}$ is given by:

$$
A_{PO} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{p,i} \times L_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{p,i}}
$$

The resulting $A_{PO}$ is a continuous value in the range $[1, 3]$. It is then categorized back into levels using the same $T_1$ and $T_2$ thresholds applied to a **percentage-of-maximum** scale:

$$
\% \text{Reached} = \frac{A_{PO} - 1}{3 - 1} \times 100\%
$$

> *Textual conversion:* Take each level (1 to 3), map it to a 0–100% scale, then use the threshold rule. This produces the final "PO Attainment Level" reported in the **SAR (Self-Assessment Report)** of the department.

## 3.4 Worked Example — PO1 Attainment

Suppose PO1 (Engineering Knowledge) is mapped to CPO1 ($C_p = 3$), CPO2 ($C_p = 2$), and CPO3 ($C_p = 1$) with attainments $L_1 = 3$, $L_2 = 3$, $L_3 = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{PO1} &= \frac{(3 \times 3) + (2 \times 3) + (1 \times 2)}{3 + 2 + 1} \\
&= \frac{9 + 6 + 2}{6} \\
&= \frac{17}{6} \\
&\approx 2.833
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual conversion:* Multiply each level by its correlation, add them up, divide by the sum of correlations. The result is a single weighted average between 1 and 3.

The corresponding percentage on a 0–100% scale:

$$
\% \text{Reached} = \frac{2.833 - 1}{3 - 1} \times 100\% = \frac{1.833}{2} \times 100\% = 91.67\%
$$

> *Textual conversion:* Convert the level-based number into a percentage-of-maximum value, so it can be compared against the standard 60% and 70% thresholds.

Since $91.67\% \ge 70\%$, the PO1 Attainment Level is **Level 3 (Substantial)**.

# 4. Structural Schematics — Project Evaluation Flow and CO-PO Mapping

## 4.1 End-to-End Project Evaluation Lifecycle

This diagram visualizes the **three-phase evaluation pipeline** of a KTU 2024 Scheme course project, from syllabus-driven CPO design all the way to the final attainment report submitted for NBA audit.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Syllabus and OBE Policy] --> B[Step 1: Identify Domain Sub-topics]
    B --> C[Step 2: Pick Bloom Action Verbs]
    C --> D[Step 3: Draft 3 to 5 CPO Statements]
    D --> E[Step 4: Map CPOs to POs and PSOs with Cp]
    E --> F[Step 5: Define Performance Indicators PIs]
    F --> G[Step 6: Build 5 Point Rubric]
    G --> H[Step 7: Assign Phase Weights W1 W2 W3]
    H --> I[Phase 1 Review Problem Definition 20 percent]
    I --> J[Phase 2 Review Mid Term Prototype 30 percent]
    J --> K[Phase 3 Final Evaluation 50 percent]
    K --> L[Compute Average Percentage P_Ci per CPO]
    L --> M{Compare P_Ci to T1 60 percent and T2 70 percent}
    M -->|Below T1| N[Level 1 Needs Improvement]
    M -->|Between T1 and T2| O[Level 2 Acceptable]
    M -->|Above T2| P[Level 3 Substantial]
    N --> Q[Compute PO Attainment A_PO]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[NBA Self Assessment Report SAR Submission]
```

A common tool in OBE documentation is a **correlation matrix** linking every CO to every PO. While Mermaid cannot render a true spreadsheet, the following **functional block diagram** shows how the correlations feed the attainment engine.

    end

    subgraph Attain_Block[Attainment Engine]
        CP[Correlation Level Cp 1 to 3]

    end

    CPO1 -->|Cp 3| PO1
    CPO1 -->|Cp 3| PO3
    CPO1 -->|Cp 2| PO5
    CPO2 -->|Cp 2| PO1

## 4.3 Rubric-Construction Block Diagram

This schematic illustrates the **anatomy of a single rubric criterion** in a project evaluation sheet, showing how raw observations are converted into the final score.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Obs[Raw Observation during Demo] --> Crit[Specific Rubric Criterion e.g. Working Model]
    Crit --> PI[Performance Indicator e.g. Demonstrates all modes]
    PI --> Scale{1 to 5 Likert Mapping}
    Scale -->|Score 1| L1[Unsatisfactory does not work]
    Scale -->|Score 2| L2[Developing partial functionality]
    Scale -->|Score 3| L3[Satisfactory works as specified]
    Scale -->|Score 4| L4[Proficient works with documentation]
    Scale -->|Score 5| L5[Exemplary works with edge case handling]
    L1 --> Weight[Multiply by Phase Weight W]
    L2 --> Weight
    L3 --> Weight
    L4 --> Weight
    L5 --> Weight
    Weight --> Final[Final Phase Marks]
```
