# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Rapid Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q1.** Define **Course Project Outcomes (CPOs)**. List any **two characteristics** that a well-formed CPO must possess under the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** A Course Project Outcome is a measurable statement, written using an action verb from the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**, that describes what a student is expected to demonstrate, design, implement, evaluate, and communicate after completing the project work assigned in a course.

**Characteristics (2 Marks — 1 each):**
1. **Measurability & Specificity:** A CPO must be observable and scoreable, not vague. Example — "Design a PLC ladder program to sort objects by weight with a cycle time under **2 s**" is measurable; "Understand PLCs" is not.
2. **PO/PSO Mapping:** Every CPO must be correlated to at least one Program Outcome or Program Specific Outcome using a correlation level (1 = Low, 2 = Medium, 3 = High) so that attainment can be quantitatively computed.

*(Alternative acceptable characteristics: 1) Uses a single high-level Bloom verb, 2) Specifies an artifact + condition + standard, 3) Aligned with industry/societal relevance.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **Q2.** State the **two-threshold rule** used by KTU 2024 to compute the **Course Outcome Attainment Level**. What is the attainment level if the average percentage of marks in a particular CO is **65%**?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Two-Threshold Rule (2 Marks):** The institution fixes two thresholds, conventionally:

$$
T_1 = 60\% \quad \text{and} \quad T_2 = 70\%
$$

The attainment level $L$ is assigned as:

$$
L = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } P_C < 60\% \\ 2 & \text{if } 60\% \le P_C < 70\% \\ 3 & \text{if } P_C \ge 70\% \end{cases}
$$

**Application (1 Mark):** For $P_C = 65\%$, since $60\% \le 65\% < 70\%$, the CO Attainment Level is **Level 2 (Acceptable)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Common Stem:** *"A final-year LDCS batch is executing a project titled 'Design of a Distributed PLC Network for an Automated Bottle-Filling Plant using SCADA.' The internal evaluation framework uses 3 review phases with weights $W_1 = 0.20$, $W_2 = 0.30$, $W_3 = 0.50$, and the class of 40 students records the average percentage marks shown in the table."*

| CPO | Phase 1 % | Phase 2 % | Phase 3 % | Total Weighted % |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| CPO1 | 80 | 78 | 85 | ? |
| CPO2 | 70 | 72 | 75 | ? |
| CPO3 | 55 | 60 | 58 | ? |
| CPO4 | 62 | 65 | 70 | ? |
| CPO5 | 75 | 80 | 82 | ? |

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **A (a)** Compute the **weighted total percentage** of marks for each CPO using the formula $P_{C_i} = W_1 P_1 + W_2 P_2 + W_3 P_3$. Hence, identify the **Attainment Level** ($L_1$ to $L_5$) of each CPO using the two-threshold rule. **(7 Marks)**

> **A (b)** Suppose the Program Outcomes **PO1, PO2, and PO3** are correlated to the five CPOs as in the table below. Using the attainment levels from (a), compute the **PO Attainments** $A_{PO1}$, $A_{PO2}$, and $A_{PO3}$, and identify the attainment level of each PO. **(7 Marks)**

| | CPO1 | CPO2 | CPO3 | CPO4 | CPO5 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| PO1 $C_p$ | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| PO2 $C_p$ | 0 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 0 |
| PO3 $C_p$ | 3 | 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 |

---

### **Solution to A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Weighted percentage for CPO1 (1 Mark)**

$$
P_{C_1} = (0.20 \times 80) + (0.30 \times 78) + (0.50 \times 85)
$$

$$
P_{C_1} = 16 + 23.4 + 42.5 = 81.9\%
$$

> *Textual conversion:* Multiply each phase's percentage by its weight, then add them up.

**Step 2: Weighted percentage for CPO2 (1 Mark)**

$$
P_{C_2} = (0.20 \times 70) + (0.30 \times 72) + (0.50 \times 75)
$$

$$
P_{C_2} = 14 + 21.6 + 37.5 = 73.1\%
$$

**Step 3: Weighted percentage for CPO3 (1 Mark)**

$$
P_{C_3} = (0.20 \times 55) + (0.30 \times 60) + (0.50 \times 58)
$$

$$
P_{C_3} = 11 + 18 + 29 = 58.0\%
$$

**Step 4: Weighted percentage for CPO4 (1 Mark)**

$$
P_{C_4} = (0.20 \times 62) + (0.30 \times 65) + (0.50 \times 70)
$$

$$
P_{C_4} = 12.4 + 19.5 + 35 = 66.9\%
$$

**Step 5: Weighted percentage for CPO5 (1 Mark)**

$$
P_{C_5} = (0.20 \times 75) + (0.30 \times 80) + (0.50 \times 82)
$$

$$
P_{C_5} = 15 + 24 + 41 = 80.0\%
$$

**Step 6: Attainment levels using $T_1 = 60\%$ and $T_2 = 70\%$ (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &: 81.9\% \ge 70\% \;\Rightarrow\; L_1 = 3 \\
L_2 &: 73.1\% \ge 70\% \;\Rightarrow\; L_2 = 3 \\
L_3 &: 58.0\% < 60\% \;\Rightarrow\; L_3 = 1 \\
L_4 &: 60\% \le 66.9\% < 70\% \;\Rightarrow\; L_4 = 2 \\
L_5 &: 80.0\% \ge 70\% \;\Rightarrow\; L_5 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual conversion:* Apply the threshold rule to each weighted percentage. CPO3 falls below 60%, so it is at Level 1.

---

### **Solution to A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: PO1 Attainment (2 Marks)**

Only CPO1 ($C_p = 3$), CPO2 ($C_p = 2$), and CPO3 ($C_p = 1$) are mapped to PO1.

$$
A_{PO1} = \frac{(3 \times 3) + (2 \times 3) + (1 \times 1)}{3 + 2 + 1}
$$

$$
A_{PO1} = \frac{9 + 6 + 1}{6} = \frac{16}{6} \approx 2.667
$$

> *Textual conversion:* Weight each CO level by its correlation, then average.

Convert to percentage:

$$
\% \text{Reached}_{PO1} = \frac{2.667 - 1}{3 - 1} \times 100\% = \frac{1.667}{2} \times 100\% \approx 83.3\%
$$

Since $83.3\% \ge 70\%$, **PO1 Attainment Level = 3 (Substantial)**.

**Step 2: PO2 Attainment (2 Marks)**

Mapped COs: CPO2 ($C_p = 1$), CPO3 ($C_p = 3$), CPO4 ($C_p = 2$).

$$
A_{PO2} = \frac{(1 \times 3) + (3 \times 1) + (2 \times 2)}{1 + 3 + 2}
$$

$$
A_{PO2} = \frac{3 + 3 + 4}{6} = \frac{10}{6} \approx 1.667
$$

Convert to percentage:

$$
\% \text{Reached}_{PO2} = \frac{1.667 - 1}{2} \times 100\% = \frac{0.667}{2} \times 100\% \approx 33.3\%
$$

Since $33.3\% < 60\%$, **PO2 Attainment Level = 1 (Needs Improvement)**.

**Step 3: PO3 Attainment (2 Marks)**

Mapped COs: CPO1 ($C_p = 3$), CPO2 ($C_p = 2$), CPO5 ($C_p = 1$).

$$
A_{PO3} = \frac{(3 \times 3) + (2 \times 3) + (1 \times 3)}{3 + 2 + 1}
$$

$$
A_{PO3} = \frac{9 + 6 + 3}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3.0
$$

Convert to percentage:

$$
\% \text{Reached}_{PO3} = \frac{3 - 1}{2} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

Since $100\% \ge 70\%$, **PO3 Attainment Level = 3 (Substantial)**.

**Step 4: Summary Table (1 Mark)**

| PO | $A_{PO}$ | % Reached | Level |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| PO1 | 2.667 | 83.3% | 3 |
| PO2 | 1.667 | 33.3% | 1 |
| PO3 | 3.000 | 100% | 3 |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **B (a)** Define a **Course Project Outcome** in the context of the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework. Write **three CPOs** (using Revised Bloom action verbs) for an LDCS project titled *"SCADA-based Real-Time Monitoring of a Three-Phase Induction Motor with Overcurrent Protection."* Map each CPO to a PO/PSO with an appropriate correlation level. **(7 Marks)**

> **B (b)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the **three-phase evaluation lifecycle** of a course project under the KTU 2024 Scheme. For each phase, state the **typical weightage** and the **focus area** of the evaluator. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution to B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 Marks):** A *Course Project Outcome* is a measurable statement that specifies the engineering competency a student must demonstrate at the end of the project, expressed using a Revised Bloom's Taxonomy action verb, a concrete artifact, a performance condition, and an acceptable standard, and is correlated to one or more Program Outcomes.

**Three CPOs (3 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **CPO1 — Apply:** *Implement* a ladder-logic program in a PLC to start, stop, and reverse a three-phase induction motor with overload protection, using a network cycle time not exceeding **100 ms**. (Mapped to **PO1 — Engineering Knowledge, $C_p = 3$** and **PO5 — Modern Tool Usage, $C_p = 2$**)

2. **CPO2 — Create:** *Design* a SCADA HMI screen that displays real-time motor speed, current, voltage, and trip status, with a refresh rate under **500 ms**, on a PC connected via Modbus TCP. (Mapped to **PSO1 — DCS Design, $C_p = 3$** and **PO5, $C_p = 2$**)

3. **CPO3 — Evaluate:** *Evaluate* the system's response to a forced overcurrent condition by injecting a fault and reporting the trip time, with a target trip time under **50 ms**. (Mapped to **PO4 — Conduct Investigations, $C_p = 3$** and **PO6 — The Engineer and Society, $C_p = 2$**)

> *Textual conversion:* Each statement uses one action verb (Apply/Create/Evaluate), names an artifact, names a performance condition, and names an acceptable numerical standard. Each is mapped to a PO/PSO with a correlation level.

---

### **Solution to B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Three-Phase Evaluation Lifecycle (7 Marks — 2 per phase + 1 for the diagram):**

The course project evaluation is structured into three sequential review phases. The block diagram below shows the flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1: Problem Definition and Design Review] -->|Weight 20 percent| P2[Phase 2: Mid Term Implementation Review]
    P2 -->|Weight 30 percent| P3[Phase 3: Final Evaluation University]
    P3 -->|Compute| Attain[CO and PO Attainment Levels]
```

**Phase 1 — Problem Definition and Design Review (2 Marks):**
- **Typical Weightage:** $W_1 = 0.20$ (20% of the total internal marks).
- **Focus Area:** Clarity of the problem statement, depth of the literature survey, completeness of the block diagram / circuit diagram, and the suitability of the proposed methodology. The student is expected to demonstrate *systematic thinking* rather than finished work.

**Phase 2 — Mid-Term Implementation Review (2 Marks):**
- **Typical Weightage:** $W_2 = 0.30$ (30% of the total internal marks).
- **Focus Area:** Progress against the timeline, a partially working prototype, demonstrated individual contribution, and an early integration of hardware and software. The student must show *engineering momentum* and *problem-solving resilience*.

**Phase 3 — Final Evaluation (2 Marks):**
- **Typical Weightage:** $W_3 = 0.50$ (50% of the total marks, including the viva-voce).
- **Focus Area:** A fully working model or simulation, a complete project report, a 10–15 minute demonstration, and a viva-voce testing the student's conceptual clarity, design justification, and awareness of safety / societal impact.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** The three-phase lifecycle is the KTU 2024 Scheme's mechanism for *continuous outcome-based assessment* — it ensures that final attainment is not a one-shot examination but a *rolling, weighted, auditable record* of the student's project journey.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Verb-mixing:** Writing a CPO with two action verbs (e.g., "Design and Implement") — examiners reject the CO because attainment becomes ambiguous. Use *one* verb per CPO.
> 2. **Threshold confusion:** Students often write $T_1 = 40\%$ and $T_2 = 60\%$. The KTU 2024 convention is **$T_1 = 60\%$ and $T_2 = 70\%$** (or institutional variants clearly stated). Always check the institution's stated values.
> 3. **Correlation-level misuse:** Marking *every* CPO as $C_p = 3$ for *every* PO inflates attainment artificially. The NBA audit *flags* this; examiners will deduct 1 to 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the final 1% — 100% conversion:** When reporting PO attainment, students forget to map $[1, 3]$ back to a 0–100% scale using the formula $\frac{A_{PO} - 1}{2} \times 100\%$. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Generic "viva-voce" rubrics:** A viva must have *mapped* PIs, not free-form impressions. Examiners will return generic rubrics as "non-compliant with OBE."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

- **CPO definition:** A measurable Bloom-verb-led statement of what the student must do, under conditions, to a standard.
- **Number of CPOs:** Typically **3 to 5** per project, with **1 to 3 PIs** per CPO.
- **PO correlation levels:** $C_p \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ — must be *declared upfront* by faculty, not retroactively by students.
- **Three evaluation phases:** $W_1 + W_2 + W_3 = 1$, with the final review typically weighted at **0.50**.
- **Two thresholds for attainment:** $T_1 = 60\%$, $T_2 = 70\%$ (institutional variants exist; always check the syllabus).
- **Attainment levels:** **1 = Needs Improvement**, **2 = Acceptable**, **3 = Substantial**.
- **Rubric scale:** KTU 2024 prefers a **5-point Likert** scale (1 = Unsatisfactory to 5 = Exemplary).
- **PO Attainment formula:** $A_{PO} = \dfrac{\sum C_{p,i} \cdot L_i}{\sum C_{p,i}}$, then rescaled to a 0–100% range as $\dfrac{A_{PO} - 1}{2} \times 100\%$.
- **Common LDCS-CPO verbs:** *Design* (ladder logic, HMI), *Implement* (SCADA, Modbus), *Analyze* (loop delay, settling time), *Evaluate* (fault response, safety).
- **Always pair** the engineering artifact (ladder logic, SCADA screen, response curve) with a **numerical standard** (cycle time under **200 ms**, trip time under **50 ms**).
- **Final reports** must include the **CO–PO Attainment Matrix** — without it, the project is non-compliant with the KTU 2024 OBE framework.
- **Audit-ready documentation:** Keep the *rubric, the marks, the raw scores, the CO attainment table, and the PO attainment table* in a single appendices file; the **NBA SAR (Self-Assessment Report)** will ask for them during accreditation visits.
- **Examiner's last word:** A great LDCS project is *not* judged by the number of components soldered — it is judged by the **clarity of its declared outcomes** and the **rigour of the evidence** presented against them.
